title: IMO GHG Strategy 2023

Framework approved 63-16-24 but key implementation details pending. ACTION NOW: Monitor MEPC 84 outcomes. Model long-term ocean freight cost scenarios with and without IMO carbon pricing. reasoning: Sets the global trajectory for carrier fleet investment and green fuel adoption — every ocean freight contract is shaped by this.
whatIsIt: The 2023 IMO Strategy on Reduction of GHG Emissions from Ships, adopted at MEPC 80 (July 2023), commits global shipping to net-zero GHG emissions by or around 2050 with interim checkpoints. The strategy is implemented through the IMO Net-Zero Framework combining a Global Fuel Standard (GFI) and a carbon pricing mechanism. Approved at MEPC 83 (April 2025) but formal adoption adjourned at MEPC ES.2 (October 2025) — now scheduled for MEPC 84 (Spring 2026) with entry into force from 2028.
whyMatters: This is the 25-year investment signal for the entire maritime industry. Every carrier fleet decision — newbuilds, fuel contracts, engine retrofits — is shaped by these targets. For freight forwarders: (1) carriers investing ahead of the curve will have lower compliance costs and more competitive green freight rates, (2) the pricing mechanism will create a new global carbon cost layer on ocean freight separate from EU ETS, (3) US opposition (walked out of MEPC ES.2 vote) creates enforcement fragmentation on US-origin trade lanes, (4) long-term contract pricing must account for escalating decarbonization costs.
keyData: Targets: 20% GHG reduction by 2030 (striving 30%), 70% by 2040 (striving 80%), net-zero by ~2050
Net-Zero Framework: Global Fuel Standard (GFI) + carbon pricing mechanism
MEPC ES.2 (October 2025): adoption adjourned by one year — US opposition
MEPC 84 (Spring 2026): implementation guidelines scheduled for approval
Entry into force: 2027-2028 for mandatory instruments
Applies to vessels >5,000 GT — 85% of international shipping CO₂ emissions
US formally opposed at MEPC ES.2 — creates compliance fragmentation on US trade lanes

whatIsIt: FuelEU Maritime Regulation (EU) 2023/1805 mandates progressive reduction of the greenhouse gas intensity of energy used onboard ships calling at EU/EEA ports. Sets a GHG intensity limit that tightens from 2% below 2020 baseline (2025) to 80% below by 2050. Enforced by EU Member State verifying bodies. Separate from but complementary to EU ETS — FuelEU targets fuel quality while ETS targets emissions quantity.
whyMatters: Creates a new cost layer separate from ETS surcharges. Non-compliant vessels face a fixed penalty of €2,400 per tonne of VLSFO-equivalent GHG intensity deficit. Carriers will structure compliance costs as 'FuelEU surcharges' on freight invoices. The penalty escalates 10% for each consecutive year of non-compliance. For freight forwarders: (1) expect new surcharge line items from carriers starting 2025, (2) carriers using LNG, methanol, or biofuels may offer lower FuelEU surcharges — creating procurement leverage, (3) pooling mechanisms between vessels create complex surcharge allocation that needs monitoring.

Penalty: €2,400/tonne VLSFO-equivalent deficit, +10% per consecutive non-compliance year
Shore power mandate: Container ships and passenger vessels must connect to on-shore power supply at major EU ports from 2030

Compliance via: vessel pooling, banking/borrowing compliance surplus, or paying penalty

title: EU ETS for Shipping

reasoning: Directly binding ETS obligation with financial penalties. Immediate cost pass-through on every EU shipment.
whatIsIt: The EU Emissions Trading System extended to maritime transport under Directive 2023/959 (amending Directive 2003/87/EC). Shipping companies operating vessels over 5,000 GT on voyages to, from, or between EU/EEA ports must monitor, report, and surrender EU Allowances (EUAs) for their verified CO₂ emissions. Enforced by EU Member State competent authorities. From 1 January 2026, scope expanded to include methane (CH₄) and nitrous oxide (N₂O) alongside CO₂.
whyMatters: Carriers pass through ETS costs as surcharges on every ocean freight invoice touching EU ports. At the current EUA price of ~€68-81/tCO₂ (Q1 2026), a typical container vessel voyage generates €50,000-200,000 in allowance costs per round trip. Freight forwarders face: (1) ETS surcharge line items from carriers that need verification, (2) customer questions about 'EU ETS included?' in tender responses, (3) risk of under-recovery or double-charging if surcharge governance is weak, (4) Scope 3 reporting requirements to allocate ETS costs to specific shipments.
keyData: Scope: CO₂ from 2024, CH₄ + N₂O added from 1 January 2026
Coverage: 100% intra-EU voyages, 50% of emissions from voyages into/out of EU
Surrender deadline: 30 September annually for previous year's emissions
~€15-25/TEU additional cost on EU port-calling container shipments
Applies to vessels >5,000 GT — covers ~85% of maritime CO₂ emissions

title: CII (Carbon Intensity Indicator)
note: IN FORCE since 1 January 2023. Third year of ratings. Rating thresholds are tightening annually, pushing more vessels into D/E categories. ACTION NOW: Request CII ratings from all contracted carriers. Exclude D/E vessels from tenders where clients require it. Factor CII-driven slow steaming into transit time planning. Owner: Ocean Product + Procurement.
reasoning: Annual vessel ratings directly affect carrier fleet viability and charter market pricing. Phase 1 review complete — thresholds confirmed tightening.
whatIsIt: The Carbon Intensity Indicator (CII) under IMO MARPOL Annex VI amendments (Resolution MEPC.352(78)) rates individual ships on an A-to-E scale based on their annual operational carbon intensity (grams CO₂ per tonne-nautical mile). Ships rated D for three consecutive years or E in any year must submit a corrective action plan. Enforced by flag state administrations. Operational since 1 January 2023.
whyMatters: CII ratings directly affect vessel availability and routing. Ships rated D or E face operational restrictions — slow steaming, voyage re-routing, or port avoidance. For freight forwarders: (1) D/E rated vessels may not call at certain ports or may be excluded from tenders by clients with sustainability commitments, (2) carriers operating constrained vessels may reduce service frequency or capacity on affected routes, (3) CII-driven slow steaming increases transit times by 5-15%, affecting supply chain planning, (4) clients increasingly request CII data for their Scope 3 reporting and tender evaluations.
keyData: Rating scale: A (superior) to E (inferior) based on Annual Efficiency Ratio (AER)
Corrective action required: D rating for 3 consecutive years OR E in any single year
Rating thresholds tighten annually — vessels that were C in 2023 may become D by 2026
~30% of the global fleet estimated to be rated D or E by 2026
No direct financial penalty — but operational restrictions and reputational impact
Applies to all ships >5,000 GT in international trade
Legal instrument: MEPC.352(78), MEPC.353(78), MARPOL Annex VI

whyMatters: MARPOL Annex VI is the base layer of all maritime environmental regulation. Every compliance cost from CII, ETS, and FuelEU ultimately traces back to this framework. fullBrief:
I'll generate a comprehensive intelligence brief for IMO MARPOL Annex VI, using web search to verify current implementation status, dates, and penalty amounts.

# IMO MARPOL Annex VI: International Convention for the Prevention of Pollution from Ships

**Regulatory Fact Document** | April 2026
**Primary source:** [MARPOL Annex VI](https://www.imo.org/en/OurWork/Environment/Pages/Air-Pollution.aspx)

---

## What This Regulation Is and Why It Applies



MARPOL Annex VI is the main international agreement covering air pollution prevention requirements from ships

, adopted through the International Maritime Organization (IMO) in 1997 with major revisions in 2005, 2011, and 2021. 

The current framework limits sulphur in fuel oil used on board ships operating outside designated emission control areas to 0.50% m/m (mass by mass) - known as "IMO 2020" - while within specific designated emission control areas the limits are stricter at 0.10%

.



The MARPOL regulations apply to all ships, with all sizes of ships required to use fuel oil that meets the 0.50% limit since 1 January 2020

. Beyond sulphur limits, the regulation encompasses NOx emission standards (Tier I-III requirements), the Energy Efficiency Design Index (EEDI) for new vessels, and 

the Energy Efficiency Existing Ship Index (EEXI) and Carbon Intensity Indicator (CII) requirements that entered into force on 1 November 2022, with certification requirements effective from 1 January 2023

.

For freight forwarders, MARPOL Annex VI represents the foundational regulatory architecture upon which all maritime environmental compliance costs flow. 

The global sulphur cap reduction from 3.5% to 0.50% represents the largest operational cost driver in ocean freight

, manifesting as Very Low Sulphur Fuel Oil (VLSFO) and Marine Gas Oil (MGO) surcharges that are directly passed through to cargo owners. The regulation's Emission Control Areas (ECAs) impose additional route-specific costs, particularly affecting North Sea, Baltic, and North American coastal trades where 

the limit for sulphur in fuel oil is 0.10% mass by mass

.

**Source:** MARPOL Annex VI Regulations 12-18, 22-28

---

## Issues Requiring Immediate Action

### EEXI Compliance Verification Deadline Extensions


Vessels to which EEXI applies must demonstrate compliance by their next scheduled survey – annual, intermediate or renewal – for the International Air Pollution Prevention Certificate (IAPPC) to be issued or endorsed, or the initial survey before the ship enters service for the International Energy Efficiency Certificate (IEEC) to be issued, whichever is the first on or after 1 January 2023

. However, 

certificates should be issued by the classification society of the vessel or RO latest by 31 May 2024

 for vessels that have not yet completed compliance verification.

**Action Required — Confirm for Your Business:** Verify with your ocean carrier partners whether all vessels in their fleet have completed EEXI technical file approval and received updated International Energy Efficiency Certificates. Non-compliant vessels risk detention.

### New ECA Designations and Implementation Dates


Amendments to MARPOL Annex VI are expected to enter into force on 1 May 2024, with new sulphur limits taking effect from 1 May 2025 for the Canadian Arctic Waters and Norwegian Sea as emission control areas

. 

The Mediterranean Sea is proposed as an Emission Control Area for Sulphur Oxides and Particulate Matter, where the limit for sulphur in fuel oil used on board ships would be 0.10% mass by mass

.

**Action Required — Confirm for Your Business:** Review your European and Arctic trade lanes for potential ECA fuel surcharge impacts starting May 2025.

### U.S. Enforcement Protocol Updates


On June 20, 2019, the United States Coast Guard and the Environmental Protection Agency issued a revised protocol on referral under MARPOL Annex VI, which tells us exactly what these two agencies will do for the enforcement of Annex VI

. 

The EPA and USCG jointly and cooperatively enforce the provisions of Annex VI and APPS, with efforts including inspections, investigations and enforcement actions if a violation is detected

.

**Action Required — Confirm for Your Business:** Ensure your U.S. import documentation processes account for enhanced port state control inspections and potential fuel sampling requirements.

### CII Rating Implications for Vessel Availability


The first annual CII reporting was completed in 2023, with initial ratings given in 2024

. Ships receiving poor CII ratings (D or E) face operational restrictions that could affect vessel availability and schedule reliability on specific trade routes.

**Action Required — Confirm for Your Business:** Request CII rating transparency from your ocean carrier partners to assess potential service disruption risks on key trade lanes.

---

## Operational Impact by Transport Mode

### Ocean Freight
The most significant operational impact stems from the global sulphur cap implementation. 

The rule limits sulphur in fuel oil to 0.50% m/m - a significant reduction from the previous limit of 3.5%

. This translates to VLSFO price premiums of $150-300 per metric ton over High Sulphur Fuel Oil, creating bunker adjustment factors (BAF) that are passed directly to shippers.

Within ECAs, the cost differential is even more pronounced. 

Within specific designated emission control areas the limits are already stricter at 0.10%

, requiring Marine Gas Oil with price premiums of $400-600 per metric ton over VLSFO. Major ECA routes include:
- North American ECA: 200 nautical miles from U.S./Canadian coastline
- Baltic Sea ECA: Entire Baltic Sea area
- North Sea ECA: North Sea and English Channel
- U.S. Caribbean ECA: Around Puerto Rico and U.S. Virgin Islands



While EEXI generally applies to each ship of 400 gross tonnage and above, CII applies to ships 5,000 gross tonnage and above

. The CII requirement creates operational constraints where vessels may need to reduce speeds or optimize routes to maintain good carbon intensity ratings, potentially affecting transit times and capacity allocation.

### Air Freight
MARPOL Annex VI does not directly apply to aviation, but it establishes regulatory precedent for environmental cost pass-through mechanisms that inform air cargo fuel surcharge structures.

### Road Transport
Not applicable - MARPOL Annex VI applies exclusively to maritime operations.

### Customs and Reporting


According to regulation 18.2 of MARPOL Annex VI, it is the responsibility of the Party of the destination port, through its competent authority, to scrutinize fuel oil non-availability reports and take action, as appropriate

. Enhanced port state control inspections require carriers to maintain detailed fuel sampling records and bunker delivery notes, with potential delays for documentation verification.

---

## Key Data and Figures

| Global sulphur limit | 0.50% m/m | Regulation 14 |
| ECA sulphur limit | 0.10% m/m | Regulation 14 |
| EEXI compliance threshold | Ships ≥400 GT | Regulation 23 |
| CII compliance threshold | Ships ≥5,000 GT | Regulation 28 |
| EEXI certification deadline | First survey after 1 Jan 2023 | Regulation 25 |
| CII first ratings issued | 2024 | Resolution MEPC.328(76) |
| Mediterranean ECA entry | 1 May 2025 (proposed) | MEPC.361(79) |
| Arctic Waters/Norwegian Sea ECA | 1 May 2025 (proposed) | MEPC amendments |

---

## Compliance Risk Register

| Risk | Severity | Likelihood | Deadline |
|---|---|---|---|
| Vessel detention for non-compliant fuel | High | Medium | Immediate - ongoing |
| EEXI certification gaps | High | Low | Extended to 31 May 2024 |
| CII rating degradation affecting vessel availability | Medium | High | Annual assessment |
| Enhanced U.S. port state control inspections | Medium | High | Immediate - ongoing |
| New ECA fuel cost impacts | High | Medium | 1 May 2025 |

---

## Recommended Actions

| Priority | Action | Owner | Timeframe |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Audit ocean carrier EEXI compliance status across contracted vessel fleet | Ocean Product | Within 30 days |
| 2 | Update fuel surcharge pass-through mechanisms for new ECA designations | Finance | Q2 2024 |
| 3 | Implement CII rating monitoring in carrier performance scorecards | Ocean Product | Q3 2024 |
| 4 | Review U.S. import documentation for enhanced PSC requirements | Customs | Q2 2024 |
| 5 | Develop Mediterranean ECA cost modeling for European trades | Ocean Product | Q4 2024 |
| 6 | Train operations team on fuel sampling and documentation requirements | Operations | Q3 2024 |

---

## Implementation Timeline

| Date | Milestone | Status |
|---|---|---|
| 1 Jan 2020 | Global 0.50% sulphur cap in force | In force |
| 1 Nov 2022 | EEXI/CII amendments entered into force | In force |
| 1 Jan 2023 | EEXI/CII certification requirements active | In force |
| 31 May 2024 | Extended deadline for EEXI certificates | Pending |
| 1 May 2025 | Mediterranean Sea ECA designation | Pending IMO approval |
| 1 May 2025 | Canadian Arctic/Norwegian Sea ECA | Pending IMO approval |
| 1 Jan 2026 | CII and EEXI effectiveness review deadline | Upcoming |

---

## Open Questions

• **Mediterranean ECA Final Approval**: 

The proposed Mediterranean Sea Emission Control Area for Sulphur Oxides and Particulate Matter

 requires final IMO MEPC adoption, with implementation timing dependent on member state ratification.

• **EEXI Technical Requirements for Retrofits**: Guidance on EEXI recalculation requirements for vessels undergoing major modifications or engine retrofits remains under development through IACS recommendations.

• **CII Rating Enforcement Mechanisms**: While CII ratings are being issued, specific enforcement actions for persistently poor-performing vessels (consecutive D/E ratings) are still being defined by individual flag states.

• **U.S. Criminal Enforcement Scope**: 

The Department of Justice sanctions for intentional violation of MARPOL Annex VI regulation 14 as class D felonies carrying maximum fines of $500,000 for each violation

, but application scope beyond ECA violations remains unclear.

• **Port State Control Harmonization**: Regional enforcement coordination through mechanisms like 

the Bonn Agreement's 2019 formal inclusion of MARPOL Annex VI within its jurisdiction aims to achieve significant decrease in air pollution violations

, but implementation consistency across jurisdictions varies.

---

| IMO 2020 Sulphur Implementation | [https://www.imo.org/en/mediacentre/hottopics/pages/sulphur-2020.aspx](https://www.imo.org/en/mediacentre/hottopics/pages/sulphur-2020.aspx) | Tier 1 - IMO official guidance |
| EEXI and CII FAQ | [https://www.imo.org/en/mediacentre/hottopics/pages/eexi-cii-faq.aspx](https://www.imo.org/en/mediacentre/hottopics/pages/eexi-cii-faq.aspx) | Tier 1 - IMO official guidance |
| U.S. EPA Enforcement Policy | [https://www.epa.gov/enforcement/marpol-annex-vi-and-act-prevent-pollution-ships-apps](https://www.epa.gov/enforcement/marpol-annex-vi-and-act-prevent-pollution-ships-apps) | Tier 2 - National implementing authority |
| U.S. Coast Guard Work Instructions | [https://www.dco.uscg.mil/Portals/9/DCO%20Documents/5p/CG-5PC/CG-CVC/CVC_MMS/CVC-WI-022_signed.pdf](https://www.dco.uscg.mil/Portals/9/DCO%20Documents/5p/CG-5PC/CG-CVC/CVC_MMS/CVC-WI-022_signed.pdf) | Tier 2 - National implementing authority |

fullBrief:
# EU Monitoring, Reporting and Verification (MRV) Regulation

**Regulatory Fact Document** | April 2026  
**Primary source:** [Regulation (EU) 2015/757](https://eur-lex.europa.eu/eli/reg/2015/757/oj/eng) as amended by [Regulation (EU) 2023/957](https://eur-lex.europa.eu/legal-content/EN/TXT/?uri=CELEX:32023R0957)

---

## What This Regulation Is and Why It Applies



The EU Monitoring, Reporting and Verification (MRV) Regulation is Regulation (EU) 2015/757 of the European Parliament and of the Council of 29 April 2015 on the monitoring, reporting and verification of carbon dioxide emissions from maritime transport

, 

as amended by Regulation (EU) 2023/957

. 

The EU MRV Regulation establishes a framework for monitoring, reporting, and verifying CO₂, CH4 and N2O emissions from ships

. 

It is part of the EU's broader strategy to decarbonise transport and improve transparency in the shipping sector

. 

The EU MRV regulation underpins the EU ETS regulation for maritime compliance

.

The regulation applies to 

ships ≥ 5,000 GT (gross tonnage), regardless of flag

, that are 

conducting commercial voyages (cargo or passengers) to, from, or between EU ports

. 

As of January 2025, the scope includes offshore ships and general cargo ships ≥ 400 GT

. 

The regulated entity under the EU MRV Regulation is the shipping company responsible for the operation of the ship

.

For freight forwarders, MRV is operationally critical because it is the data foundation that determines EU ETS surcharges on every shipment. 

It serves as the foundation for carbon tax determination through the EU Emissions Trading System (EU ETS) and FuelEU Maritime regulation

. Without verified MRV reports, vessels cannot comply with either EU ETS or FuelEU Maritime. The MRV data directly determines the carbon cost passed through to freight forwarders on each voyage, making carrier selection and surcharge validation essential business decisions. 

Emissions reporting data in THETIS-MRV is publicly available

, enabling freight forwarders to verify carrier emissions claims and calculate accurate Scope 3 emissions for their customers.

**Source:** Articles 1, 2, 3, 6, 8, 9 of Regulation (EU) 2015/757; Article 1 of Regulation (EU) 2023/957

---

## Issues Requiring Immediate Action

### Annual Emissions Report Deadline - 31 March 2026



On 31 March 2025 verified emission reports for 2024 must be submitted to the administering authorities pursuant to the EU Monitoring, Reporting and Verification Regulation

. 

From 2025, by 31 March of each year, companies must, for each ship under their responsibility, submit an emissions report for the entire reporting period of the previous year, which has been verified as satisfactory by a verifier

. 

Annually, by March 31, verified emissions reports must be submitted to the European Commission, the relevant flag state authorities, and the administering authority via the THETIS-MRV platform

.

**Action Required — Confirm for Your Business:** Verify that all carriers serving your routes have submitted 2025 emissions reports by 31 March 2026. Access THETIS-MRV to confirm carrier compliance and obtain verified emissions factors for your Scope 3 calculations.

### Document of Compliance Verification



Enforcement of EU MRV is through Port State Control (PSC). The PSC will check that there is a DoC on board. If a copy is not on board, the ship is not in compliance with the legislation and is thus liable for fines, or even detention, as determined by the member State

. 

Each Member State shall ensure that any inspection of a ship in a port under its jurisdiction carried out in accordance with Directive 2009/16/EC includes checking that a valid document of compliance is carried on board

.

**Action Required — Confirm for Your Business:** Ensure all booked vessels carry valid Documents of Compliance. Include DoC verification in your carrier due diligence process.

### Expanded Scope from 2025 - Ships 400-5,000 GT



From 1 January 2025, the revised EU MRV regulations encompass general cargo ships between 400 and 5000 GT, as well as offshore ships of 400 GT and above

. 

In addition, from January 1, 2025, EU MRV is extended to apply to offshore ships above 400 GT and general cargo ships below 5,000 GT but not below 400 GT

.

**Action Required — Confirm for Your Business:** Audit your carrier base to identify smaller vessels (400-5,000 GT) that are now in scope. Ensure these carriers have compliant monitoring plans and reporting procedures.

### Maritime Operator Holding Account (MOHA) Requirements



Shipping companies falling within the scope of the EU ETS must also open a Maritime Operator Holding Account ("MOHA") and enter verified emission data from 2024 in the EU ETS Registry by 31 March 2025

. 

Shipping companies will need to open a Maritime Operator Holding Account (MOHA) in the EU Member State which is its administering authority

.

**Action Required — Confirm for Your Business:** Confirm that major carriers have established MOHAs and are submitting data to the Union Registry. MOHA blocking affects vessel operations and your supply chain reliability.

### Non-Compliance Risk - Expulsion Orders



In the case of ships that have failed to comply with the monitoring and reporting requirements for two or more consecutive reporting periods and where other enforcement measures have failed to ensure compliance, the competent authority of the Member State of the port of entry may issue an expulsion order. As a result of the issuing of such an expulsion order, every Member State shall refuse entry of the ship concerned into any of its ports

.

**Action Required — Confirm for Your Business:** Monitor carrier compliance history in THETIS-MRV to avoid booking vessels at risk of expulsion orders.

---

## Operational Impact by Transport Mode

### Ocean Freight Operations

**Direct Impact on Costs:** MRV data feeds directly into EU ETS calculations, determining the exact carbon cost per voyage that carriers pass through as surcharges. 

Emissions in 2024： 40% of emissions in the scope (to be surrendered in 2025) Emissions in 2025： 70% of emissions in the scope (to be surrendered in 2026) Emissions in 2026： 100% of emissions in the scope (to be surrendered in 2027)

. This creates escalating cost pressure that will be reflected in freight rates and dedicated carbon surcharges.

**Carrier Selection Impact:** 

Emissions reporting data in THETIS-MRV is publicly available

, enabling freight forwarders to compare actual carrier performance rather than relying on carrier claims. Use THETIS-MRV data to identify the most carbon-efficient vessels for environmentally sensitive customers.

**Contract Implications:** MRV reporting directly affects FuelEU Maritime compliance and GHG intensity calculations. Carriers with poor MRV compliance may face penalties that increase operational costs, affecting their ability to honor contracted rates.

### Customs and Reporting Systems

**Scope 3 Emissions Reporting:** MRV provides the verified emissions factors needed for ISO 14083-compliant transport emissions calculations. 

These reports should include data such as fuel consumption, distance traveled, time at sea, and cargo carried

. This data enables accurate gCO2/TEU-km calculations for customer sustainability reporting.

**Due Diligence Requirements:** 

Which documents need to be on board for MRV compliance?

 Include MRV Document of Compliance verification in standard carrier qualification procedures. Non-compliant vessels face detention and operational disruption.

---

Article 11

 |

Article 8

 |
| Geographic Scope | EU/EEA ports + voyages to/from/between | Article 7 |
| Penalty Example (Germany) | Up to €50,000 for reporting failures | 

National implementation

 |
| Platform | THETIS-MRV (EMSA) | 

https://mrv.emsa.europa.eu

 |
| Public Data Access | Full emissions data published annually | 

Article 21

 |

## Compliance Risk Register

| Carrier MRV non-compliance affecting bookings | High | Medium | Ongoing verification |
| DoC absence causing vessel detention | High | Low | Every port call |
| 

Expulsion order blocking EU access

 | High | Low | Two consecutive reporting failures |
| Inaccurate Scope 3 emissions reporting | Medium | Medium | Customer reporting deadlines |
| MOHA blocking affecting vessel operations | Medium | Medium | 31 March annually |
| Smaller vessel (400-5,000 GT) non-compliance | Medium | High | 1 January 2025 |

---

## Recommended Actions

| Priority | Action | Owner | Timeframe |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | 

Access THETIS-MRV public database

 to verify carrier emissions data | Sustainability + Ocean Product | Immediate |

fullBrief:
# Getting to Zero Coalition Green Shipping Corridors Initiative

**Regulatory Fact Document** | April 2026
**Primary source:** [Getting to Zero Coalition Annual Progress Report 2024](https://globalmaritimeforum.org/getting-to-zero-coalition/)

---

## What This Regulation Is and Why It Applies

The Getting to Zero Coalition Green Shipping Corridors Initiative is not a regulation in the traditional legal sense, but rather 

a partnership of more than 200 companies, key governments, and intergovernmental organisations working to make zero-emission fuels and vessels a scalable and affordable reality by 2030

. 

The Coalition is managed by the Global Maritime Forum, which initially founded it together with the World Economic Forum and Friends of Ocean Action

.

The initiative operates primarily through green shipping corridors – 

specific trade routes where the feasibility of zero-emission shipping is catalysed by public and private action, accelerating shipping's transition to zero emissions by testing and deploying fuels, vessels, and infrastructure in a coordinated way

. 

In the last year, 18 new green shipping corridor initiatives emerged worldwide, and two-fifths of existing initiatives advanced to a new phase of development. At least six green corridor initiatives have moved towards enabling real-world implementation

.

For freight forwarders, this initiative creates immediate operational implications across three dimensions: First, green corridors offer verifiably lower-emission ocean freight options that command premium pricing from sustainability-conscious shippers with Science-Based Targets (SBTi) or Corporate Sustainability Reporting Directive (CSRD) commitments. Second, corridor development signals where zero-emission bunkering infrastructure will be first available, affecting route planning and service offerings. Third, early knowledge of active corridors enables proactive sales positioning to sustainability-focused clients who increasingly demand demonstrable emission reductions in their supply chains.



The signatories of the Clydebank Declaration support the establishment of green shipping corridors – zero-emission maritime routes between 2 (or more) ports. The collective aim is to establish at least 6 green corridors by the middle of this decade, while aiming to scale activity up in the following years

.

**Source:** Global Maritime Forum Getting to Zero Coalition reports, Clydebank Declaration (COP26)

---

## Issues Requiring Immediate Action

### Commercial Product Development for Green Ocean Freight



The number of green corridor initiatives increased by 40% in 2023 to a total of 62 initiatives globally. A third of existing corridors have advanced to a new phase of exploration, including feasibility studies, implementation roadmaps, and cost assessments. Six frontrunning initiatives are now preparing for real-world implementation

. 

**Action Required — Confirm for Your Business:** Your Ocean Product team needs to identify which active green corridors overlap with your existing trade lanes and develop green ocean freight product offerings with verifiable emission reduction claims.

### Client Segmentation for Sustainability-Conscious Shippers



Just the six frontrunning initiatives alone could require over two million tonnes of hydrogen-based fuel per year by 2030. With an increased number of governments focusing on incentivising the adoption of hydrogen in multiple sectors, providing shipping-specific support could catalyse both national hydrogen economies and the decarbonisation of the maritime sector

.

**Action Required — Confirm for Your Business:** Your Sales team needs to identify clients with SBTi commitments, CSRD obligations, or specific Scope 3 emission reduction targets who will pay premiums for green corridor routing.

### Infrastructure Availability Assessment



Timely, accessible public support to bridge the fuel cost gap must be the immediate priority for governments committed to making green corridors a success. Coalescing around green market-maker schemes, such as H2Global, may be the most cost-effective and timely option

.

**Action Required — Confirm for Your Business:** Your Operations team must assess alternative fuel bunkering availability at corridor endpoints to validate service promises to clients.

### Premium Pricing Model Development



A 35-45% increase in total cost of ownership is expected for vessel operators in 2030. Given the expected demand from cargo owners, mechanisms such as targeted demand coalitions and a corridor specific book-and-claim system could enable sharing of the additional cost across the value chain

.

**Action Required — Confirm for Your Business:** Your Finance team needs to develop premium pricing models for green corridor services based on cost pass-through mechanisms from carriers.

### Corridor-Specific Contract Terms



Given the challenging economics of zero-emission solutions, green corridor initiatives must put business-as-usual thinking aside and prioritize commercial innovation around fuel procurement and chartering/cargo

.

**Action Required — Confirm for Your Business:** Your Legal team must develop contract language for green corridor services including emission verification, force majeure provisions for fuel unavailability, and premium cost allocation.

---

## Operational Impact by Transport Mode

### Ocean Transport

Green corridors directly impact ocean freight operations through designated zero-emission shipping routes. 

The Australia-Japan iron ore route offers a promising zero-emission fuel pathway owing to favorable production conditions and significant announced green hydrogen capacity. Australian companies have announced plans to build 29 GW of hydrogen electrolysis capacity by 2030

.

Cost pass-through mechanisms operate as follows: Carriers participating in green corridors face 

a 50-65% increase in the total cost of ownership for vessel operators in 2030

 on routes like Australia-Japan. This translates to green shipping surcharges that freight forwarders must incorporate into client pricing. The premium structure typically includes fuel cost differential, infrastructure access fees, and carbon accounting verification charges.



The Asia-Europe container route, which currently generates more greenhouse gas emissions than any other single global trading lane, also presents a promising option for developing a green corridor. The pipeline of announced green hydrogen projects along the route amounts to 62 GW of electrolyzer capacity in Europe, the Middle East, and Australia

.

### Customs and Reporting

Green corridor operations create new documentation requirements for emission verification and carbon accounting. 

A book and claim chain of custody system is a method for tracking specific attributes of fuel (such as GHG emissions) separately from the actual physical movement of the fuel within the transportation supply chain. Low-emission solution providers can "book" the emissions profile associated with fuels into a tracking system. The system then allows a different customer, regardless of location, to "claim" the emissions profile of that fuel for climate disclosures

.

Freight forwarders must integrate these book-and-claim documentation processes into their standard customs clearance and compliance procedures, requiring system updates and staff training.

### Contract Management

Green corridor services require specialized contract terms addressing fuel availability, emission verification, and cost volatility. Standard freight forwarding contracts need amendments covering force majeure provisions for zero-emission fuel shortages, emission performance guarantees, and premium cost allocation mechanisms between forwarder and client.

---

| Clydebank Declaration Target | 6 corridors by 2025 | COP26 Declaration |
| Clydebank Signatory Countries | 22 countries | COP26 Declaration |

---

## Compliance Risk Register

| Risk | Severity | Likelihood | Deadline |
|---|---|---|---|
| Client emission reporting obligations unmet | High | Medium | Ongoing (CSRD/SBTi deadlines) |
| Green corridor service promises unfulfilled due to infrastructure gaps | High | Medium | 2025-2026 |
| Premium pricing rejected by cost-sensitive clients | Medium | High | Immediate |
| Book-and-claim documentation failures | Medium | Medium | 2025 |
| Force majeure claims from zero-emission fuel shortages | Medium | Medium | 2026-2027 |
| Competitive disadvantage from late green corridor adoption | High | Medium | 2025 |

---

## Recommended Actions

| Priority | Action | Owner | Timeframe |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Map existing trade lanes against 62 active green corridor initiatives | Ocean Product | Q2 2026 |
| 2 | Develop green ocean freight product with emission verification | Ocean Product + Sustainability | Q2 2026 |
| 3 | Create client segmentation for sustainability-conscious shippers | Sales + Sustainability | Q2 2026 |
| 4 | Establish premium pricing models for green corridor services | Finance + Ocean Product | Q2 2026 |
| 5 | Assess zero-emission fuel bunkering availability at key ports | Operations + Procurement | Q3 2026 |
| 6 | Develop contract terms for green corridor services | Legal | Q3 2026 |
| 7 | Implement book-and-claim documentation processes | IT + Customs | Q3 2026 |
| 8 | Train sales teams on green corridor value proposition | Sales | Q3 2026 |
| 9 | Establish partnerships with green corridor operators | Ocean Product | Q4 2026 |
| 10 | Monitor Clydebank Declaration implementation progress | Sustainability | Ongoing |

---

## Implementation Timeline

| Date | Milestone | Status |
|---|---|---|
| 2021 | Getting to Zero Coalition founded with 70 signatories | Complete |
| November 2021 | Clydebank Declaration signed at COP26 by 22 countries | Complete |
| 2023 | 40% growth in corridor initiatives to 62 globally | Complete |
| 2024 | Six frontrunning corridors preparing implementation | In Progress |
| 2025 | Clydebank target: at least 6 operational green corridors | Upcoming |
| 2026 | Infrastructure "feasibility wall" critical period | Upcoming |
| 2030 | Coalition target: commercially viable zero-emission vessels | Upcoming |
| 2030 | "Many more" corridors operational per Clydebank Declaration | Upcoming |
| 2050 | Full shipping decarbonization target | Long-term |

---

## Open Questions

• **Government policy support**: 

Future progress is threatened by a "feasibility wall" due to the cost of switching to zero-emissions fuels. The lack of national policy incentives to bridge the cost gap is now the number one bottleneck

 – timing and scale of government financial support remains unclear.

• **Fuel availability scaling**: While hydrogen production capacity is announced, actual delivery timing and pricing for zero-emission fuels at scale depends on infrastructure deployment schedules not yet finalized.

• **Book-and-claim standardization**: Maritime book-and-claim systems for emission tracking are still being developed – final standards and verification requirements pending.

• **Global South participation**: 

There is a need to explore what policies and sources of finance can support the realization of green corridors and zero-emission fuel supply chains in the Global South. Corridors based in the Global South face specific challenges that will need to be addressed in a bespoke fashion. Closer engagement with multilateral development banks can help identify solutions

.

• **China participation**: 

Asian maritime giants like China, India, Vietnam and Indonesia are not signatories to the Clydebank Declaration

 – this limits Asia-Europe corridor effectiveness.

• **Carbon pricing mechanisms**: 

The objective of the USD 2/tonne tax is only to support research and development, and is not designed to provide the incentive for a switch to zero-emissions/low-emissions fuels, which would need to be a considerably higher carbon tax. A carbon tax is increasingly accepted as the most administratively and operationally straightforward market-based solution

 – actual carbon pricing implementation timeline unclear.

---

fullBrief:
# Alternative Fuels Insight (IRENA/IMO)

**Regulatory Fact Document** | April 2026
**Primary source:** [DNV Alternative Fuels Insight Platform](https://www.dnv.com/services/alternative-fuels-insights-afi--128171/)

---

## What This Regulation Is and Why It Applies

The Alternative Fuels Insight (AFI) platform is not a regulation itself, but rather 

a freely accessible tool for visualizing and analysing comprehensive and high-quality data, supplemented by an extensive collection of technical resources

 operated by DNV in partnership with IRENA (International Renewable Energy Agency) and the International Maritime Organization (IMO). 

The latest developments in shipping's uptake of alternative fuel provided by DNV's Alternative Fuels Insight (AFI) platform. AFI is an open platform that allows stakeholders across the value chain to assess the uptake of alternative fuels and technologies.



While AFI itself is not legally binding, it serves as the critical data foundation that freight forwarders need to understand carrier compliance strategies under mandatory regulations like EU FuelEU Maritime, IMO's Net-Zero Framework, and regional emission control measures. 

It will assist shipowners in selecting a fuel for the vessels they order today and in coming years, and fuel suppliers weighing up investments in new bunkering infrastructure.

 This platform directly impacts freight forwarding operations because alternative fuel availability at specific ports determines carrier compliance costs, which flow through as surcharges to freight invoices.

The platform covers all major shipping routes globally and tracks 

LNG, LPG and methanol

 alongside 

methanol, ammonia, hydrogen and LPG, in addition to the existing data on LNG, batteries and scrubbers.

 For freight forwarders, this intelligence is essential for route optimization, carrier selection, and surcharge forecasting under FuelEU Maritime and similar compliance regimes.

**Source:** DNV AFI Platform Documentation | IRENA/IMO Partnership Agreement

---

## Issues Requiring Immediate Action

### Alternative Fuel Availability Assessment for Key Trade Lanes



LNG bunkering services are now available in 198 ports worldwide, with an additional 78 ports planning to introduce bunkering facilities.

 However, methanol infrastructure remains limited with 

only one methanol bunkering vessel in operation and eight on order.

 Freight forwarders must immediately audit their primary trade lanes against AFI data to identify ports where carriers can access lower-emission fuels.

**Action Required — Confirm for Your Business:** Map your top 20 port pairs against AFI's bunkering infrastructure database to identify where your carriers can access alternative fuels and reduce FuelEU surcharges.

### Carrier Fleet Alternative Fuel Capability Verification



According to data from DNV's Alternative Fuels Insight (AFI) platform, new orders for alternative-fuelled vessels reached 19.8 million gross tonnes (GT) in the first six months of 2025, exceeding the 2024 figure by 78%.

 

With nearly as many alternative-fuel ships on order as currently in service, the fleet of such vessels is set to almost double between 2024 and 2028, based on the current order book.



**Action Required — Confirm for Your Business:** For each major carrier you use, verify which vessels in their fleet are alternative fuel-capable and when new alternative fuel vessels will enter service on your key trades.

### FuelEU Surcharge Impact Modeling



The LNG pathway's lower costs of compliance are driven by the fact that it offers immediate GHG reductions while ammonia and methanol dual fuel vessels must either buy large quantities of expensive green versions of those fuels to comply, from day one, or use VLSFO and pay for remedial units.

 This directly impacts freight forwarding margins as 

the Owners shall provide to the Charterers the calculation and independently validated information used in the calculation of a surcharge equal to the FuelEU Penalty expected

 under standard time charter arrangements.

**Action Required — Confirm for Your Business:** Establish surcharge pass-through mechanisms in carrier contracts that account for alternative fuel availability at origin/destination ports and interim bunkering locations.

---

## Operational Impact by Transport Mode

### Ocean Freight Operations

The AFI platform's data shows significant variance in alternative fuel infrastructure by region and port. 

The Maritime and Port Authority of Singapore (MPA) recorded a fourfold increase in LNG bunkering volumes in 2024, reaching nearly 340,000 tonnes.

 In contrast, 

In Q1 2025, volumes in Rotterdam increased by 7% compared with the same period in 2024 and Singapore reported 18% growth over the first five months of 2025 versus 2024. LNG bunkering is developing rapidly in the Western Mediterranean and China too, with volumes increasing by over 60% in Shanghai in the first five months of 2025 versus that period in 2024.



For ocean freight operations, this creates a three-tier cost structure:
1. **Tier 1 Ports (Strong Alternative Fuel Infrastructure):** Singapore, Rotterdam, Shanghai, Antwerp-Bruges - carriers can access LNG, biomethane, and emerging methanol supplies, resulting in lower FuelEU compliance costs
2. **Tier 2 Ports (Limited Alternative Fuel Access):** Most secondary European and Asian ports - carriers rely primarily on truck-to-ship LNG delivery with higher costs
3. **Tier 3 Ports (No Alternative Fuel Infrastructure):** Carriers must use conventional marine gas oil with maximum FuelEU penalties

### Carrier Selection and Route Optimization



LNG was the clear fuel of choice, accounting for 87 new vessels ordered, totaling 14.2 million GT so far in 2025. The fuel remains dominant in the container segment, with 13.6 million GT (81 vessels). Methanol has also shown strong momentum, with 4.6 million GT (40 vessels) ordered across the container, RoPax, tanker, offshore, and car carrier segments.



This vessel ordering pattern means freight forwarders should prioritize carriers with LNG-capable vessels for immediate cost advantages, while monitoring methanol vessel deployments for future flexibility. 

The number of LNG-fuelled vessels in operation grew by an impressive 33%, reaching 638 vessels worldwide. This expansion is expected to continue, with over 1,200 LNG-fuelled vessels projected to be operational by 2028.



### Cost Pass-Through Mechanisms



If the aggregated Compliance Balance has a negative value, the Owners shall provide to the Charterers the calculation and independently validated information used in the calculation of a surcharge equal to the FuelEU Penalty expected for that previous month or that Voyage (whichever applies) and upon redelivery (the Surcharge). The Surcharge shall be payable in accordance with subclause (f).



Freight forwarders face direct exposure to alternative fuel cost differentials through standardized charter party clauses. Where carriers cannot access alternative fuels at specific ports, penalty costs flow through to freight bills.

---

## Key Data and Figures

| Parameter | Value | Source |
|---|---|---|
| Global LNG Bunkering Ports | 198 operational, 78 planned | SEA-LNG 2024 |
| Global Methanol Bunkering Vessels | 1 operational, 8 on order | SEA-LNG 2024 |
| Alternative Fuel Vessel Orders 2025 | 19.8 million GT (78% increase) | DNV AFI Platform |
| LNG-Fueled Vessels Operating | 638 vessels (33% growth) | SEA-LNG 2024 |
| Projected LNG Fleet by 2028 | Over 1,200 vessels | SEA-LNG 2024 |
| Singapore LNG Bunkering 2024 | 340,000 tonnes (4x increase) | MPA Singapore |
| Container Ship Alt-Fuel Orders 2024 | 69% of all new orders | DNV AFI |
| LNG Bunkering Vessels Global | Over 60 vessels (22% increase) | SEA-LNG 2024 |

---

## Compliance Risk Register

| Risk | Severity | Likelihood | Deadline |
|---|---|---|---|
| FuelEU penalty pass-through from carriers using conventional fuel | High | High | Every voyage to EU from Jan 2025 |
| Limited methanol bunkering infrastructure constraining carrier options | Medium | High | 2025-2027 |
| Regional alternative fuel price differentials affecting route economics | Medium | Medium | Ongoing |
| Carrier pooling strategies affecting individual voyage surcharges | Medium | Medium | Annual compliance periods |

---

## Recommended Actions

| Priority | Action | Owner | Timeframe |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Create AFI platform access and conduct port-specific alternative fuel mapping for top 50 trade lanes | Ocean Product + Procurement | Q2 2026 |
| 2 | Establish carrier alternative fuel capability database with vessel-specific tracking | Ocean Product | Q2-Q3 2026 |
| 3 | Develop FuelEU surcharge forecasting model based on AFI bunkering data | Finance + Ocean Product | Q3 2026 |
| 4 | Negotiate alternative fuel cost pass-through clauses in carrier contracts | Procurement | Q3-Q4 2026 |
| 5 | Implement route optimization algorithm incorporating alternative fuel port availability | IT + Ocean Product | Q4 2026 |
| 6 | Train sales team on alternative fuel impact on customer pricing | Sales | Q3 2026 |

---

## Implementation Timeline

| Date | Milestone | Status |
|---|---|---|
| January 2025 | FuelEU Maritime regulation enforcement begins | In force |
| March 2025 | IMO Net-Zero Framework reward mechanism decision | Pending |
| April 2026 | Current date - immediate action required | Active |
| Q2 2026 | AFI platform integration deadline | Upcoming |
| Q4 2026 | Alternative fuel surcharge mechanisms operational | Target |
| 2027 | Methanol infrastructure scaling expected | Projected |
| 2028 | LNG fleet doubling completion | Projected |

---

## Open Questions

• **Methanol bunkering infrastructure scaling timeline:** AFI shows only one operational methanol bunkering vessel globally. When will sufficient infrastructure develop to support the 40 methanol-capable vessels on order?

• **Regional alternative fuel pricing convergence:** Will alternative fuel prices converge globally, or will regional differences create permanent route optimization advantages?

• **IMO reward mechanism design:** How will the March 2027 decision on the Net-Zero Framework reward mechanism affect the comparative advantage of different alternative fuel pathways?

• **Biofuel supply chain visibility:** AFI tracks infrastructure but not biofuel supply availability. How will supply constraints affect actual alternative fuel accessibility at equipped ports?

• **Cross-fuel conversion capabilities:** As vessels become dual-fuel or multi-fuel capable, how will carriers optimize fuel selection on a voyage-by-voyage basis?

---

title: Norway Zero-Emission Shipping
note: World's first zero-emission fjord requirements by 2026.
reasoning: Norway leads with mandatory zero-emission requirements — signals direction for other jurisdictions.
whatIsIt: Norway's pioneering regulations requiring zero-emission vessel operations in Norwegian fjords from 2026.
whyMatters: Norway's requirements signal the direction for other jurisdictions. Freight forwarders routing through Scandinavian ports should monitor for compliance implications on vessel selection.

fullBrief:
# Norway Zero-Emission Shipping Requirements in World Heritage Fjords

**Regulatory Fact Document** | April 2026
**Primary source:** [Environmental Safety Regulations Amendment](https://www.regjeringen.no/en/whats-new/zero-emission-requirements-for-passenger-ships-in-world-heritage-fjords/id3097475/)

---

## What This Regulation Is and Why It Applies



The Norwegian Maritime Authority has enacted amendments to Norway's Environmental Safety Regulations that impose mandatory zero-emission operating requirements for all passenger ships entering Norway's World Heritage fjords

. 

Since 1 January 2026, all passenger ships below 10,000 GT must operate on energy sources that produce no direct carbon dioxide (CO₂) or methane (CH₄) emissions while within the World Heritage fjords. From 1 January 2032, the same requirement will extend to ships of 10,000 GT and above

.



The World Heritage Fjords include five fjords: Nærøyfjorden, Aurlandsfjorden, Geirangerfjorden, Sunnylvsfjorden, and Tafjorden

. 

Responsibility for compliance rests with the shipping company

. 

The requirements apply regardless of whether ships operate entirely within the fjords or only transit them

.

This regulation creates the world's first mandatory zero-emission zone for shipping operations, fundamentally altering access requirements for freight forwarders routing cargo and passengers through Norwegian waters. For freight forwarders, this means vessel selection for Norwegian fjord routing now depends on propulsion technology rather than traditional commercial factors like schedule, capacity, or cost alone. The regulation affects all passenger vessels, which includes ferries carrying cargo and passengers—a critical transport link for goods moving in and out of Norway's coastal communities.

**Source:** Environmental Safety Regulations Amendment, Norwegian Maritime Authority

---

## Issues Requiring Immediate Action

### Vessel Compliance Assessment for Current Routes



All passenger ships below 10,000 GT must operate on energy sources that produce no direct carbon dioxide (CO₂) or methane (CH₄) emissions while within the World Heritage fjords since 1 January 2026

. 

The requirements apply regardless of whether ships operate entirely within the fjords or only transit them

.

**Action Required — Confirm for Your Business:** Identify all current and planned routes through Nærøyfjorden, Aurlandsfjorden, Geirangerfjorden, Sunnylvsfjorden, and Tafjorden. Confirm propulsion technology and fuel systems for each vessel serving these routes.

### Shore Power Connection Requirements



Shipping companies must ensure that their ships connect to shore power systems whenever compatible facilities are available to minimise emissions when berthed in fjord ports

. 

The government has allocated NOK 100 million in support to Aurland Port Authority in the 2025 state budget for shore power establishment in Flåm

.

**Action Required — Confirm for Your Business:** Verify shore power capabilities for all vessels calling Norwegian fjord ports. Confirm electrical connection specifications and operational procedures.

### Alternative Fuel Documentation Requirements



Ships must carry onboard documentation demonstrating compliance with sustainability and GHG reduction criteria

. 

Requirements for fuel sustainability and certification are aligned to FuelEU Maritime and the EU Renewable Energy Directive (RED). Fuel types such as blue hydrogen must be certified under FuelEU Maritime and demonstrate required GHG reductions

.

**Action Required — Confirm for Your Business:** Review current fuel supply chain documentation systems. Confirm certification requirements for any alternative fuels used in Norwegian operations.

---

## Operational Impact by Transport Mode

### Ocean Freight Services



The regulation is technology neutral, allowing shipowners flexibility in selecting compliant energy solutions. Zero emission energy sources may include battery electric systems, green hydrogen, green ammonia, or other fuels meeting the regulatory definition

. 

Biogas is permitted as an alternative to full zero emission solutions, including beyond 2032, as long as it achieves 50% to 65% GHG reduction depending on production date, is not produced from food or feed crops, is bunkered within the final month prior to entering the fjords, the quantity bunkered matches the expected energy demand during the fjord voyage, and is kept separate from fossil fuels until bunkering

.

For freight forwarders, vessel charter costs will increase due to limited availability of compliant vessels. 

Larger vessels can continue operating as now until 2032 and have more time to adjust to the net-zero emissions requirements. The phased approach recognises that the fuel technology for the largest ships is still being developed

.

### Ferry Operations



The nation's government has already mandated all ferries operating along its coastline transition to electric propulsion by 2030

. 

More than 60 electrical ferries are expected to go seaborne in Norway over the next few years

. Ferry services represent critical cargo transport links for goods moving to Norwegian communities, making this transition essential for freight continuity.

### Cruise and Passenger Services



From 1st January 2026, yachts under 10,000 GT and certified for a maximum of 12 passengers will still be permitted to enter the UNESCO-listed Geirangerfjord and Nærøyfjord. However, larger yachts carrying more than 12 passengers must be zero-emission to gain access

.

, but specific penalty amounts and enforcement procedures have not been published
- **Liquid biofuel acceptance:**

- **Pilot fuel thresholds:** 

Limited quantities of traditional "pilot fuels" may still be used where necessary to ignite alternative fuels

, but specific quantity limits await clarification
- **Technology evolution impact:** 

The phased approach recognises that the fuel technology for the largest ships is still being developed

, potentially affecting the 2032 deadline

# Lloyd's Register Maritime Decarbonisation Hub

**Service Intelligence Document** | April 2026
**Primary source:** [Lloyd's Register Maritime Decarbonisation Hub](https://www.thedecarbhub.org/)

---

## What This Service Platform Is and Why It Applies



The Lloyd's Register Maritime Decarbonisation Hub (commonly known as the Decarb Hub) is an independent, non-profit initiative established through a partnership between Lloyd's Register Foundation — a global charity with a mission to engineer a safer world — and Lloyd's Register Group, a global provider of maritime professional services with over 260 years of heritage. This partnership brings together the Foundation's public benefit mandate and focus on societal resilience, with the Group's deep technical expertise and trusted relationships across the maritime industry.





The Hub was established as an independent, non-profit organisation in 2020, and was formally incorporated as Lloyd's Register Maritime Decarbonisation Hub Limited on 3 March 2023.

 

Its mission is to drive the safe, sustainable, and human-centric transition of the maritime industry, by building coalitions that deliver programmes of research, insights, and policy to unlock shipping's decarbonisation and accelerate zero emissions fuel adoption.



This matters specifically to freight forwarders because the Hub provides critical data and analysis tools that directly impact carrier selection, contract negotiations, and regulatory compliance costs. 

The Hub provides key stakeholders in the value chain with guidance at every step of their journey as they reduce the carbon intensity of their operations.

 For freight forwarders, this translates to access to vessel-level performance data that enables informed carrier selection based on environmental performance, verification of carrier sustainability claims, and forecasting of carrier cost exposure under tightening regulations like EU ETS and FuelEU Maritime.

**Source:** Lloyd's Register Maritime Decarbonisation Hub website, UK Companies House records, Lloyd's Register Foundation publications

---

## Issues Requiring Immediate Action

### Carrier Environmental Benchmarking Integration



Access CII and AER ratings to support chartering, vetting, and investment decisions through vessel-level ratings and historical trends to analyse emissions performance over time to optimise fleet strategy.

 Freight forwarders must integrate this data into their carrier selection processes to meet growing shipper demands for environmental performance transparency.

**Action Required — Confirm for Your Business:** How will your ocean product team access and integrate Lloyd's Register fleet analytics data into carrier selection criteria? What systems need updating to incorporate vessel-level carbon intensity ratings?

### FuelEU Maritime Cost Forecasting



Analysis by Lloyd's Register reveals that by 2035, the financial impact of FuelEU Maritime could already have overtaken the EU ETS. By 2050, the cost in FuelEU penalties could be six to eight times greater than the cost of buying EUAs.

 

For a large handy bulk carrier, the cost of EUAs in 2026 would be €0.58 million, while the FuelEU Maritime penalty would be €0.20 million. But by 2035, the FuelEU Maritime penalty would be €0.71 million, while the EUA price would stay similar. Looking to 2050, the FuelEU Maritime penalty reaches €3.60 million.



**Action Required — Confirm for Your Business:** How will your pricing team incorporate these escalating cost projections into long-term contracts? What mechanisms exist to pass through carrier FuelEU penalties to shippers?

### Alternative Fuel Readiness Assessment



The MJTTF's interim training frameworks to prepare seafarers for working on ammonia, methanol, and hydrogen-fuelled ships was developed with The Decarb Hub as technical lead. The frameworks aim to set harmonised global standards for training institutions, regulators, and companies to ensure safe operations with alternative fuels.



**Action Required — Confirm for Your Business:** Which carriers in your network have alternative fuel readiness assessments? How will your procurement team evaluate carrier investments in decarbonisation technology?

### EU ETS and CII Compliance Deadline Management



Annual emissions data and relevant supporting evidence should be submitted to LR in the Emissions Verifier portal on or before 31 March 2026.

 

Shipowners must submit vessel reports by 31 January 2026 after the initial reporting period closed on 31 December 2025, with third-party verification due by 31 March and compliance logged by 30 April. Penalties (if any) will then be issued on or around 30 June along with FuelEU Documents of Compliance.



**Action Required — Confirm for Your Business:** Which carriers have confirmed their compliance with these reporting deadlines? What contingency plans exist if key carriers face EU ETS penalties or FuelEU non-compliance?

---

## Operational Impact by Transport Mode

### Ocean Freight Operations



Seasearcher embeds voyage-level CO₂ estimates, CII and AER ratings, efficiency trends, and competitor benchmarking into a unified platform. Emissions performance data from Siglar Carbon is refreshed every 24 hours and automatically validated for speed and carbon price changes.

 This real-time data enables freight forwarders to make informed carrier selection decisions and respond to shipper environmental performance requests.

The Lloyd's Register classification services cover vessel monitoring and verification for multiple regulatory frameworks. 

Each year, owners, managers or charterers operating cargo and passenger ships of 5000GT and above calling at an EEA port will need to collect and report data for 100% of energy used for voyages between two EEA ports of call and at berth, on a well-to-wake basis, and 50% of energy used for voyages between EEA and non-EU ports.



Cost pass-through mechanisms vary by carrier and contract structure. 

EU ETS and FuelEU Maritime costs are combined into one clear surcharge, with surcharges based on recognized emission factors and market indices, following the cost recovery principle.

 Freight forwarders must understand how these surcharges flow through to their invoices and contract negotiations.

### Customs and Reporting Integration



CII connects directly to EU ETS exposure, FuelEU Maritime trajectory, and corporate sustainability reporting.

 

Classification societies including DNV and Lloyd's Register emphasize incorporating operational carbon performance into dry docking scope, energy-saving device retrofits, hull coating selection, fleet renewal timing, and resale strategy.



The Hub's compliance tools provide integrated reporting capabilities across multiple regulatory frameworks, reducing administrative burden for freight forwarders managing environmental reporting requirements across their carrier network.

# Blue Visby Solution Digital Platform for Maritime Emissions Optimization

**Regulatory Fact Document** | April 2026
**Primary source:** [Blue Visby Solution Official Platform](https://www.bluevisby.com/)

---

## What This Tool Is and Why It Applies



Blue Visby Solution is a multilateral platform for reducing shipping GHG emissions by 15% through eradicating the practice of "Sail Fast, Then Wait"

. Unlike traditional maritime regulations, this is a commercial digital optimization platform that has emerged as a critical compliance tool in response to 

the EU ETS expanding and IMO regulations tightening

.



The Blue Visby Solution deploys state-of-the-art systems – optimization software, technologies for weather and maritime conditions monitoring and forecasting, earth observation systems, and electronic charts – but requires no new on-board systems or retrofits and is based on a contractual architecture that is designed to be compatible with the standard terms of maritime contracts, and does not require new legislation or regulations

.

The platform addresses 

the IMO regulatory requirements of the Carbon Intensity Indicator (CII), as well as the commitments of charterers, traders and cargo owners under SCOPE 3

. For freight forwarders, this represents a significant operational tool that clients will increasingly demand as shipping lines adopt speed optimization to meet emissions compliance requirements. 

As the shipping industry enters a new regulatory era – with the EU ETS expanding and IMO regulations tightening – the Blue Visby Solution represents a pragmatic and forward-looking tool. By reducing fuel use and emissions through better timing, the platform helps shipowners and charterers align commercial performance with environmental responsibility

.

**Source:** Blue Visby Solution platform documentation and Bureau Veritas validation reports

---

## Issues Requiring Immediate Action

### Client Demand for Lower-Carbon Ocean Freight Options


Eradicating SFTW will allow ships to reduce speed, thereby reducing the carbon footprint of the maritime industry by about 15% or overall 45 million tonnes of CO2 across the tanker fleet and bulker fleets, based on figures for 2019

. Freight forwarders must understand how this tool impacts their service offerings.

**Action Required — Confirm for Your Business:** Determine which ocean carriers in your network are adopting Blue Visby or similar optimization platforms, and how to incorporate these emissions reductions into client sustainability reporting.

### EU ETS Cost Pass-Through Implications


As the shipping industry enters a new regulatory era – with the EU ETS expanding and IMO regulations tightening – the Blue Visby Solution represents a pragmatic and forward-looking tool. By reducing fuel use and emissions through better timing, the platform helps shipowners and charterers align commercial performance with environmental responsibility

. This directly affects EU ETS allowance costs.

**Action Required — Confirm for Your Business:** Assess how Blue Visby-enabled voyages impact EU ETS surcharge calculations and client cost structures for Europe-bound cargo.

### Contractual Integration Requirements


The participants of the Blue Visby Solution incorporate into their charter parties and cargo sale contracts a set of terms that implement the Blue Visby Operations System and Blue GA, which create the contractual basis for the operational system of the Blue Visby Solution. They also become members of an association modelled on Protection & Indemnity Clubs, the Blue Visby Mutual Association

.

**Action Required — Confirm for Your Business:** Review whether your freight forwarding agreements need modification to accommodate Blue Visby contractual architecture when clients request optimized voyages.

### Performance Verification and Reporting


The Blue Visby Solution team and BV will continue to analyze simulation results, validate assumptions, and jointly consolidate findings based on a representative sample of anonymized voyages, employing arrival time optimization as a practical and impactful decarbonization strategy. This process aims to provide the industry with transparent, verifiable data supporting the system's impact

.

**Action Required — Confirm for Your Business:** Establish processes for verifying emissions reductions achieved through Blue Visby-optimized voyages for client sustainability reporting.

---

## Operational Impact by Transport Mode

### Ocean Freight Operations


The Blue Visby Solution delivers emissions reductions of about 15% through a speed reduction of about 1 knot on average, which is well within the operational parameters of the existing commercial fleet

. This impacts voyage scheduling and requires coordination between freight forwarders and shipping lines.



NAPA has conducted extensive studies using its unparalleled data and systems on over 150 000 voyages and over 1,500 ships. The results show a median CO2 reduction of 15.9% over analyzed voyages, with a typical speed reduction of voyages of about 1.0kn

.

**Cost Pass-Through Mechanism:** 

The Blue Visby Solution includes a multilateral sharing mechanism for the costs and benefits of its implementation amongst all stakeholders of the vessel's voyage

. Fuel savings from speed reduction are shared among shipowners, charterers, and cargo interests through a "Blue GA" (General Average-inspired) mechanism.

**Surcharge Implications:** 

The parties also had a choice as to whether to calculate the financial value of fuel savings and of the prolongation of the ocean passage by using contract rates or market rates provided by the Baltic Exchanges (also a Blue Visby Consortium member). The parties reached commercial agreement as to the applicable benchmark for the purposes of the benefit-sharing mechanism

.

### Contract and Documentation Impact


The Blue Visby Solution is based on a contractual architecture that is designed to be compatible with the standard terms of maritime contracts, and does not require new legislation or regulations. The Blue Visby Solution leverages one of the most enduring features of the world of maritime trade: Party autonomy and freedom of contract under English law

.

**Documentation Requirements:** Freight forwarders may need to incorporate Blue Visby optimization clauses into Bills of Lading and freight contracts when clients specifically request carbon-optimized shipping.

whatIsIt: The Carbon Offsetting and Reduction Scheme for International Aviation (CORSIA) under ICAO Annex 16 Volume IV requires airlines to offset CO₂ emissions growth above 2019 baseline levels on international routes. Phase 1 (2024-2026) is mandatory for states that volunteered. Enforced by national civil aviation authorities. Airlines must purchase CORSIA Eligible Emissions Units (CEUs) to cover excess emissions.
whyMatters: CORSIA offset costs flow directly into air cargo fuel surcharges. IATA projects total compliance costs reaching $1.7 billion in 2026, up from $1.3 billion in 2025. For freight forwarders: (1) air cargo rates on international routes include CORSIA cost pass-through, (2) CEU prices projected at $25-36/tonne CO₂ by 2027, potentially spiking to $60+/t if supply is scarce, (3) only 15.84 million credits issued from one eligible programme (Guyana REDD+) — supply constraints are real, (4) airlines will add CORSIA as a separate surcharge line on air waybills.
keyData: Phase 1: 2024-2026 (mandatory for volunteering states), Phase 2: 2027-2035 (broader coverage)
Baseline: 2019 emissions levels — airlines offset growth above this
CEU price range: $25-36/tCO₂ projected 2027, potentially $60+ if supply scarce
2026 compliance cost: $1.7 billion (IATA projection, up from $1.3B in 2025)
Current supply: only 15.84 million units issued (Guyana REDD+ programme)
Legal instrument: ICAO Annex 16, Volume IV; Assembly Resolution A41-22

whatIsIt: EU Aviation ETS under Directive 2003/87/EC (as amended by Directive 2023/958) covers CO₂ emissions from all flights departing from EU/EEA airports. Full scope covers intra-EEA flights; extra-EEA departures may be covered by CORSIA instead. From 2025, free allocation of allowances to airlines is being phased out. Enforced by EU Member State administering authorities.
whyMatters: All intra-European air freight already carries ETS carbon cost. At current EUA prices (~€68-81/tCO₂, Q1 2026), this adds measurable cost to every air cargo shipment within Europe. For freight forwarders: (1) intra-EU air cargo rates include embedded ETS costs, (2) phase-out of free allowances (from 2026) will increase the cost airlines need to recover via surcharges, (3) scope expansion to international flights would significantly increase transatlantic and EU-Asia air cargo rates, (4) interaction with CORSIA on international routes creates dual compliance complexity.
keyData: Coverage: all flights departing EU/EEA airports (intra-EEA mandatory)
Monitoring: EU ETS MRV for aviation — annual emissions report + surrender
Interaction: CORSIA covers international flights to avoid double-counting

note: IN FORCE from 1 January 2025. First compliance year (2% mandate) underway. SAF production capacity lags mandate requirements globally. ACTION NOW: Include SAF surcharge pass-through in all air freight contracts. Offer clients modal shift analysis (air → ocean) with quantified emissions and cost savings. Track SAF availability at key departure airports. Owner: Air Product + Sales.

whatIsIt: ReFuelEU Aviation Regulation (EU) 2023/2405 mandates increasing minimum shares of sustainable aviation fuel (SAF) in jet fuel supplied at EU airports. Sets binding blending mandates escalating from 2% (2025) to 70% (2050), with a sub-mandate for synthetic aviation fuels (e-fuels/PtL) from 2030. Enforced by EU Member State competent authorities at each airport. Published OJ L 2023/2405, 31.10.2023.
whyMatters: SAF costs 2-5x more than conventional jet fuel — at current prices, SAF is ~$2,286/mt vs $741/mt for conventional Jet-A1 (January 2026). Airlines pass through SAF costs as surcharges on air cargo. For freight forwarders: (1) air cargo rates from EU airports carry escalating SAF surcharges, (2) each mandate step-up (2% → 6% → 20% → 34% → 42% → 70%) will increase surcharges proportionally, (3) clients requesting 'sustainable air freight' need to understand the cost premium and that SAF availability varies by airport, (4) modal shift from air to ocean with quantified emissions savings is the primary cost mitigation strategy.

E-SAF price: up to 12x conventional jet fuel cost
Global SAF production (2024): ~0.5Mt — less than 0.2% of global jet fuel demand (~300Mt)

whatIsIt: UK Sustainable Aviation Fuel (SAF) Mandate under the Energy Act 2023 and SAF Mandate Order 2024 requires jet fuel suppliers at UK airports to blend SAF at increasing percentages: 2% in 2025, rising to 10% by 2030 and 22% by 2040. Includes a buy-out mechanism (£4.70/litre for main SAF obligation, £5.00/litre for Power-to-Liquid) and a HEFA fuel cap starting at 2% in 2025. Enforced by UK Department for Transport.

At 2% mandate, the direct cost impact is $36-58/tonne of fuel depending on SAF procurement price. For freight forwarders: (1) air freight quotes ex-UK include SAF surcharges, (2) non-compliance penalties for airlines are 2-13x the cost of compliance — airlines will comply and pass costs through, (3) UK mandate runs parallel to EU ReFuelEU — cross-channel air freight faces both, (4) cost will escalate significantly at the 10% (2030) step.
