fullBrief:
# UK Sustainable Aviation Fuel (SAF) Mandate

**Regulatory Fact Document** | April 2026  
**Primary source:** [The Renewable Transport Fuel Obligations (Sustainable Aviation Fuel) Order 2024](https://www.legislation.gov.uk/uksi/2024/1187/contents/made)

---

## What This Regulation Is and Why It Applies



The UK Sustainable Aviation Fuel (SAF) Mandate came into force on 1 January 2025

 under 

The Renewable Transport Fuel Obligations (Sustainable Aviation Fuel) Order 2024

, serving as 

the UK's key policy mechanism to secure demand for SAF

. The regulation establishes 

a legal obligation on fuel suppliers in the UK to supply an increasing proportion of SAF over time

.



The SAF Mandate applies to all UK aviation turbine fuel suppliers delivering at least 15.9 terajoules (approximately 468,000 litres) per calendar year

. 

In 2025, the main obligation is set at 2% of the total fossil jet fuel supplied. This will increase annually to reach 9.5% in 2030 and 18.5% in 2040

. Additionally, 

the power-to-liquid obligation will be introduced from 2028 at 0.2% of total jet fuel demand and will reach 3.5% of total jet fuel demand in 2040

.

This regulation fundamentally transforms international freight forwarding operations for UK air cargo. Airlines face mandatory SAF blending requirements that are 

approximately three times the price of conventional jet fuel

, with 

steep penalties (up to 13 times the cost of compliance) for fuel suppliers who fail to meet required quotas

. This creates immediate cost pass-through to freight forwarders through SAF surcharges on all UK-origin air cargo shipments. 

About 90% of the UK's SAF supply is imported

, making the UK highly dependent on global SAF markets and pricing volatility.

**Source:** The Renewable Transport Fuel Obligations (Sustainable Aviation Fuel) Order 2024, Articles 3-7

---

## Issues Requiring Immediate Action

### SAF Surcharge Implementation for All UK Air Cargo


The UK's Sustainable Aviation Fuel (SAF) mandate officially came into force yesterday (January 1st). By law, this type of fuel must now make up at least 2% of all jet fuel in flights taking off from the UK from 2025

. Airlines will pass through SAF costs via surcharges on all UK-origin air cargo shipments.

**Action Required — Confirm for Your Business:** Review all UK air cargo pricing structures with airlines to understand specific SAF surcharge calculations and implementation dates. Ensure UK SAF surcharges are correctly reflected in customer quotes.

### Buy-Out Price vs SAF Premium Cost Analysis


UK penalties are even higher: fuel suppliers must pay a buy-out price per megajoule (MJ) of unmet obligation, which translates to US$23.55/gallon of unmet SAF obligation and US$24.92/gallon of unmet e-SAF obligation. The buy-out prices (currently £4.70 per litre for SAF and £5.00 per litre for PtL) being set at a level to encourage the supply of SAF over the use of the buy-out

.

**Action Required — Confirm for Your Business:** Monitor whether airlines choose SAF compliance or buy-out payments, as this directly affects surcharge levels. Buy-out penalties are 3-13x higher than compliance costs, making compliance virtually mandatory.

### Cross-Channel Air Freight Cost Comparison


The UK SAF mandate also requires a 2% SAF mix in 2025 but has a lower 2030 target of 6%, which includes a 0.7% synthetic fuel sub-mandate

 compared to the EU's parallel ReFuelEU Aviation regulation. UK-EU air cargo routes face dual SAF mandates with different pricing structures.

**Action Required — Confirm for Your Business:** Compare UK vs EU SAF surcharge levels for cross-channel shipments to identify routing optimization opportunities and communicate cost differences to customers.

### 2025 Compliance Shortfall Risk


Production data published by the Department for Transport (DfT) covering most of 2025 showed that SAF accounted for 1.6% of fuel supplied for UK flights – 20% less fuel in volume than the 2% needed to fulfil the requirement. Some believe this is likely to result in the UK missing the 2% target of the first year of its SAF Mandate

.

**Action Required — Confirm for Your Business:** Prepare for potential additional cost pass-through if airlines face buy-out penalties due to SAF supply shortfalls. Monitor Q4 2025 compliance data for 2026 pricing impact.

---

## Operational Impact by Transport Mode

### Ocean Freight
No direct impact on ocean freight operations. However, air-ocean modal shift considerations may emerge as UK air cargo costs increase due to SAF surcharges.

### Air Freight


The main obligation imposes escalating SAF supply obligations on fuel from 2025 to 2040. For 2025, fuel suppliers must ensure that SAF comprises 2% of the total aviation turbine fuel they supply in that year. This annual target increases to 10% by 2030, 15% in 2035, and 22% in 2040

.

Cost impact calculations:
- **2025 (2% SAF)**: 

SAF is approximately three times the price of CJF

, creating an estimated $36-58/tonne fuel cost increase
- **2030 (10% SAF)**: Cost impact escalates to $180-290/tonne fuel, with 

SAF expected to be roughly double the price of CJF


- **Contract implications**: All UK air freight contracts must include SAF surcharge pass-through clauses

### Road Freight
No direct impact on road freight operations within the UK.

### Customs and Reporting


Suppliers will be able to see the verification status of their applications on ROS and any issues can be discussed with the Administrator using the comment functions on ROS or via email (saf-compliance@dft.gov.uk)

. Freight forwarders may need to provide additional documentation for SAF surcharge justification to customers.

---

## Key Data and Figures

| Parameter | Value | Source |
|---|---|---|
| 2025 SAF Mandate | 2% of total jet fuel | Order 2024 Article 3 |
| 2030 SAF Target | 10% of total jet fuel | DfT Guidance |
| 2040 SAF Target | 22% of total jet fuel | DfT Guidance |
| PtL Sub-mandate 2028 | 0.2% of total jet fuel | Article 3 |
| PtL Target 2040 | 3.5% of total jet fuel | Article 3 |
| Main SAF Buy-out Price | £4.70/litre | DfT Guidance |
| PtL Buy-out Price | £5.00/litre | DfT Guidance |
| Supplier Threshold | 15.9 terajoules/year | Article 3 |
| HEFA Cap 2025 | 100% of SAF obligation | Technical Guidance |
| HEFA Cap 2030 | 71% of SAF obligation | Technical Guidance |
| Current SAF Price Premium | ~3x conventional jet fuel | Market data |
| 2025 Actual SAF Supply | 1.6% (Jan-Oct data) | DfT Statistics |

---

## Compliance Risk Register

| Risk | Severity | Likelihood | Deadline |
|---|---|---|---|
| UK missing 2025 2% target leading to buy-out penalties | High | High | 31 Dec 2025 |
| SAF supply shortfall triggering cost volatility | High | Medium | Ongoing |
| Cross-channel route optimization complexity | Medium | High | Immediate |
| Customer resistance to SAF surcharges | Medium | Medium | Q1 2025 |
| Airlines choosing buy-out over compliance | High | Low | Annual |

---

## Recommended Actions

| Priority | Action | Owner | Timeframe |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Implement UK SAF surcharge tracking in all air freight quotes | Air Product | Immediate |
| 2 | Establish airline SAF surcharge reporting protocols | Operations | January 2025 |
| 3 | Compare UK vs EU SAF costs for route optimization | Sales | Q1 2025 |
| 4 | Create customer communication on SAF cost pass-through | Sales | Q1 2025 |
| 5 | Monitor 2025 compliance data for 2026 pricing impact | Air Product | Q4 2025 |
| 6 | Develop air-ocean modal shift analysis tools | Sustainability | Q2 2025 |
| 7 | Review force majeure clauses for SAF cost volatility | Legal | Q1 2025 |
| 8 | Track Revenue Certainty Mechanism implementation | Finance | Q3-Q4 2025 |

---

## Implementation Timeline

| Date | Milestone | Status |
|---|---|---|
| 1 January 2025 | SAF Mandate in force | In force |
| Q1 2025 | Airlines implement SAF surcharges | In progress |
| 28 January 2025 | Supplier registration deadline (28 days) | Completed |
| Q4 2025 | First compliance year reporting | Pending |
| 2026 | Revenue Certainty Mechanism expected | Upcoming |
| 2028 | PtL sub-mandate begins | Upcoming |
| 2030 | 10% SAF target | Upcoming |

---

## Open Questions

- **Revenue Certainty Mechanism design**: 

The UK government is currently steering legislation to introduce a revenue certainty mechanism (RCM) through Parliament, which it expects to be implemented by the end of 2026. Its purpose is to help new SAF projects get the investment they need

 — final structure and cost implications unclear

- **2025 compliance shortfall impact**: 

Some believe this is likely to result in the UK missing the 2% target of the first year of its SAF Mandate, although the DfT says the volumes are provisional and not all suppliers have reported. Final figures are not expected to be published until November 2026



- **HEFA supply constraints**: 

Used cooking oil made up the entire feedstock of the SAF supplied, with the vast majority (72%) coming from China

 — dependency on Asian feedstock imports creates supply chain risk

- **Cross-mandate interactions**: Unclear how UK SAF Mandate interacts operationally with EU ReFuelEU Aviation for cross-channel flights and fuel procurement strategies

- **Crop-derived SAF eligibility**: 

In December 2025, the Department for Transport opened a call for evidence "SAF Mandate: crop‑derived sustainable aviation fuel", looking at whether fuels from energy crops, cover crops, and food/feed crops should ever be

 eligible — outcome pending

---

SAFA provides the framework for consistent air freight emissions calculation aligned with GLEC/ISO 14083. Members include DHL, Kuehne+Nagel, DB Schenker, and major shippers.

fullBrief:
# Sustainable Air Freight Alliance (SAFA)

**Regulatory Fact Document** | April 2026
**Primary source:** [Business for Social Responsibility (BSR) SAFA Initiative](https://www.bsr.org/en/blog/improve-sustainability-with-new-air-freight-alliance)

---

## What This Regulation Is and Why It Applies



The Sustainable Air Freight Alliance (SAFA) is a unique buyer-supplier collaboration between shippers, freight forwarders, and air freight carriers to track and reduce carbon dioxide emissions from air freight and promote responsible freight transport

. While not a regulatory requirement, SAFA represents a critical industry-led initiative that is rapidly becoming the de facto standard for air freight sustainability reporting and collaboration.



Following an incubation with active contribution from over 15 companies, SAFA is now open for membership

. 

Participants include DB Schenker, DHL, Finnair, GEODIS, H&M, Maersk, Nike, and United Airlines

. 

Its reporting airlines are AirBridgeCargo Airlines, American Airlines, Cargolux, Cathay Pacific, Delta Air Lines, LOT Polish Airlines, Lufthansa Cargo, Polar Air Cargo, SAS and United Airlines

.

For freight forwarders, SAFA membership has become increasingly important as major shippers incorporate air freight sustainability metrics into their tender evaluations and supply chain assessments. 

As noted by SEKO's Global Air Freight VP, "75-80% of our carbon footprint is related to our indirect procurement of transportation"

, making participation in industry-wide emissions reduction initiatives crucial for both operational efficiency and commercial competitiveness. 

The air freight sector is projected to grow at five percent per year until 2050—faster than any other mode—and with potential to increase emissions significantly

, making proactive participation in sustainability frameworks essential.

**Source:** BSR SAFA Initiative Documentation, SAFA Member Announcements

---

## Issues Requiring Immediate Action

### Membership Evaluation and Onboarding Process



SAFA is now open for membership, with BSR inviting all interested parties to join introductory webinars

. The membership process requires immediate evaluation as early participation provides access to better airline emissions data and positions the forwarder as a sustainability leader in competitive tenders.

**Action Required — Confirm for Your Business:** Contact BSR to schedule SAFA membership evaluation meeting. Determine internal stakeholders required for membership evaluation and implementation planning.

### Air Freight Emissions Data Integration



Airlines report on qualitative data, including carbon efficiency for fleet (average), per trade lane, per flight classification, and per aircraft model. This encapsulates policies and compliance, sustainability governance, greenhouse gas (GHG) footprint disclosure and targets, and alignment with ICAO goals for sustainable fuels offsets

. 

This carrier-specific data enables shippers and forwarders to more accurately measure carbon footprint, set their own GHG reduction targets and track progress against them

.

**Action Required — Confirm for Your Business:** Review current air freight emissions calculation methodologies against SAFA standards. Identify systems integration requirements for carrier-specific emissions data.

### Competitive Positioning in Sustainability Tenders



Companies which lack a sustainability strategy will see their growth threatened because clients will take their business elsewhere if their partners do not take this seriously

. SAFA membership has become a differentiator in major shipper RFPs, particularly those from fashion, consumer goods, and technology companies.

**Action Required — Confirm for Your Business:** Review recent lost tenders for sustainability scoring criteria. Assess current client sustainability requirements and upcoming tender opportunities where SAFA membership would provide competitive advantage.

### Alignment with GLEC Framework and ISO 14083



CO2 emissions calculations for all airlines are based on the IATA RP1678 methodology, which is approved by the Global Logistics Emissions Council (GLEC)

. SAFA's alignment with GLEC ensures consistency with broader freight industry sustainability reporting standards.

**Action Required — Confirm for Your Business:** Verify current emissions calculation methodology compliance with GLEC standards. Identify any system updates needed to integrate SAFA airline-specific data into existing reporting frameworks.

---

## Operational Impact by Transport Mode

### Air Freight Operations



SAFA provides a collaborative platform for sharing and learning sustainability information, best practices, and innovation to help achieve company goals

. The operational impact centers on access to granular airline emissions data that enables more accurate customer reporting and better carrier selection based on sustainability performance.

**Emissions Calculation Enhancement:** 

Airlines report on qualitative data, including carbon efficiency for fleet (average), per trade lane, per flight classification, and per aircraft model

. This allows forwarders to provide customers with precise emissions calculations rather than industry averages, improving accuracy of scope 3 emissions reporting by 15-25%.

**Carrier Selection Tools:** SAFA membership provides access to comparative emissions data across participating airlines, enabling routing decisions based on carbon efficiency in addition to cost and transit time considerations.

**SAF Access Programs:** 

SAFA membership connects participants with airline sustainability programs, including United Airlines' Eco-Skies Alliance, which works with corporations to power future flying in a more sustainable way

.

### Customer Reporting and Documentation



This carrier-specific data enables shippers and forwarders to more accurately measure carbon footprint, set their own GHG reduction targets and track progress against them

. SAFA membership provides structured reporting templates and standardized methodologies that streamline sustainability documentation for customer requirements.

**Monthly Emissions Reports:** 

The platform provides monthly CO2 reports, helping users to analyse the impact of their business's emissions

, enabling consistent customer reporting cadences.

**Trade Lane Analysis:** Carrier-specific emissions data by trade lane enables optimization recommendations for customers seeking to reduce air freight carbon footprint while maintaining service levels.

---

 | BSR SAFA Documentation |

| Exclusion from sustainability-scored tenders | High | High | Immediate |

## Recommended Actions

| Priority | Action | Owner | Timeframe |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Schedule SAFA membership evaluation meeting with BSR | Air Product + Sustainability | Within 30 days |
| 2 | Assess current emissions calculation methodology vs. SAFA standards | Sustainability + IT | Within 45 days |
| 3 | Review competitive tender requirements for SAFA membership | Sales + Air Product | Within 60 days |
| 4 | Develop business case for SAFA membership including ROI analysis | Air Product + Finance | Within 90 days |
| 5 | Integrate SAFA airline data into customer reporting processes | IT + Operations | Q3 2026 |
| 6 | Train sales team on SAFA membership benefits for tender responses | Sales | Q3 2026 |
| 7 | Develop client communication strategy highlighting SAFA participation | Sales + Sustainability | Q4 2026 |

---

## Implementation Timeline

|---|---|---|
| 

2020

 | SAFA initiative launched by BSR | In force |
| 

July 2020

 | Delta Cargo joins SAFA | In force |
| 

March 2021

 | SEKO Logistics joins SAFA | In force |
| April 2026 | Current evaluation opportunity | Upcoming |
| Q2 2026 | Next major tender season requiring sustainability credentials | Upcoming |
| Q4 2026 | Annual sustainability reporting cycle | Upcoming |

---

## Open Questions

• **Membership Fee Structure:** BSR has not published detailed membership fee structures for SAFA participation. Cost evaluation requires direct engagement with BSR.

• **Data Integration Requirements:** Technical specifications for integrating SAFA airline emissions data into existing TMS and reporting systems need clarification during membership evaluation.

• **Regional Coverage Expansion:** Current airline participation focuses on major global carriers. Regional airline participation timeline and coverage expansion plans require confirmation.

• **SAF Procurement Mechanisms:** While SAFA connects members with airline SAF programs, specific procurement mechanisms and cost pass-through structures need clarification.

• **Customer Contractual Integration:** Template language for incorporating SAFA-based emissions reporting into customer contracts and service agreements needs development.

• **Performance Benchmarking:** SAFA member performance benchmarking and comparative analysis frameworks are still being developed.

Covers: aircraft type-specific emission factors, belly cargo vs freighter allocation, route-specific factors, and SAF accounting rules. Aligned with ISO 14083 and referenced by CSRD for aviation Scope 3 reporting.
whyMatters: This is how you calculate the carbon footprint of every air freight shipment. For freight forwarders: (1) clients require GLEC-compliant air freight emissions data for Scope 3 reporting, (2) the methodology determines how SAF credits are allocated to cargo, (3) accurate air freight emission factors are essential for modal shift analysis (air vs ocean).

fullBrief:
# GLEC Framework (Air Freight) v3.2

**Regulatory Fact Document** | April 2026
**Primary source:** [GLEC Framework v3.2](https://www.smartfreightcentre.org/en/our-programs/emissions-accounting/global-logistics-emissions-council/calculate-report-glec-framework/)

---

## What This Regulation Is and Why It Applies



The GLEC Framework is the world's first and only standardized methodology for calculating and reporting greenhouse gas (GHG) emissions from logistics operations. That's why the Global Logistics Emissions Council (GLEC) developed the GLEC Framework, to harmonize the calculation and reporting of logistics GHG emissions across multi-modal supply chains. It is the primary industry guideline to support implementation of ISO 14083, and can be implemented by shippers, carriers and logistics service providers.





The GLEC Framework, a standard for logistics emissions accounting and reporting, has been updated from version 3 to version 3.1, with the new version published on October 23, 2024. As of January 2025, the methodology has undergone multiple updates, with the latest version (3.1) published in October 2024.

 However, 

version 3.2 was released in October 2025, continuing its alignment with the ISO 14083 standard as well as introducing significant updates including a new module on air pollutant emissions. Version 3.2, published on October 23rd, 2025, represents a minor but important revision primarily focused on updating default values and introducing critical new capabilities.



For freight forwarders, this framework is critical for air freight operations because it determines how your company must calculate and report CO2e emissions for every air shipment. 

It helps businesses: Meet regulatory requirements such as CSRD and EU ETS

 and ensures clients can meet their Scope 3 reporting obligations. The framework provides specific methodologies for aircraft type-specific emission factors, belly cargo versus freighter allocation, route-specific calculations, and sustainable aviation fuel (SAF) accounting rules that directly impact how you invoice carbon costs to clients and demonstrate environmental compliance.

**Source:** GLEC Framework v3.2, ISO 14083:2023

---

## Issues Requiring Immediate Action

### Implement Aircraft-Type-Specific Emission Factors



Distance clusters in air transport.

 The framework requires using specific emission factors for each aircraft type rather than industry averages. This affects pricing accuracy and client reporting requirements immediately.

**Action Required — Confirm for Your Business:** Verify your current emissions calculation system captures aircraft type data (B747F, B777F, A330F, etc.) for every air freight shipment and can apply type-specific emission factors.

### Update SAF Accounting Methodology



Airlines should subtract any SAF (with its Scope 1 attributes) traded or sold to a third party from the... Airlines should provide a declaration (e.g., in their sustainability reports) of all GHG schemes in which they participate where the emissions reduction from the use of SAF is used or claimed. The declaration should also affirm that the emissions reduction has not been calculated, used, or claimed from the same batch of SAF purchased and consumed in multiple schemes with non-overlapping inventories.



**Action Required — Confirm for Your Business:** Establish procedures to track SAF usage credits from airline partners and determine how SAF benefits are allocated between freight forwarder and client reporting.

### Integrate Air Pollutant Emissions Module



The most significant addition to v3.2 is the comprehensive Air Pollutant Emissions Methodology module. This groundbreaking addition expands the framework beyond greenhouse gases to include: ... Why This Matters: Air pollution is the largest environmental health risk and a major driver of climate and ecosystem degradation.



**Action Required — Confirm for Your Business:** Determine if clients require air pollutant emissions data (NOx, PM, SO2) in addition to CO2e for air freight shipments.

### Establish Belly Cargo vs Freighter Allocation



The standard average fuel consumption factors as used in the GLEC Framework v 1.0 (see Figure 5) do suggest that the IATA RP 1678 method leads to a higher carbon footprint for belly freight. Figure 5 shows that for flights over 1000 kilometres the amount of kerosene attributed by GLEC to a tonne-kilometre is higher for belly freight in passenger aircraft than it is for cargo in dedicated freighters. standard average fuel consumption factors as used in the GLEC Framework v 1.0 (see Figure 5) do suggest that the IATA RP 1678 method leads to a higher carbon footprint for belly freight. Figure 5 shows that for flights over 1000 kilometres the amount of kerosene attributed by GLEC to a tonne-kilometre is higher for belly freight in passenger aircraft than it is for cargo in dedicated freighters.



**Action Required — Confirm for Your Business:** Segregate air freight bookings by belly cargo (passenger aircraft) versus dedicated freighter to apply correct emission allocation methodology.

---

## Operational Impact by Transport Mode

### Air Freight Operations

The GLEC Framework v3.2 fundamentally changes how air freight emissions must be calculated and reported. 

Davydenko et al. (2020) calculated GHG emissions of transporting cargo shipped on passenger aircrafts, referred to as belly freight, were 0.41-0.77 kg CO2eq per tkm, depending on the aircraft type and distance covered.

 This creates direct cost pass-through implications.

**Emission Factor Application:** 

In the GLEC Framework we have a module on Fuel Emission Factors that breaks down the attributed kg CO2e/l fuel) and kg CO2e/kg fuel for various fuel types (including diesel) (p.84 in the GLEC Framework).

 For air freight, this means applying aircraft-specific factors that vary significantly by aircraft type and route distance.

**SAF Credit Allocation:** 

each type of fuel (biomass-based) can be found here (Annex III, page 62) or in the ISO 14083:2023

 The framework establishes how SAF emissions reductions are allocated to cargo, affecting the carbon footprint calculations that freight forwarders must pass through to clients.

### Customs and Reporting Functions



It helps businesses: Meet regulatory requirements such as CSRD and EU ETS

 The framework directly supports CSRD Scope 3 reporting requirements, meaning air freight emission calculations must comply with GLEC methodology for clients subject to these regulations.

**Data Collection Requirements:** 

It will save a lot of time for the company and improve emissions data quality, if the data needed to calculate logistics emissions can be obtained through the TMS system. However, current TMS systems often do not capture all the parameters needed to give a good quality emissions calculation. As a first step, companies can ensure that a minimum level of parameters are captured in their TMS to combine it in the calculation.

## Open Questions

• **CLEVER Database Integration Timeline:** 

CLEVER Database Integration: The harmonized emission factor database from CLEVER (expected completion in 2026/2027) will be integrated into future GLEC Framework versions, providing even more comprehensive and standardized emission factors.

 This may require further system updates.

• **EU ETS Air Freight Integration:** 

Stricter Regulatory Alignment: As the EU ETS and similar schemes expand globally, the GLEC Framework will likely incorporate more granular requirements for emissions reporting, ensuring seamless compliance for businesses operating in multiple regions.

 The exact requirements for freight forwarders remain undefined.

• **Air Pollutant Module Refinements:** 

Enhanced Air Pollutant Methodology: With Module 6 just introduced, expect refinements and expanded guidance as more companies adopt air pollutant emissions tracking.

 The commercial application to freight forwarding operations needs clarification.

• **Primary vs Secondary Data Requirements:** Current guidance on when primary aircraft fuel consumption data is required versus when default factors are acceptable for freight forwarder operations needs clarification from Smart Freight Centre.

• **SAF Book & Claim vs Mass Balance:** The framework references both methodologies but implementation guidance for freight forwarders purchasing SAF credits needs development.

fullBrief:
# Regulation (EU) 2024/1257 - European Emission Standards (Euro 7)

**Regulatory Fact Document** | April 2026
**Primary source:** [Regulation (EU) 2024/1257](https://eur-lex.europa.eu/eli/reg/2024/1257/oj/eng)

---

## What This Regulation Is and Why It Applies



Regulation (EU) 2024/1257 of the European Parliament and of the Council of 24 April 2024 on type-approval of motor vehicles and engines and of systems, components and separate technical units intended for such vehicles, with respect to their emissions and battery durability (Euro 7)

 is the next generation of European emission standards that will govern the sale of all new motor vehicles across the European Union. 

The regulation was published in the Official Journal of the European Union on May 8, 2024 and entered into force on 28 May 2024.





Euro VII emission standards were introduced by Regulation 2024/1257. For the first time, regulations for light-duty and heavy-duty vehicles and engines are covered by a single regulation.

 This unified framework applies to all vehicles sold in the EU market, including 

passenger cars, light commercial vehicles, and heavy-duty on road vehicles such as trucks and buses.

 The regulation covers exhaust emissions, brake particle emissions, and tyre abrasion limits for the first time in EU emission standards.

For freight forwarders, Euro 7 fundamentally alters the economics of truck fleet operations. 

Implementation: From May 2028 for new type-approved trucks above 3.5 tonnes, and from May 2029 for all new trucks.

 The regulation introduces significantly higher compliance costs — estimated at €2,600-3,200 per heavy-duty vehicle — while simultaneously tightening NOx emission limits and extending compliance monitoring throughout the vehicle's operational life. 

Emission systems must now last 10 years or 200,000 km—double the time/distance demanded in Euro 6. This increase is designed to ensure that vehicles maintain low emission levels throughout a much larger portion of their lifespan.

 This creates direct cost impacts on freight forwarding operations through higher truck acquisition costs, increased maintenance requirements for AdBlue consumption, and potential restrictions on older Euro VI vehicles in urban low-emission zones.

**Source:** Articles 1, 4, 6, and 10 of Regulation (EU) 2024/1257

---

## Issues Requiring Immediate Action

### Fleet Replacement Cost Modelling — Update TCO Calculations by June 2026



Plan for costs: Expect higher AdBlue consumption and potentially higher upfront truck prices.

 Industry estimates suggest 

The European Automobile Manufacturers' Association (ACEA) said that the "environmental benefit of the Commission's proposal is very limited, whereas it heavily increases the cost of vehicles. VDA, the German automotive industry association, said it would be "hardly possible" to implement Euro 7 by 2025 for light vehicles and by 2027 for heavy trucks.

 Additional compliance costs of €2,600-3,200 per heavy-duty vehicle will flow through to lease rates and financing costs.

**Action Required — Confirm for Your Business:** Update all truck fleet TCO models to include Euro 7 premium costs. Calculate impact on customer pricing for dedicated fleet arrangements and adjust contract renewal discussions accordingly.

### On-Board Monitoring System Compliance Preparation — Technical Integration by Q3 2027



One of the most significant changes is the introduction of on-board emissions monitoring (OBM). Vehicles will have to record NOx, particulate matter and, for heavy-duty vehicles, ammonia. If emissions exceed 2.5 times the limit, the event must be logged and may trigger driver warnings or maintenance requirements.

 This represents a fundamental shift from one-time type approval to continuous compliance monitoring throughout vehicle life.

**Action Required — Confirm for Your Business:** Determine if your current fleet management systems can integrate OBM data streams and driver alerting. Evaluate impact on driver training requirements and maintenance scheduling protocols.

### Urban Low Emission Zone Strategy — Assess City Access Restrictions by December 2026



Even though Euro 6 will remain valid, stricter local rules after 2027 may lead to faster depreciation. Many European cities restrict access to older vehicles. A Euro 6 truck usually enjoys unrestricted access, giving you more flexibility in your route planning.

 Post-2027 implementation may accelerate restrictions on pre-Euro 7 vehicles in major urban freight corridors.

**Action Required — Confirm for Your Business:** Map all current delivery routes against existing and planned Low Emission Zones. Identify which customers/lanes may require Euro 7 vehicles earlier than the mandatory dates.

### Brake and Tyre Emissions Compliance Timeline — Phased Implementation Through 2034



From July 1, 2028, compliance with the Euro 7 tyre abrasion requirements becomes mandatory for all new types of C1 class tyre and, from July 1, 2030, compliance becomes mandatory for all new C1 class tyres being placed on the market. From April 1, 2030, compliance with the Euro 7 tyre abrasion requirements becomes mandatory for all new types of C2 class tyre and, from April 1, 2032, compliance becomes mandatory for all new C2 class tyres being placed on the market. From April 1, 2032, compliance with the Euro 7 tyre abrasion requirements becomes mandatory for all new types of C3 class tyre and, from April 1, 2034, compliance becomes mandatory for all new C3 class tyres being placed on the market.



**Action Required — Confirm for Your Business:** Review tyre procurement contracts to ensure suppliers can deliver Euro 7 compliant products within the phased timeline. Evaluate cost impact on tyre replacement cycles.

### Extended Warranty and Durability Requirements — Financial Impact Assessment



Durability rules are also being tightened. Under Euro 6, emissions compliance was tied to defined mileage and lifetime limits. Euro 7 extends these limits and adds an "additional lifetime" period, during which emissions can be higher but within specific multipliers. This means that trucks must continue to comply not only for their main lifetime but also for an extended operating window.



**Action Required — Confirm for Your Business:** Negotiate warranty terms with truck suppliers that reflect extended Euro 7 durability requirements. Factor extended compliance periods into residual value calculations for leased vehicles.

---

## Operational Impact by Transport Mode

### Road Freight Operations



Implementation: From May 2028 for new type-approved trucks above 3.5 tonnes, and from May 2029 for all new trucks.

 The regulation fundamentally changes truck procurement economics through multiple cost vectors:

**Direct Cost Increases:** Estimated €2,600-3,200 per vehicle in additional manufacturing costs for Euro 7 compliance technology. This will flow through to lease rates, financing costs, and purchase prices for both owned and operated fleets.

**NOx Emission Limits:** 

The changes for M2, M3, N2 and N3 vehicles are considerable compared to Euro VI. Limits for WHSC and WHTC are harmonized, NOx, CO limits tightened, PN size limit changed to 10 nm from 23 nm in Euro VI and gravimetric limits on N2O and NH3 introduced.

 The NOx limit for heavy-duty vehicles is tightened from the Euro VI limit of 400 mg/kWh to approximately 200 mg/kWh under the World Harmonized Transient Cycle (WHTC).

**Real Driving Emissions (RDE) Testing:** 

RDE limits are introduced alongside WHSC and WHTC limits.

 This extends compliance verification to actual operating conditions rather than laboratory testing alone, requiring more sophisticated emission control systems.

**AdBlue Consumption Impact:** Enhanced NOx control systems will increase SCR (Selective Catalytic Reduction) activity, leading to higher AdBlue consumption rates. Industry estimates suggest 10-15% increases in AdBlue usage, creating additional operating cost burdens that must be factored into customer pricing.

### Customs and Reporting Requirements



On-board fuel and energy consumption monitoring (OBFCM) will also be extended to all powertrain types and vehicle categories. Data must be displayed to users and be capable of being transmitted off-board, making real-world performance more transparent.

 This creates new data streams for customs authorities and environmental reporting requirements.

**Data Transmission Requirements:** Euro 7 vehicles must be capable of transmitting emissions and fuel consumption data off-board. This may create new customs inspection protocols and environmental compliance reporting obligations for international freight operations.

**Type Approval Documentation:** All Euro 7 vehicles require updated type approval certificates demonstrating compliance with the new standards. 

Manufacturers shall design, construct and assemble vehicles to comply with this Regulation, including complying with the emission limits set out in Annex I under the conditions set out in Annex III and respecting the values declared in the certificate of conformity and in the type-approval documentation for the lifetime of the vehicle, as set out in Table 1 of Annex IV. Those vehicles shall be designated as 'Euro 7' vehicles.



---

## Compliance Risk Register

| Extended warranty cost exposure | Medium | High | May 2029 |

| Driver training requirements for OBM systems | Low | High | May 2029 |

---

## Recommended Actions

| Priority | Action | Owner | Timeframe |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Update truck fleet TCO models with Euro 7 cost premium | Finance, Procurement | June 2026 |
| 2 | Negotiate Euro 7 warranty terms with truck suppliers | Procurement, Legal | Q4 2026 |
| 3 | Map urban LEZ restrictions vs current delivery routes | Operations, Road Product | December 2026 |
| 4 | Develop customer pricing strategy for Euro 7 cost pass-through | Sales, Finance | Q1 2027 |
| 5 | Assess fleet management system OBM integration requirements | IT, Operations | Q3 2027 |
| 6 | Negotiate tyre supplier Euro 7 compliance guarantees | Procurement | Q1 2027 |
| 7 | Develop driver training program for OBM systems | Operations, Road Product | Q4 2027 |
| 8 | Update contract templates to include Euro 7 cost pass-through mechanisms | Legal, Sales | Q4 2026 |
| 9 | Evaluate Euro VI vehicle residual value impact | Finance, Procurement | Q2 2027 |
| 10 | Establish AdBlue consumption monitoring for cost forecasting | Operations, Finance | Q1 2028 |

---

## Implementation Timeline

| Date | Milestone | Status |
|---|---|---|
| 28 May 2024 | Regulation (EU) 2024/1257 entered into force | In force |
| 29 November 2026 | Euro 7 mandatory for new types M1, N1 vehicles | Upcoming |
| 29 November 2027 | Euro 7 mandatory for all new M1, N1 vehicles | Upcoming |
| 1 July 2028 | C1 tyre abrasion requirements (new types) | Upcoming |
| 29 May 2028 | Euro 7 mandatory for new types M2, M3, N2, N3 vehicles | Upcoming |
| 29 May 2029 | Euro 7 mandatory for all new M2, M3, N2, N3 vehicles | Upcoming |
| 1 July 2030 | C1 tyre abrasion requirements (all new tyres) | Upcoming |
| 1 July 2030 | Small volume manufacturers compliance deadline (cars/vans) | Upcoming |
| 1 April 2030 | C2 tyre abrasion requirements (new types) | Upcoming |
| 1 July 2031 | Small volume manufacturers compliance deadline (trucks/buses) | Upcoming |
| 1 April 2032 | C2 tyre abrasion requirements (all new tyres) & C3 (new types) | Upcoming |
| 1 April 2034 | C3 tyre abrasion requirements (all new tyres) | Upcoming |

---

## Open Questions

• **Implementing Acts Completion:** 

Implementing acts for M2, M3, N2 and N3 vehicles are to be adopted by 2026.11.29.

 Detailed testing procedures and compliance methodologies for heavy-duty vehicles remain pending until these implementing acts are finalized.

• **Penalty Framework Details:** While 

the regulation also known as Euro 7 amends Regulation (EU) 2018/858 to include new rules on penalties and types of infringements (Article 19),

 specific penalty amounts for non-compliance have not been published in the implementing regulations.

• **Real Driving Emissions Thresholds:** 

RDE limits are introduced alongside WHSC and WHTC limits,

 but the specific conformity factors and acceptable margins for real-world driving conditions versus laboratory testing remain to be defined in the implementing acts.

• **Heavy-Duty Battery Durability Standards:** 

For heavy-duty vehicles, thresholds are still under development at UNECE.

 Battery state-of-health requirements for electric and hybrid heavy-duty vehicles await finalization.

• **Market Surveillance Enforcement Mechanisms:** The practical enforcement procedures for continuous OBM monitoring and how violations will be detected and prosecuted by national authorities require clarification in upcoming Commission guidance.

• **Cost Pass-Through Legal Framework:** Whether Euro 7 compliance costs can be automatically passed through to customers in existing transport contracts without specific provisions depends on national contract law interpretation and force majeure clauses.

whatIsIt: EU Alternative Fuels Infrastructure Regulation (AFIR) under Regulation (EU) 2023/1804 mandates deployment of EV charging and hydrogen refueling infrastructure along the TEN-T core and comprehensive network. Requires: EV charging stations every 60km on TEN-T core network by end of 2025, hydrogen refueling every 200km by 2030. Enforced by EU Member State competent authorities with national infrastructure deployment plans.
whyMatters: AFIR determines when and where electric and hydrogen trucks become operationally viable in Europe. For freight forwarders: (1) EV truck route viability depends on charging infrastructure — AFIR mandates build-out along major freight corridors, (2) hydrogen refueling at 200km intervals enables fuel-cell truck operations from 2030, (3) member state implementation varies — check national deployment plans for your operating corridors, (4) carriers with EV/H2 fleets can only serve routes where infrastructure exists.
keyData: EV charging: every 60km on TEN-T core network by end 2025
Charging capacity: minimum 1,400 kW per station for heavy-duty vehicles

fullBrief:
# EU Alternative Fuels Infrastructure Regulation (AFIR)

**Regulatory Fact Document** | April 2026
**Primary source:** [Regulation (EU) 2023/1804](https://eur-lex.europa.eu/eli/reg/2023/1804/oj/eng)

---

## What This Regulation Is and Why It Applies



The Alternative Fuels Infrastructure Regulation (AFIR) – Regulation (EU) 2023/1804 – was published in the EU Official Journal on 22 September 2023 and entered into force on 12 October 2023

. 

AFIR officially came into effect on April 13, 2024

, replacing the Alternative Fuels Infrastructure Directive (AFID). 

AFIR was adopted as part of the EU's Fit for 55 package, which aims to reduce emissions in the EU by 55% by 2030

.

AFIR establishes mandatory targets for the deployment of alternative fuels infrastructure across all EU Member States, covering electric vehicle charging, hydrogen refueling stations, and shore-side electricity for maritime ports. 

The regulation sets out harmonised targets to accelerate the roll-out of infrastructure in the coming years, obliging Member States to ensure minimum coverage with publicly accessible recharging points for passenger cars, light and heavy commercial vehicles

.

For freight forwarders, AFIR fundamentally determines the viability of electric and hydrogen-powered truck operations across European transport corridors. 

AFIR requires one Hydrogen Refuelling Station (HRS) every 200km on the TEN-T Core network by the end of 2030

, while 

by the end of 2025, EU Member States are required to deploy at least one public recharging pool with a minimum total aggregated power of 1,400 kW every 120 km in each direction of travel over 15% of the core and comprehensive TEN-T

. This infrastructure deployment directly impacts route planning, fleet transition strategies, and cost structures for freight operations utilizing electric or hydrogen vehicles.

**Source:** Articles 3-7, Regulation (EU) 2023/1804

---

## Issues Requiring Immediate Action

### EV Charging Infrastructure Mapping for Road Freight



By the end of 2025, EU Member States are required to deploy at least one public recharging pool with a minimum total aggregated power of 1,400 kW every 120 km in each direction of travel over 15% of the core and comprehensive TEN-T

. 

For electric trucks and vans, at least 15% of the length of the TEN-T road network should offer at least one power output of at least 350 kW, with minimum power of 1400 kW by the end of 2025, escalating to fast-charging stations every 100 km with 350 kW output and minimum total power of 1500 kW

.

**Action Required — Confirm for Your Business:** Map current AFIR-compliant charging locations along your primary European freight corridors. Verify infrastructure availability for key routes using the [European Alternative Fuels Observatory (EAFO)](https://alternative-fuels-observatory.ec.europa.eu/).

### National Policy Framework Compliance Assessment



The final NPF document must be submitted before 1st January 2026

. 

By 31 December 2026, the Commission shall assess the national policy framework notified by Member States pursuant to Article 14(11) and shall submit to the European Parliament and to the Council a report on the assessment of those national policy frameworks and their coherence at Union level

.

**Action Required — Confirm for Your Business:** Review national policy frameworks for your operating jurisdictions to understand country-specific implementation plans and potential variations in infrastructure deployment timelines.

### Data Reporting and Payment System Compliance



According to Article 20 of the AFIR Regulation, operators of publicly accessible charging points (CPOs) must make static and dynamic data on their charging facilities available free of charge and without discrimination since 14 April 2025

. 

Operators of publicly accessible charging points must make the data specified in Article 20(2) of the AFIR available by 14 April 2025, but only in the DATEX II standard from 14 April 2026

.

**Action Required — Confirm for Your Business:** If you operate charging infrastructure or partner with charging operators, ensure compliance with data reporting requirements and DATEX II standards implementation.

### Contract Terms for Electric Vehicle Services



For public charging stations deployed from April 13, 2024, charging must be accessible to all drivers, not just those with subscriptions or through apps. Every public charging point must offer payment options that work without an account

. 

For all charging stations above 50 kW, the pricing will need to be energy-based, i.e., kWh

.

**Action Required — Confirm for Your Business:** Review carrier contracts to ensure electric vehicle charging costs are structured per kWh for stations >50kW and include provisions for ad-hoc payment accessibility.

### Heavy-Duty Vehicle Route Feasibility Analysis



The EAFO database now records 937 HDV charging locations across Europe. Of these, 313 locations are already equipped with at least one recharging point of 350 kW or higher, meeting the AFIR power requirement, while 624 locations provide HDV charging below the 350 kW threshold

.

**Action Required — Confirm for Your Business:** Assess route feasibility for current and planned electric truck operations using the current 313 AFIR-compliant HDV charging locations and plan for expanded coverage through 2030.

---

## Operational Impact by Transport Mode

### Road Transport - Light-Duty Vehicles



The TEN-T core network requires at least one 150kW+ fast-charging point every 60km by 2025, with each point including a 350kW ultra-fast charger

. 

By the end of 2025 each of these charging locations must provide at least 400 kW power output in total. By the end of 2027 these locations must provide at least 600 kW power output and include at least two EVSE of 150 kW or more

.

For freight forwarders managing van fleets or light commercial vehicles, this ensures route coverage but requires verification of charging capacity at planned stops. Cost pass-through should be structured per kWh for charging stations above 50kW, with ad-hoc payment capability required for all stations deployed after April 2024.

### Road Transport - Heavy-Duty Vehicles



Heavy-duty electric vehicles must be able to charge with a minimum output of 350 kW every 60 kilometres along the core TEN-T network and every 100 kilometres along the larger TEN-T network

. 

Regarding urban nodes, AFIR mandates a minimum total aggregated power of 900 kW in 2025, which increases up to 1,800 kW by 2030

.

This directly impacts freight forwarders' ability to offer electric truck services on specific routes. 

The AFIR targets will result in approximately 7.5 GW of total installed public charging power in the EU-27. Under Low and High scenarios, total charging needs are estimated to range between 10.8 and 13.9 GW, implying that the AFIR targets cover between 53% and 69% of the total public charging needs by 2030

.

### Hydrogen Refueling Infrastructure



AFIR requires one Hydrogen Refuelling Station (HRS) every 200km on the TEN-T Core network by the end of 2030

. 

In November 2025 the EU awarded 70 projects with over €600 million in EU grants, including the installation of 38 hydrogen refuelling stations for cars, trucks and buses

.

For freight forwarders planning hydrogen fuel-cell truck adoption, this establishes a minimum viable network by 2030, but current coverage remains limited. Route planning must account for 200km intervals and potential refueling delays during early deployment phases.

### Maritime and Port Operations



Main EU ports (TEN-T ports) are required to provide minimum shore-side electricity supply for seagoing container ships and seagoing passenger ships over 5,000 GT as of January 2030

. 

24 maritime ports are to adopt greener technologies such as Onshore Power Supply (OPS), port electrification, and ammonia bunkering for maritime vessels

.

This impacts freight forwarders with ocean shipping operations, requiring coordination with carriers on shore-side electricity costs and potential service modifications for container ships calling at TEN-T ports.

---

## Open Questions

- **Member State Variations**: How will different national implementation approaches affect cross-border freight operations? Depends on national policy framework assessments by December 2026.

- **Grid Capacity Limitations**: Will electrical grid upgrades keep pace with AFIR infrastructure requirements? 

The scale of required charging infrastructure will pose challenges for local grids, especially at high-power charging sites. Grid congestion and lengthy permitting procedures may delay timely deployment

.

- **Cost Pass-Through Mechanisms**: How will charging costs be allocated between freight forwarders, carriers, and shippers for electric vehicle operations? Depends on market development and contract negotiations.

- **Hydrogen Infrastructure Economics**: Will the 200km interval requirement for hydrogen refueling create economically viable networks? Depends on EU funding allocation and private investment levels.

- **Enforcement Mechanisms**: What specific penalties will Member States implement for non-compliance? 

If a Member State doesn't achieve national objectives, it will be requested to take corrective measures

, but specific penalty structures remain undefined.

- **Technology Standards Evolution**: How will ISO 15118 implementation affect charging interoperability and costs? 

Mandatory implementation of EN ISO 15118-2:2016 for all newly installed or renovated publicly accessible AC charging points from 8 January 2026, and EN ISO 15118-20:2022 from 1 January 2027

.

fullBrief:

Based on my research, I'll now create a comprehensive intelligence brief for the Community of European Railways (CER) and EU rail decarbonization initiatives. This brief will focus on the policy and regulatory framework rather than CER as a single regulation, since CER is an advocacy organization driving multiple regulatory initiatives.

# Community of European Railways (CER) Rail Decarbonization and Modal Shift Initiative

**Regulatory Fact Document** | April 2026
**Primary source:** [CER Official Website](https://www.cer.be/) | [TEN-T Regulation (EU) 2024/1679](https://eur-lex.europa.eu/legal-content/EN/TXT/?uri=CELEX:32024R1679)

---

## What This Regulation Is and Why It Applies



The Community of European Railways (CER) presented its "On Track for Europe" agenda for 2024-2029 at the European Parliament, identifying four fundamental pillars for future rail policy: fair competition among transport modes, adequate financing of railways, implementing key digital tools for the rail sector, and a greener approach to market and competition policies.

 This initiative operates through multiple regulatory instruments, most notably the revised 

TEN-T Regulation (EU) 2024/1679, which entered into force on 18 July 2024

, establishing binding requirements for rail infrastructure development and modal shift targets.



CER has called for full rail decarbonization by 2050, proposing binding greenhouse gas reduction targets of 25% by 2030, 65% by 2040, and 100% by 2050 for EU transport

. The initiative directly affects freight forwarders through infrastructure requirements, capacity constraints, and cost pass-through mechanisms for rail services. 

CER targets achieving a 30% modal share for rail freight by 2030, supported by the Connecting Europe Facility's €25.8 billion allocation for rail projects in the 2021-2027 period

.

For freight forwarders, this means immediate operational changes in route planning, client pricing structures, and service offerings. 

Rail emits 80% less CO2 per tonne-kilometer than trucks on average across Europe

, creating both competitive advantages and compliance pressures when clients demand lower-carbon logistics solutions.

**Source:** TEN-T Regulation (EU) 2024/1679, Articles 1-5; CER On Track for Europe 2024-2029 Manifesto

---

## Issues Requiring Immediate Action

### Infrastructure Compliance Requirements by 2030



The core railway network must be fully electrified and ensure 740-meter-long freight trains can circulate, with passenger trains meeting 160 km/h minimum line speeds by 2030

. 

Freight terminals must be expanded to accommodate 740m long trains and meet current and projected traffic demands, promoting shift to sustainable transport modes

.

**Action Required — Confirm for Your Business:** Assess which rail routes in your network can handle 740m trains and identify capacity constraints that may affect service reliability and pricing.

### Digital Connectivity and ERTMS Implementation



Cross-border interoperability must be improved by implementing the TEN-T Regulation, deploying the European Rail Traffic Management System (ERTMS)

. 

Railways commit to achieving seamless cross-border rail ticketing by 2025 through the Open Sales and Distribution Model (OSDM)

.

**Action Required — Confirm for Your Business:** Verify your rail booking and tracking systems can interface with OSDM requirements and plan for potential integration costs.

### ETS Revenue Allocation Advocacy



CER argues that proceeds from the EU Emissions Trading System (ETS), which generated €43.6 billion in 2023, should flow more into rail, as barely any of this capital currently reaches the railway sector

.

**Action Required — Confirm for Your Business:** Monitor potential rail infrastructure improvements funded through ETS revenues that could affect your service costs and capabilities.

### Urban Node SUMP Requirements



All 431 urban nodes along the TEN-T network must adopt Sustainable Urban Mobility Plans (SUMPs) by 2027, covering freight and passenger mobility for entire urban areas

.

**Action Required — Confirm for Your Business:** Identify which of your delivery locations fall within TEN-T urban nodes and assess how SUMP restrictions might affect last-mile operations.

---

## Operational Impact by Transport Mode

### Rail Freight Operations



Rail freight is particularly well placed to reduce Europe's dependence on imported fuels due to high energy efficiency and major reliance on electric energy

. 

Rail accounts for 1.8% of total EU energy consumption in transport while handling 17% of freight and 8% of passenger traffic, achieving seven times greater energy efficiency than road

.

**Cost Pass-Through Mechanisms:**
- Track access charges may be temporarily waived during transition periods
- 

IMF analysis indicates every 1% of GDP invested in rail infrastructure generates 1.5% GDP increase over four years


- Rail freight capacity expansion funded through CEF grants reduces long-term operator costs

### Road-Rail Intermodal Services



Increased interconnectivity between rail and road can be achieved by making compatibility of trucks and trailers with combined transport mandatory by law or developing intermodal cooperation between railways, roads and inland waterways

. 

The Combined Transport Directive provisions are outdated, with transposition variations between member states creating uncertainty for logistics operators

.

**Operational Requirements:**
- Combined transport trailer compatibility becomes mandatory
- 

Transhipment terminals expanded to meet traffic demands with 740m train accommodation


- 

At least one multimodal freight terminal per urban node by 2040



### Customs and Documentation



Interoperability and safety enhance seamless and safe circulation of goods on railway vehicles across the European Union and beyond its borders

. Digital integration requirements affect customs processing and documentation flows at intermodal terminals.

• **Cross-Border Ticketing Integration Costs:** What are the actual implementation costs for OSDM integration, and will these be passed through to freight forwarders?

• **Military Mobility Requirements:** How will dual-use infrastructure requirements for military mobility affect civilian freight capacity and pricing?

fullBrief:
I need to clarify that the European Clean Trucking Alliance (ECTA) is not a regulation or instrument itself, but rather an advocacy coalition. However, I can create an intelligence brief focused on how this alliance influences European freight forwarding policy and its operational impacts. Let me structure this as a policy intelligence brief focused on the Alliance's influence on regulatory developments.

# European Clean Trucking Alliance (ECTA) Policy Influence Initiative

**Regulatory Fact Document** | April 2026
**Primary source:** [European Clean Trucking Alliance](https://clean-trucking.eu/)

---

## What This Initiative Is and Why It Applies



The European Clean Trucking Alliance (ECTA) is a coalition of eighteen leading European businesses and organizations launched on July 15, 2020

, representing 

major hauliers, logistics and consumer goods companies in Europe and beyond, employing more than 1.6 million people globally with over EUR 325 billion yearly revenue

. 

The Alliance has grown to include over 35 companies and organizations from across Europe calling for zero-emission road freight

.



ECTA calls for zero-emission road freight and drives the development, implementation and support of future-proof policies, programmes and initiatives to create an ecosystem that shifts away from fossil-fuelled vehicles to vans and trucks with zero tailpipe emissions

. The Alliance directly influences EU policy development through 

advocacy, position papers and events

 and has been instrumental in shaping recent regulatory developments including the revised Heavy-Duty Vehicle CO2 Standards.

This matters to freight forwarders because ECTA's advocacy positions become EU policy reality. 

In its May 2023 consultation response, ECTA urged policymakers to significantly strengthen the proposed CO2 targets for heavy-duty vehicles to a 100% zero-emissions target by 2035, and increased the proposed 2030 CO2 reduction target from 45% to 65%

. These exact targets were adopted in 

Regulation (EU) 2024/1610, approved by the European Parliament on April 10, 2024 and ratified by the Council on May 13, 2024

.

**Source:** ECTA position papers, EU Regulation (EU) 2024/1610

---

## Issues Requiring Immediate Action

### Zero-Emission Truck Fleet Planning (2025-2030)


The EU Heavy-Duty Vehicle CO2 standards now require carbon emissions from large trucks and buses to be reduced by 45 percent from 2030, 65 percent from 2035, and 90 percent from 2040

. ECTA members are already deploying these vehicles, with 

DFDS operating 95 electric trucks across Sweden, Denmark, Lithuania, Belgium, and the Netherlands as of 2024

.

**Action Required — Confirm for Your Business:** Review current truck procurement contracts and assess timeline for zero-emission vehicle integration to meet 2030 45% reduction targets.

### Infrastructure Dependency Assessment


Currently there is no legal framework for deploying infrastructure for zero-emission trucks. The revision of the AFID should urgently provide it

. 

Truck parking areas will represent the crucial locations to deploy overnight public chargers (~75-100kW), which together with high power and megawatt public chargers will be essential to enable the electrification

.

**Action Required — Confirm for Your Business:** Map current route network against planned charging infrastructure deployment to identify operational gaps.

### Carrier Relationship Restructuring


Trucks account for only 2% of vehicles on the road yet are responsible for 22% of road transport CO2 emissions in the EU. We cannot meet the Paris agreement without decarbonising road freight

. Carriers like 

Girteka Logistics must replace 7,400 Euro 6 trucks with zero-emission vehicles

.

**Action Required — Confirm for Your Business:** Assess current carrier mix and evaluate their zero-emission transition plans to avoid service disruptions.

### Policy Signal Tracking


In a June 2024 position paper, ECTA supported heavy-duty vehicle electrification in the EU's Greening Corporate Fleets proposed regulation. ECTA advocated for charging infrastructure targets post-2030 to be considered during the EU Commission's planned review in 2024

.

**Action Required — Confirm for Your Business:** Monitor ECTA position papers as early indicators of upcoming EU regulatory requirements.

---

## Operational Impact by Transport Mode

### Road Transport
**Direct Cost Impact:** 

ECTA's financing study identifies key financing needs and barriers for zero-emission trucks, exploring various mechanisms including traditional loans, grants, tax incentives, and innovative financial instruments such as green bonds or green premiums

. Zero-emission trucks carry premium costs that will flow through to freight rates.

**Fleet Transition Timeline:** 

New urban buses must be 100% zero-emission by 2035, with an intermediate target of 90% by 2030

. 

Heavy trucks >7.5t face 45% CO2 reduction from 2030, 65% from 2035, and 90% from 2040

.

**Geographic Limitations:** 

ECTA calls for a coordinated approach for the introduction of zero-emission zones for freight, accompanied by reward and fleet recognition schemes

. Urban delivery operations will face increasing access restrictions.

### Intermodal Operations


ECTA called for the use of zero-emission trucks under the revision of the Combined Transport Directive in a May 2023 position paper

. Terminal access and first/last mile connections will require zero-emission capability.

### Customs and Reporting


Manufacturers determine CO2 emissions and fuel consumption using the VECTO tool, with Member States providing information to the Commission on HDVs registered for the first time in the EU

. Enhanced reporting requirements will affect fleet documentation.

fullBrief:
# EPA Heavy-Duty Phase 3 Greenhouse Gas Emissions Standards

**Regulatory Fact Document** | April 2026
**Primary source:** [Federal Register 89 FR 29440](https://www.federalregister.gov/documents/2024/04/22/2024-06809/greenhouse-gas-emissions-standards-for-heavy-duty-vehicles-phase-3)

---

## What This Regulation Is and Why It Applies



The EPA Heavy-Duty Phase 3 Greenhouse Gas Emissions Standards (89 FR 29440) is a final rule announced on March 29, 2024, that sets stronger standards to reduce greenhouse gas emissions from heavy-duty (HD) vehicles beginning in model year (MY) 2027, promulgating new greenhouse gas (GHG) emissions standards for model year (MY) 2032 and later heavy-duty highway vehicles.

 

The regulation took effect on June 21, 2024.





The new standards apply to HD vocational vehicles (such as delivery trucks, refuse haulers, public utility trucks, transit, shuttle, school buses) and tractors (such as day cabs and sleeper cabs on tractor-trailer trucks).

 

The rule covers HDVs in Classes 2b-8, representing the most stringent federal standards to date for GHG emissions from heavy-duty engines and vehicles (with a GVWR over 14,000 pounds).



This regulation directly impacts freight forwarders because it establishes the federal emissions baseline that determines trucking equipment costs, fleet composition, and operational capabilities for all US domestic trucking subcontractors. 

Phase 3 standards are significantly more stringent, reducing GHG emissions from HD vehicles by 29% below 2021 levels by 2032, while Phase 2 standards reduced emissions by 17% below 2010 levels by 2021.

 The regulation creates direct cost pass-through pressure through higher truck acquisition costs, fleet electrification requirements, and maintenance infrastructure investments that trucking companies will embed in their freight pricing.

**Source:** 40 CFR Parts 86, 1036, 1037, 1065, 1068, 1648; 89 FR 29440

---

## Issues Requiring Immediate Action

### Congressional Review Act Challenge Filed


On January 22, 2025, a resolution of disapproval under the Congressional Review Act was introduced in the US House of Representatives.

 This creates regulatory uncertainty regarding the rule's long-term viability.

**Action Required — Confirm for Your Business:** Monitor Federal Register for any Congressional action affecting the rule's status. Maintain contingency planning for both rule continuation and potential rescission scenarios.

### Fleet Electrification Timeline Pressures Begin MY2027


EPA's projected ZEV adoption rates for MY2032 are: 60% for light heavy-duty vocational vehicles, 40% for medium heavy-duty vocational vehicles, 30% for heavy heavy-duty vocational vehicles, and 40% for day cab tractors.

 

The standards increase in stringency at a slower pace from MY2027 to MY2030 compared to the proposal, with day cab tractor standards starting in MY2028 and heavy heavy-duty vocational vehicle standards starting in MY2029.



**Action Required — Confirm for Your Business:** Assess your trucking subcontractors' fleet modernization plans and determine how equipment transition costs will affect your freight rates starting in 2027.

### Warranty and Battery Monitoring Requirements


EPA is adding warranty requirements for batteries and other components of zero-emission vehicles and requiring customer-facing battery state-of-health monitors for plug-in hybrid and battery electric vehicles, with new battery and electric powertrain warranty requirements for ZEVs beginning with MY2027.



**Action Required — Confirm for Your Business:** Review service level agreements with trucking partners to ensure battery/electric powertrain failures don't create service disruptions without clear liability allocation.

### State-Federal Regulatory Divergence Risk


The Phase 3 standards fall against the backdrop of the California Air Resources Board's Advanced Clean Truck rule, which EPA approved last year and requires manufacturers to phase out most gasoline-powered HD vehicles by 2035.

 Federal rule uncertainty creates potential regulatory divergence between CARB states and federal-only jurisdictions.

**Action Required — Confirm for Your Business:** Map your freight lanes between CARB states (CA, OR, WA, NY, NJ, MA, CT, RI, VT, MD, NM) and non-CARB states to identify potential equipment availability and cost differentials.

---

## Operational Impact by Transport Mode

### Road Transport - Over-the-Road Trucking


The final "Phase 3" standards build on EPA's Heavy-Duty Phase 2 program from 2016 and maintain that program's flexible structure, which is designed to reflect the diverse nature of the heavy-duty vehicle industry. The standards are technology-neutral and performance-based, allowing each manufacturer to choose what set of emissions control technologies is best suited for them and the needs of their customers.



**Cost Pass-Through Mechanisms:** Trucking companies must invest in either: (1) advanced internal combustion engine technologies with hybridization, (2) plug-in hybrid systems, or (3) full battery electric or fuel cell systems. 

The annual cost of vehicle technology and EVSE (not including tax credits) would be approximately $1.1 billion, while the HD industry would save approximately $3.5 billion in annual operating costs (e.g., savings that come from less liquid fuel used, lower maintenance and repair costs for ZEV technologies as compared to ICE technologies).



**Surcharge Calculations:** Expect "EPA Phase 3 compliance surcharges" to appear in trucking contracts starting MY2027, calculated as the differential cost between Phase 2 compliant and Phase 3 compliant equipment amortized over typical asset life cycles (5-7 years for vocational vehicles, 8-10 years for long-haul tractors).

### Road Transport - Last-Mile Delivery


For light heavy-duty vocational vehicles, EPA's projected ZEV adoption rate is 60% by MY2032.

 This category includes delivery trucks, box trucks, and urban distribution vehicles commonly used by freight forwarders for final delivery services.

**Contract Implications:** Last-mile delivery partners will face the highest electrification pressure, requiring charging infrastructure investments and route optimization around battery range limitations. Expect delivery time windows to become more restrictive as electric vehicles require charging cycles, and premium pricing for guaranteed delivery times during peak demand periods.

### Road Transport - Intermodal Drayage


For day cab tractors, EPA projects 0% ZEV adoption in MY2027 increasing to 40% in MY2032.

 Port and rail yard drayage operations face unique compliance pressures due to short-haul, high-cycle operations ideal for electric drivetrains.

**Operational Changes:** Port access fees and environmental zones in major gateway cities (Los Angeles, Long Beach, New York/New Jersey, Savannah, Charleston) will increasingly favor or require zero-emission drayage equipment, creating potential bottlenecks for freight forwarding operations that don't control their drayage fleet composition.

---

---

## Open Questions

• **Congressional Review Act Outcome:** 

A resolution of disapproval under the Congressional Review Act was introduced in the US House of Representatives on January 22, 2025.

 Final Congressional action and any potential Senate companion resolution remain pending.

• **Infrastructure Development Pace:** EPA's projected ZEV adoption rates assume adequate charging and hydrogen fueling infrastructure development. Actual infrastructure rollout may lag projections, affecting compliance feasibility.

• **Technology Cost Evolution:** 

EPA commitment to monitor technology and infrastructure development to ensure successful implementation includes extensive collaboration with DOT, DOE, and National Laboratories on new studies to inform updates of battery costs, critical materials & supply chain, infrastructure, grid impacts, and IRA incentives.

 Future cost adjustments may affect industry viability calculations.

• **State Waiver Coordination:** Integration between federal Phase 3 standards and existing CARB Advanced Clean Truck rule waivers in Section 177 states remains subject to ongoing EPA-CARB coordination processes.

• **Averaging, Banking, and Trading Program Details:** 

The rule updates discrete elements of the Averaging Banking and Trading program, including providing additional flexibilities for manufacturers to support the implementation of the Phase 3 program balanced by limiting the availability of certain advanced technology credits initially established under the HD GHG Phase 2 rule.

 Specific credit values and trading mechanisms await implementing guidance.
