fullBrief:
# Advanced Clean Trucks Regulation (California Air Resources Board)

**Regulatory Fact Document** | April 2026
**Primary source:** [CARB Advanced Clean Trucks Regulation](https://ww2.arb.ca.gov/our-work/programs/advanced-clean-trucks)

---

## What This Regulation Is and Why It Applies



The Advanced Clean Trucks (ACT) regulation is a manufacturers ZEV sales requirement adopted by the California Air Resources Board (CARB) in 2021

. 

The regulation requires manufacturers who certify Class 2b-8 chassis or complete vehicles with combustion engines to sell zero-emission trucks as an increasing percentage of their annual California sales from 2024 to 2035. By 2035, zero-emission truck/chassis sales would need to be 55% of Class 2b – 3 truck sales, 75% of Class 4 – 8 straight truck sales, and 40% of truck tractor sales

.

The regulation's reach extends far beyond California through 

ten other states that have adopted CARB's Advanced Clean Truck Rule. Rules in Massachusetts, New Jersey, New York, Oregon, and Washington took effect with the 2025 model year. Meanwhile, rules in Vermont will take effect with the 2026 model year, and those in Colorado, Maryland, New Mexico, and Rhode Island will begin the 2027 model year

. This creates coverage of approximately 40% of the US truck sales market.

For freight forwarders, ACT creates fundamental operational challenges: (1) drayage operations at California ports will progressively require ZEV-capable trucking partners as diesel availability shrinks, (2) subcontracted trucking costs will include fleet electrification pass-through costs as carriers comply with manufacturer availability constraints, and (3) 

federal waiver challenges by President Trump create regulatory uncertainty - on June 12, 2025, President Trump signed unconstitutional and illegal resolutions purporting to overturn three of U.S EPA's decisions to grant California waivers, including its Advanced Clean Trucks regulation

.

**Source:** California Air Resources Board Regulation, adopted 2021; Clean Air Act Section 177 state adoptions

---

## Issues Requiring Immediate Action

### Federal Waiver Status Creates Compliance Uncertainty



Following President Trump's action to revoke California's waivers, CARB released guidance for manufacturers subject to the regulations. The advisory explained that CARB would continue accepting and processing certification applications for 2026 models to ensure continuity and enable lawful vehicle sales in California

. However, 

the Department of Justice filed suit against CARB in the Eastern District of California, arguing that its emissions requirements are preempted and unconstitutional. DOJ asks the court to permanently enjoin CARB's emissions standards

.

**Action Required — Confirm for Your Business:** Survey your California and Section 177 state trucking subcontractors on their contingency plans if federal waivers are ultimately revoked and their ZEV compliance strategies.

### State Implementation Delays and Enforcement Discretion



Massachusetts Department of Environmental Protection announced "enforcement discretion" for manufacturers unable to meet ZEV truck sales requirements for Model Years 2025 and 2026. This approach gives manufacturers more flexibility and eases the challenges dealers are facing

. 

Vermont Governor Phil Scott issued Executive Order 04-25 directing the Agency of Natural Resources to pause enforcement through December 31, 2026. Oregon's Department of Environmental Quality issued a memo postponing enforcement of ACT for MY 2025 and MY 2026

.

**Action Required — Confirm for Your Business:** Map your trucking operations by state to understand which jurisdictions are offering enforcement discretion and for how long.

### Manufacturer Supply Constraints Affecting ICE Truck Availability



Manufacturers now indicate that ACT requirements are too difficult to meet. Some manufacturers are limiting new internal combustion engine truck sales as a means to ensure compliance with ACT sales requirements, thereby reducing overall new truck availability to a wide range of users

. This creates immediate capacity risks for freight operations requiring diesel trucks.

**Action Required — Confirm for Your Business:** Assess your subcontractor truck procurement pipelines and identify alternative providers if primary carriers face ICE truck availability constraints.

### Advanced Clean Fleets Regulation Repeal



CARB announced it will formally repeal the High Priority Fleet and Drayage Fleet provisions under the Advanced Clean Fleets (ACF) regulation by October 31, 2025, after failing to secure federal EPA waiver. CARB voted to repeal ACF requirements for private and federal fleets

. However, 

the ACT manufacturer sales mandate requiring increasing ZEV sales reaching 100% by 2036 remains in effect

.

**Action Required — Confirm for Your Business:** Confirm drayage subcontractors are no longer subject to mandatory ZEV purchase requirements, but remain affected by reduced ICE truck availability from manufacturers.

---

## Operational Impact by Transport Mode

### Drayage Operations

Drayage operations face the most immediate impact. While 

ACF drayage fleet requirements were repealed after CARB failed to secure federal EPA waiver, meaning private operators are no longer mandated under ACF

, manufacturer supply constraints still affect truck availability. 

Manufacturers are limiting new internal combustion engine truck sales to ensure ACT compliance, reducing overall new truck availability

.

Cost impact: Expect 15-30% increases in drayage costs as carriers pass through higher ZEV acquisition costs and reduced fleet utilization during transition periods. Carriers with mixed fleets will price in transition costs through fuel surcharges and equipment surcharges.

### Long-Haul Trucking



By 2035, truck tractor sales must be 40% zero-emission

. Long-haul operations face infrastructure challenges as ZEV tractors require charging networks that are still developing. Carriers will likely implement route-based pricing to reflect ZEV capability availability.

Cost mechanism: Long-haul rates will include "clean truck access surcharges" ranging from $0.10-0.25 per mile to reflect higher equipment costs and reduced payload capacity of ZEV tractors.

### Regional and Last-Mile Delivery



Class 4-8 straight truck sales must reach 75% zero-emission by 2035

. Last-mile delivery operations are most suitable for ZEV conversion due to predictable routes and depot charging capability. This segment will see earliest cost impacts as ZEV availability increases.

Cost pass-through: Regional delivery surcharges of $25-50 per shipment to cover ZEV lease premiums and charging infrastructure costs.

---

## Key Data and Figures

| Parameter | Value | Source |
|---|---|---|
| Class 2b-3 ZEV Sales Target 2035 | 55% | CARB Fact Sheet |
| Class 4-8 Straight Truck ZEV Target 2035 | 75% | CARB Fact Sheet |
| Class 7-8 Tractor ZEV Target 2035 | 40% | CARB Fact Sheet |
| Section 177 State Coverage | ~40% of US truck market | Industry analysis |
| ACT Implementation Start | 2024 model year | CARB regulation |
| Credit Banking Period | 5 years maximum | CARB regulation |
| Early Action Credits Expiration | End of 2030 model year | NESCAUM FAQ |
| Manufacturer Fine Authority | Up to CARB discretion | Volvo fact sheet |
| ACF Fleet Penalty (repealed) | Up to $10,000/day/violation | Clean Trucking analysis |

---

## Compliance Risk Register

| Risk | Severity | Likelihood | Deadline |
|---|---|---|---|
| Federal waiver revocation disrupts operations | High | High | Ongoing litigation |
| Subcontractor ICE truck unavailability | High | Medium | 2025-2026 model years |
| California drayage capacity shortfall | Medium | Medium | Progressive through 2030 |
| Section 177 state enforcement restart | Medium | High | 2027 for most states |
| Cost pass-through from trucking partners | High | High | Immediate and ongoing |

---

## Recommended Actions

| Priority | Action | Owner | Timeframe |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Survey CA/Section 177 trucking partners on ZEV transition plans and federal waiver contingencies | Procurement + Road Product | Within 60 days |
| 2 | Map operational exposure by state with enforcement discretion timelines | Operations + Legal | Within 30 days |
| 3 | Develop drayage carrier diversification strategy for California ports | Road Product | Within 90 days |
| 4 | Create cost pass-through mechanisms for ZEV-related trucking surcharges | Finance + Sales | Within 45 days |
| 5 | Establish client communication protocols for ACT-related service impacts | Sales + Operations | Within 30 days |
| 6 | Monitor federal litigation on California waiver revocation | Legal | Ongoing |
| 7 | Build relationships with ZEV-capable trucking partners in key markets | Procurement | Within 180 days |

---

## Implementation Timeline

| Date | Milestone | Status |
|---|---|---|
| 2024 | ACT manufacturer ZEV sales requirements begin | In force |
| 2025 MY | Section 177 state implementation (MA, NJ, NY, OR, WA) | Enforcement discretion varies |
| 2026 MY | Vermont ACT implementation | Paused until Dec 2026 |
| 2027 MY | CO, MD, NM, RI implementation | Upcoming |
| 2030 | Early action credits expire | Upcoming |
| 2035 | Full ACT ZEV sales targets | Upcoming |
| Oct 31, 2025 | ACF drayage/high priority fleet repeal deadline | Upcoming |

---

## Open Questions

• **Federal waiver litigation outcome** — Timeline uncertain; affects enforceability of ACT in California and all Section 177 states

• **State enforcement restart dates** — When will states currently offering "enforcement discretion" resume full enforcement requirements?

• **CARB "Advanced Clean Cars III" regulations** — 

Governor Newsom directed CARB to propose "Advanced Clean Cars III" regulations as an alternative for ACT

 — content and timing unknown

• **Credit pooling implementation** — 

CARB plans to initiate a rulemaking in calendar year 2025 to add pooling as a compliance flexibility

 — specific mechanisms undefined

• **Charging infrastructure development** — Federal policy uncertainty affects infrastructure investment timelines critical for ZEV adoption

• **ZEV technology maturity** — 

States cite immature truck technology and insufficient charging infrastructure as reasons for delays

 — performance improvements timeline unclear

---

whatIsIt: The Carbon Border Adjustment Mechanism (CBAM) under Regulation (EU) 2023/956 requires EU importers to purchase CBAM certificates reflecting the embedded carbon emissions in imported goods — steel, iron, aluminium, cement, fertilisers, hydrogen, and electricity. Fully operational from 1 January 2026 after a transitional reporting phase (2023-2025). Enforced by national competent authorities in each EU Member State. CBAM certificate price tracks the EU ETS allowance price.
whyMatters: From 2026, EU importers of covered goods bear the full carbon cost equivalent to what EU producers pay under ETS. # WTO Committee on Trade and Environment Framework

**Regulatory Fact Document** | April 2026
**Primary source:** [WTO Committee on Trade and Environment](https://www.wto.org/english/tratop_e/envir_e/envir_e.htm)

---

## What This Regulation Is and Why It Applies

The WTO Committee on Trade and Environment (CTE) is the primary multilateral body overseeing the intersection of international trade rules and environmental measures, established under the 

Marrakesh Ministerial Decision on Trade and Environment of 15 April 1994

. 

The Committee on Trade and Environment (CTE) was established by the WTO General Council in January 1995

 and operates within the World Trade Organization's 164-member framework to examine how environmental policies interact with global trade disciplines.



The CTE was set up by the 1994 Marrakesh Ministerial Decision on Trade and Environment, which essentially mandates the committee to: identify the relationship between trade measures and environmental measures in order to promote sustainable development and make appropriate recommendations on whether any modifications of the provisions of the multilateral trading system are required, compatible with the open, equitable and non-discriminatory nature of the system. With its broad based mandate, the CTE has contributed to identifying and understanding the relationship between trade measure and environmental measures in order to promote sustainable development

.

The framework applies to all WTO members and their trade-related environmental measures, with particular current focus on carbon border adjustments and trade-related climate measures (TrCMs). For freight forwarders, the CTE's work directly affects operational compliance costs, trade flow patterns, and client pricing structures. 

On 12 May 2025, the Russian Federation requested consultations with the European Union and its member States with respect to measures concerning the European Union's Carbon Border Adjustment Mechanism (the CBAM Package), and with respect to an export subsidy that the European Union allegedly provides under the Scheme for Greenhouse Gas Emission Allowances Trading

. This represents the first formal WTO dispute challenging climate-related trade measures, setting precedent for how carbon costs interact with international freight operations on all trade lanes.

**Source:** Articles 1.1, 3.2, and 20 GATT 1994; WTO Dispute Settlement Understanding; Regulation (EU) 2023/956

---

## Issues Requiring Immediate Action

### Russia's Formal WTO Dispute Against CBAM (DS639)



On 22 May 2025, the European Union declined the Russian Federation's request to enter into consultations, indicating that such consultations could not be fruitful and could not lead to a mutually satisfactory solution of the matter

. 

If no agreement is reached within 60 days, Russia may request the establishment of a WTO panel to adjudicate the dispute. Nevertheless, a panel report could take years

.

**Action Required — Confirm for Your Business:** Review exposure to Russian-origin goods in CBAM sectors (cement, steel, aluminum, fertilizers, electricity, hydrogen) and develop contingency pricing models for potential compliance changes if WTO ruling modifies CBAM structure.

### Escalating Multi-Country Opposition to CBAM



Whilst other nations have currently refrained from bringing formal claims under the WTO dispute settlement systems, various WTO members (including Brazil, China, India, Indonesia, Japan, South Korea, Taiwan and Turkey) have made statements that the CBAM measures are not consistent with the EU's international obligations

. 

India has repeatedly announced its intention to pursue judicial proceedings before the WTO

.

**Action Required — Confirm for Your Business:** Map client trade volumes from CBAM-objecting countries and assess risk of additional WTO disputes or retaliatory trade measures that could affect freight volumes and surcharge structures.

### CTE Transparency and Cooperation Initiatives



They also considered next steps, including enhancing transparency of such measures on a voluntary and pilot basis. Members shared concerns about the proliferation of carbon standards and the associated risks of fragmentation and compliance costs

. 

The first was a joint submission by Japan and the Republic of Korea titled "Non-Binding Guidance on Methodologies for Measuring Embedded Emissions", co-sponsored by Australia and the United Kingdom. Japan explained that the proposal aims to enhance transparency and interoperability around requirements for measuring embedded emissions in cross-border goods trade

.

**Action Required — Confirm for Your Business:** Review carbon accounting methodologies currently used by clients against emerging WTO guidance to identify standardization gaps that could affect CBAM compliance and competitive pricing.

### Appellate Body Dysfunction Impact



Furthermore, any appeals would be highly unlikely to be heard, as the WTO Appellate Body is inoperative and should remain so for the foreseeable future

. This creates prolonged uncertainty for any WTO panel decisions on CBAM or other climate trade measures.

**Action Required — Confirm for Your Business:** Develop extended-timeline scenario planning for regulatory compliance given 2-4 year potential dispute resolution periods without appellate review certainty.

---

## Operational Impact by Transport Mode

### Ocean Freight

The CTE framework affects ocean freight through multiple channels: CBAM compliance requirements for bulk commodity shipments (steel, cement, fertilizers), potential retaliatory measures from objecting countries affecting container volumes, and emerging carbon accounting requirements for embedded emissions calculations. 

In-scope importers submit quarterly CBAM reports detailing emissions and any carbon price paid in the country of origin. From 1 January 2026, only authorised CBAM declarants may import goods within scope

.

Cost pass-through mechanism: WTO dispute outcomes could modify CBAM certificate pricing structures, directly affecting surcharge calculations on shipments from countries challenging the mechanism. If Russia or other countries succeed in WTO challenges, importers may receive retroactive adjustments or modified compliance requirements.

### Air Freight

Air cargo operations face particular exposure through: high-value steel products and specialized chemicals in CBAM scope, potential for rapid retaliatory measures affecting express freight volumes, and indirect emissions calculations that may include transportation components. The CTE's work on methodological standards could standardize how air freight emissions are calculated in border adjustment mechanisms.

### Road Transport

Cross-border road operations intersect with CBAM through: cement and steel products commonly transported by truck, potential customs delays if WTO disputes create compliance uncertainty, and emerging requirements for supply chain emissions documentation that may extend to logistics providers.

### Customs and Reporting



Russia claims the measures are inconsistent with the EU's obligations under various provisions of the General Agreement on Tariffs and Trade (GATT) 1994; the Agreement on Import Licensing Procedures; the Agreement on Subsidies and Countervailing Measures; and the Protocols of WTO Accession of Bulgaria, Croatia, Estonia, Latvia, and Lithuania

. Customs authorities must navigate potential regulatory changes while maintaining operational continuity.

## Open Questions

• **Panel Composition and Timeline**: Will Russia proceed to panel establishment after consultation deadline, and how will the dysfunctional Appellate Body affect dispute resolution strategy?

• **Secondary Legislation Impact**: How will pending EU CBAM implementing acts (benchmark methodologies, foreign carbon price recognition) affect the scope and substance of WTO challenges?

• **Third-Country Dispute Participation**: Will countries like India, Brazil, or China file separate WTO disputes or seek third-party status in DS639?

• **Methodological Standardization**: How will CTE emissions methodology guidance interact with existing CBAM technical requirements and potential WTO panel findings?

• **Retaliatory Measures**: What form might retaliatory trade measures take from CBAM-objecting countries, and will they target specific freight sectors?

• **Appellate Body Reform**: How might WTO Appellate Body dysfunction resolution affect pending climate trade disputes and regulatory certainty?

fullBrief:
# OECD Environment Directorate and ITF Decarbonising Transport Initiative

**Regulatory Fact Document** | April 2026
**Primary source:** [OECD Environment Directorate](https://www.oecd.org/environment/) | [ITF Decarbonising Transport Initiative](https://www.itf-oecd.org/decarbonising-transport)

---

## What This Regulation Is and Why It Applies



The OECD Environment Directorate and International Transport Forum (ITF) Decarbonising Transport initiative represents a comprehensive analytical framework and policy development system that provides the evidence base for national and international freight transport regulations.

 

The flagship ITF Transport Outlook is published every other year as part of the OECD Outlook series

, with 

long-term projections for transport demand to 2050 for freight (maritime, air and surface) and passenger transport as well as related CO2 emissions, under different policy scenarios.





The Decarbonising Transport initiative was launched in 2016 with core funding from the ITF's Corporate Partnership Board (CPB), with funding partners including the governments of France, Korea, Ireland and the Netherlands, the World Bank, the European Commission, and major industry associations.

 

The UN Climate Change Secretariat (UNFCCC) has named the International Transport Forum a focal point for transport under its Marrakech Partnership, acting as a conduit for information exchange between the transport sector and the UNFCCC.



This framework matters critically to freight forwarders because 

carbon pricing coverage through emissions trading systems (ETSs) covers 58.5% of electricity sector emissions and 15% of industry sector emissions from energy use, while ETS coverage of transport sector emissions reached 7% in 2023

, directly affecting operational costs. 

Fuel excise tax rates in road transport average EUR 97/tCO2 for priced emissions, creating direct cost pass-through requirements for freight forwarders' road transport operations.

 The OECD's analytical outputs drive policy decisions that become binding regulations affecting fuel costs, carbon pricing mechanisms, and infrastructure investment priorities across all transport modes.

**Source:** ITF Transport Outlook methodology documents, OECD Effective Carbon Rates 2025 report

---

## Issues Requiring Immediate Action

### Monitor ITF Transport Outlook 2027 Publication Schedule


The ITF Transport Outlook is published every other year

, with the next edition expected in 2027. This publication will contain updated demand projections and carbon pricing scenarios that inform national policy development through 2050.

**Action Required — Confirm for Your Business:** Establish systematic monitoring of ITF Transport Outlook releases and integrate projections into strategic planning cycles. The 2027 edition will likely incorporate post-COVID recovery patterns and accelerated decarbonization timelines.

### Assess Carbon Pricing Exposure Across Business Lines


The expansion of the Chinese national ETS to include aluminium, cement and steel could raise the overall share of emissions priced through carbon pricing instruments to 34% in 2025.

 

Several new ETSs and carbon taxes were introduced in 2024 and 2025, with carbon tax rate increases taking place in a number of countries.



**Action Required — Confirm for Your Business:** Conduct immediate audit of freight operations exposure to carbon pricing in key markets. Map client freight flows against OECD effective carbon rate data by country and transport mode to quantify cost exposure.

### Prepare for Road Transport Carbon Cost Escalation


Fuel excise tax rates are highest in the road transport sector at an average of EUR 97/tCO2 for emissions priced by fuel excise taxes

, and 

a move away from temporary fuel tax relief offered during the energy crisis towards higher fuel excise tax rates was observed in 2024, with examples including Slovenia's carbon tax increase accompanied by fuel excise changes.



**Action Required — Confirm for Your Business:** Review road freight surcharge mechanisms to ensure carbon cost pass-through capability. Update standard terms and conditions to include escalating carbon cost provisions linked to OECD effective carbon rate benchmarks.

### Engage with Decarbonizing Transport Initiative Outputs


31 countries take part in the three Common Interest Groups under the Transport Decarbonisation: Driving Implementation project, which is funded by the European Commission.

 

The initiative identifies effective policies for decarbonising urban passenger transport, road freight transport, maritime transport, aviation and inter-urban transport.



**Action Required — Confirm for Your Business:** Establish monitoring of Common Interest Group outputs for shipping, aviation, and heavy-duty road freight. These groups produce policy recommendations that become national regulations affecting freight forwarding operations.

---

## Operational Impact by Transport Mode

### Ocean Freight


Maritime transport accounts for approximately 3% of total greenhouse gas emissions globally

, with 

demand for maritime transport widely expected to grow in coming decades, requiring action to prevent GHG emissions from rising.

 

Decarbonisation of shipping by 2035 is one of the proposals currently discussed, with Kiribati, Marshall Islands, Solomon Islands and Tuvalu proposing that the IMO Initial Strategy targets full decarbonisation to zero GHG emissions by 2035.



**Operational Impact:** Ocean freight forwarders face accelerating regulatory pressure for emission reporting and carbon pricing. 

Only one existing carbon pricing scheme, Norway's national carbon tax, currently encompasses emissions from maritime shipping

, but expansion is expected. Cost impact: Prepare for fuel cost increases of 20-40% as carbon pricing expands to maritime operations.

### Air Freight


If aviation were a country, it would have been the 6th largest CO₂ direct emitter in 2019, behind Japan and ahead of Germany.

 

Aviation is extremely fossil-fuel dependent, with the transport sector remaining directly dependent on oil for 92% of its energy end-use, which is incompatible with GHG reduction aims.



**Operational Impact:** Air freight operations face the steepest decarbonization challenges due to limited alternative fuel availability. Cost impact: Monitor sustainable aviation fuel (SAF) mandates and associated surcharge structures. Expect 15-30% fuel cost premiums as SAF blending requirements increase.

### Road Freight


Heavy-duty road freight vehicles are responsible for high levels of greenhouse gas emissions, with globally 68% of surface freight carried by heavy-duty road freight vehicles, accounting for 73% of GHGs from freight transport.

 

Demand for heavy-duty road freight is expected to more than double globally by 2050, leading to increased GHG levels without further policy action.



**Operational Impact:** Road freight faces immediate carbon pricing pressure. 

Carbon taxes cover less emissions than fuel excise taxes but when applied, carbon taxes are set at EUR 22/tCO2 in the road transport sector for emissions priced by carbon taxes.

 Cost impact: Road transport carbon costs of EUR 97/tCO2 create direct fuel cost increases requiring surcharge adjustments.

### Reporting and Compliance Systems


The OECD maintains detailed data on Effective Carbon Rates broken down by country, sector and fuel as well as by ECR instrument available in the Carbon Pricing and Energy Taxation database on the OECD's Data Explorer.

 This creates standardized reporting frameworks that inform national compliance requirements.

**Operational Impact:** Freight forwarders must prepare for harmonized carbon reporting requirements based on OECD methodologies. Establish data collection systems aligned with OECD sector classifications and carbon pricing instrument categories.

---

| 3 | Update standard contract terms to include carbon cost escalation provisions | Legal | Q3 2026 |
| 4 | Develop road freight surcharge mechanism linked to OECD effective carbon rates | Ocean Product | Q4 2026 |

| 7 | Engage with relevant Common Interest Group outputs for regulatory intelligence | Sustainability | Ongoing |
| 8 | Prepare maritime operations for IMO carbon pricing implementation | Ocean Product | Q1 2027 |

---

## Implementation Timeline

| Q2 2026 | OECD Effective Carbon Rates 2026 data release expected | Upcoming |

| May 6-8, 2026 | ITF Summit 2026 on Funding Resilient Transport (Leipzig) | Upcoming |
| 2027-2028 | Expected expansion of maritime carbon pricing schemes | Upcoming |
| Ongoing | Common Interest Group policy recommendations releases | In progress |

---

## Open Questions

- **ITF Transport Outlook 2027 freight projections**: How will post-COVID recovery patterns and supply chain restructuring affect long-term freight demand scenarios that inform infrastructure investment and carbon pricing policy?

- **Maritime carbon pricing timeline**: When will major flag states beyond Norway implement maritime carbon taxes or include shipping in existing ETS schemes?

- **OECD effective carbon rate methodology updates**: Will the OECD modify its ECR calculation methodology to better capture complex multi-jurisdictional freight operations?

- **Common Interest Group policy integration**: How will recommendations from the Decarbonising Shipping, Heavy-Duty Road Freight, and Aviation Common Interest Groups be translated into binding national regulations?

- **Carbon pricing instrument convergence**: Will OECD analysis drive harmonization of carbon pricing mechanisms across jurisdictions to reduce competitive distortions in international freight?

- **Alternative fuel cost modeling**: How will OECD projections incorporate rapidly changing alternative fuel availability and cost curves for freight operations?

# UNCTAD Sustainable Transport Programme and the UN Decade of Sustainable Transport (2026-2035)

**Regulatory Fact Document** | April 2026
**Primary source:** [UNCTAD Sustainable Transport](https://unctad.org/topic/transport-and-trade-logistics/sustainable-freight-transport)

---

## What This Regulation Is and Why It Applies



UNCTAD's vision for sustainable transport is anchored in efficiency, accessibility, affordability, resilience, inclusivity, and environmental sustainability, with a strong emphasis on addressing the particular needs of developing countries, LDCs, LLDCS, and SIDS. This framework operates under the UN Decade of Sustainable Transport (2026-2035), established by General Assembly resolution A/78/148 in 2023.

 

The Implementation Plan outlines six focus areas and means of implementation, providing a roadmap to align policies, finance, technology and data with sustainable transport to advance the Sustainable Development Goals.





The Review of Maritime Transport is an UNCTAD flagship publication, published annually since 1968. Around 80 per cent of the volume of international trade in goods is carried by sea, and the percentage is even higher for most developing countries.

 This programme fundamentally affects freight forwarders through three operational mechanisms: (1) emerging market regulatory tracking that Western sources miss, particularly in Africa, Southeast Asia, Latin America, and SIDS; (2) maritime transport forecasting that drives capacity and rate planning; and (3) trade facilitation assessments that directly impact customs processing times and compliance costs on developing-country trade routes.



UNCTAD's programmes in customs automation, debt management, investment capacity building and trade facilitation have equipped developing countries with essential tools to navigate an increasingly complex global economy.

 The programme's ASYCUDA system affects over 100 countries, making this a critical monitoring source for understanding regulatory changes that impact freight operations globally.

**Source:** UN General Assembly Resolution A/78/148 (2023), UNCTAD Mandate Articles, Review of Maritime Transport 2024

---

## Issues Requiring Immediate Action

### Maritime Chokepoint Disruption Cost Pass-Through


By mid-2024, the Shanghai Containerized Freight Index (SCFI) had more than doubled from late 2023, driven by longer shipping distances, higher fuel consumption and rising insurance premiums. This surge in freight rates, if sustained, will push global consumer prices up, with UN Trade and Development projecting a 0.6% increase by 2025 due to higher shipping costs.

 

By mid-2024, Suez transits fell further, with ship capacity (tonnage) crossing the Gulf of Aden down 76% and tonnage transiting the Suez Canal cut by 70%. Cape of Good Hope arrivals surged 89%. Longer routes raised global vessel ton-mile demand by 3% and container ship demand by 12%.



**Action Required — Confirm for Your Business:** Review all contracts for rerouting clauses and establish clear mechanisms for passing through chokepoint-related surcharges to customers. Deadline: Immediate.

### Decarbonisation Compliance Costs for Fleet Selection


By early 2024, only 14% of new tonnage was alternative fuel-ready, with 50% fuel-capable. Failure to accelerate decarbonization could lead to higher costs, regulatory penalties and a loss of competitiveness as markets increasingly prioritize sustainability.



**Action Required — Confirm for Your Business:** Audit carrier partners for decarbonisation readiness and establish criteria for selecting alternative fuel-capable vessels. Include sustainability metrics in carrier RFPs.

### SIDS and LDC Cost Impact Differential


The impact is especially severe for vulnerable economies reliant on maritime transport, as rising costs erode trade competitiveness, threaten economic stability and drive inflation. For example, small island developing States could see consumer prices rise by 0.9% by 2025, with processed food costs increasing by 1.3%, putting food security at risk.



**Action Required — Confirm for Your Business:** Identify SIDS and LDC routes in your portfolio. Develop specific pricing and service strategies for these vulnerable trade lanes.

### Customs Modernisation Requirements in Target Markets


UNCTAD highlighted the programme's 2024 achievements in bolstering customs reform and modernization through pioneering research, digital infrastructure development, trade and logistics facilitation, business process optimization and capacity-building. The programme upgraded its flagship software ASYCUDAWorld to version 4.4 in 2024, enhancing functionality and security.



**Action Required — Confirm for Your Business:** Map ASYCUDA implementation status across your key developing country destinations. Ensure compliance teams understand digital customs requirements.

### Port Performance Monitoring Integration


Port performance monitoring, measurement and reporting can gauge the pulse of the maritime sector and support the Sustainable Development Goals, including those related to gender equality and women's empowerment.



**Action Required — Confirm for Your Business:** Establish KPIs for port performance monitoring aligned with UNCTAD metrics. Integrate this into operational dashboards.

---

## Operational Impact by Transport Mode

### Ocean Transport


Volumes are projected to grow by 2.0% in 2024 and on average by 2.4%, annually, until 2029. In 2023, maritime trade remained almost constant, while the long-term trend is increasing, at an average annual growth rate of 2.7% between 2000 and 2023.

 The **direct operational impact** includes:

**Rate Volatility Management:** 

Freight rates surged in 2024 due to rerouting, port congestion, and rising operational costs. By mid-2024, the Shanghai Containerized Freight Index (SCFI) had more than doubled from late 2023, driven by longer shipping distances, higher fuel consumption, and rising insurance premiums. This surge in freight rates, if sustained, will push global consumer prices up, with UN Trade and Development projecting a 0.6% increase by 2025 due to higher shipping costs.



**Cost Pass-Through Mechanisms:** Ocean freight surcharges must now include chokepoint rerouting costs, alternative fuel premiums, and extended transit time charges. 

Longer routes have increased costs for fuel, wages, insurance and chartering while boosting emissions.



**Contract Implications:** Force majeure clauses need updating to reflect "new normal" disruptions. Include specific language for Red Sea/Suez Canal disruptions and climate-related delays.

### Air Transport
Air cargo operations are indirectly affected through intermodal competition and market share shifts. When ocean rates spike due to chokepoint issues, air cargo demand increases, creating capacity constraints and rate inflation in air freight markets.

### Road Transport and Customs Processing


According to the 6th UN Global Survey on Digital and Sustainable Trade Facilitation conducted across 160 economies, the global average implementation rate of trade facilitation measures currently stands at 72%, up from 68.6% in 2023. The survey shows improvements across all regions since 2023, with developed economies leading with an 86% implementation rate.



**Customs Modernisation Impact:** 

In 2024, IMO will introduce a significant development in port infrastructure with the mandatory implementation of Maritime Electronic Single Windows. This mandate will have far-reaching implications, requiring enhanced interoperability and seamless coordination among port agencies. The Maritime Electronic Single Window aims to establish a robust digital framework to optimize port operations.



**Processing Time Changes:** Countries implementing ASYCUDA systems show measurable improvements in clearance times. 

For example, in Afghanistan, annual revenues jumped from $50 million in 2005 to $1 billion in 2018, as a result of the automation of customs procedures. And the Solomon Islands collected more than SBD$1 billion (US$126) in annual import taxes in 2017 for the first time in history three years after installing ASYCUDA.

## Open Questions

• **Alternative Fuel Cost Pass-Through:** How will the transition to alternative fuels impact freight rates across different trade routes, particularly for developing countries with limited refueling infrastructure?

• **ASYCUDA Modernisation Speed:** Which developing countries will prioritise customs digitisation upgrades and what will be the impact on processing times and compliance costs?

• **Chokepoint Infrastructure Investment:** What specific investments will be made to improve resilience at key maritime chokepoints and how will these costs affect transit charges?

• **Port Performance Standardisation:** How will UNCTAD's port performance metrics be standardised across different regions and what penalties or incentives will be implemented?

• **Climate Adaptation Financing:** 

With estimated adaptation costs in developing countries 10 to 18 times greater than current public adaptation finance flows (UNEP, 2023), how will port climate adaptation be funded? According to OECD, 2024, in 2022, total climate finance provided and mobilised by developed countries for developing countries amounted to US$ $115.9 billion. However, of this total, only US$ 32.4 billion, (28%) was for adaptation.



• **Trade Facilitation Implementation Gaps:** Which regions will show the greatest implementation gaps in trade facilitation measures and how will this affect competitive positioning?

fullBrief:
# International Carbon Action Partnership (ICAP) ETS Map

**Regulatory Fact Document** | April 2026
**Primary source:** [ICAP ETS Map Portal](https://icapcarbonaction.com/en/ets)

---

## What This Regulation Is and Why It Applies



The ICAP ETS Map provides and visualizes up-to-date information on ETSs around the world – including systems that are in force, under development and under consideration. The interactive map features downloadable factsheets and gives granular information on individual design aspects.

 The International Carbon Action Partnership (ICAP) operates as 

an international cooperative forum founded in 2007 by more than 15 government representatives. It brings together states and sub-national jurisdictions that have implemented or are planning to implement emissions trading systems (ETS).





The number of emissions trading systems (ETSs) in operation around the world continues to grow, reaching 38 in 2025, with a further 20 systems in various stages of development or consideration. This year's ICAP Emissions Trading Worldwide Status Report finds that the momentum for emissions trading is particularly strong in emerging economies adopting innovative system designs, including in Brazil, India, Indonesia, Türkiye, and Vietnam.



This comprehensive database matters critically to freight forwarders because carbon market pricing directly determines surcharge levels across international trade lanes. 

Carbon ETS covers more total emissions globally than any other carbon pricing system. The EU ETS, China ETS, California system, and South Korea K-ETS alone cover billions of tons of CO₂, including major power and industrial sectors.

 When carbon prices vary significantly between jurisdictions — EU ETS trading around €68-81/tCO₂ versus South Korea at ₩10,000-15,000 versus Singapore at S$25/tCO₂ — these differentials directly impact freight costs, client contract negotiations, and competitive positioning across trade routes.

**Source:** [ICAP Status Report 2025](https://icapcarbonaction.com/en/publications/emissions-trading-worldwide-icap-status-report-2025)

---

## Issues Requiring Immediate Action

### Carbon Price Exposure Mapping Across All Trade Lanes



Carbon prices across multiple emissions trading systems worldwide are expected to increase during the period of 2026 to 2030, compared to 2022 to 2026. The average EU ETS carbon price is expected to be 84.4 euros per metric ton of CO₂ during the period 2022 to 2025, but is projected to rise to almost 100 euros per metric ton of CO₂ during the period of 2026 to 2030, according to a survey of International Emissions Trading Association members.



**Action Required — Confirm for Your Business:** Finance teams must map current and projected carbon pricing exposure for each active trade lane. Include jurisdiction-specific carbon costs in rate modeling immediately to avoid margin erosion from unexpected surcharges.

### India Carbon Credit Trading Scheme Implementation



The Indian government is moving forward in establishing its carbon market, with the trading of first carbon credit certificates in the compliance market expected to happen in Oct. 2026, the market regulator told a conference Wednesday.

 

The CCTS compliance mechanism is set to initially cover over 700 million tonnes of CO2e, placing India among the world's largest emissions trading systems.



**Action Required — Confirm for Your Business:** Ocean and air product teams must assess client exposure to India-originating cargo. Factor emerging carbon costs into India trade lane pricing models before October 2026 launch.

### CBAM Impact from Origin-Country Carbon Pricing Differentials

Lower carbon prices at shipment origin increase CBAM costs at EU borders. 

Without this, Indian exports could suffer from carbon tariffs such as the EU CBAM.

 This creates complex cost calculations where freight forwarders must account for both origin carbon pricing and destination border adjustments.

**Action Required — Confirm for Your Business:** Customs and Europe teams must develop CBAM cost calculators that factor origin-country carbon pricing for accurate EU-bound rate calculations.

### China ETS Expansion Beyond Power Sector



Notably, China has started expansion beyond the power sector to the steel, cement, and aluminum smelter sectors, increasing the system's emissions coverage by an estimated 3 GtCO₂e, corresponding to approximately 5% of global GHG emissions.



**Action Required — Confirm for Your Business:** Procurement teams must evaluate supplier exposure in expanded Chinese sectors. Include projected carbon cost pass-through in China-origin manufacturing rate structures.

---

## Operational Impact by Transport Mode

### Ocean Freight

Current EU ETS coverage of maritime transport creates direct cost exposure for ocean carriers. 

Trading Economics Carbon Emissions Allowances Prices are sourced from the European Union Emissions Trading System (EU ETS), the world's largest cap and trade greenhouse gas emissions market.

 Ocean carriers pass through EU ETS compliance costs as Environmental Impact Fee (EIF) or similar surcharges, typically calculated per TEU or per ton based on fuel consumption and route distance.

**Cost Pass-Through Mechanism:** Ocean lines invoice ETS surcharges separately, typically ranging €15-45 per TEU for Asia-Europe routes based on current carbon prices. These costs flow directly to freight forwarder invoices and must be passed to end customers or absorbed as margin impact.

### Air Freight

Aviation's inclusion in EU ETS since 2012 creates established carbon cost mechanisms. Air carriers implement fuel surcharges and environmental fees that incorporate carbon pricing. The International Civil Aviation Organization's CORSIA offset requirements add another layer of carbon-related costs.

**Cost Pass-Through Mechanism:** Airlines incorporate carbon costs through fuel surcharges (typically per kg) and environmental levies. 

GEO's futures contracts follow the International Civil Aviation Organization's CORSIA standard. These carbon offsets from three major registries – Verra, the American Carbon Registry, and the Climate Action Reserve. Because it is based on high-quality carbon credits that adhere to the international aviation industry standard for emissions offsetting.



### Road Transport

EU ETS 2 launching in 2027 will capture road transport fuels, creating new carbon costs for trucking operations. 

Then, the second emission trading scheme covering the road transport and buildings sectors should start operating in 2027, with uncertainties as to the value at which ETS2 allowances will be auctioned and traded.



**Cost Pass-Through Mechanism:** Road carriers will implement carbon surcharges based on fuel consumption and distance. Expect separate line items for carbon compliance costs on trucking invoices starting 2027.

### Customs and Reporting

CBAM implementation creates complex customs processes requiring carbon content declarations and origin-country carbon pricing verification. This impacts document flow, customs clearance times, and creates new compliance requirements.

**Cost Pass-Through Mechanism:** Additional customs handling fees for CBAM documentation processing, typically €50-150 per shipment depending on commodity complexity.

---

---

## Recommended Actions

| Priority | Action | Owner | Timeframe |

| 1 | Map carbon pricing exposure across all active trade lanes | Finance + Strategy | 30 days |
| 2 | Develop dynamic carbon cost calculators for rate modeling | IT + Ocean Product + Air Product | 60 days |
| 3 | Create CBAM cost calculation tools for EU-bound cargo | Customs + Finance | 45 days |
| 4 | Establish supplier carbon cost tracking for China procurement | Procurement + Sustainability | 90 days |
| 5 | Build India CCTS cost forecasting for October 2026 launch | Ocean Product + Finance | 120 days |
| 6 | Develop client communication templates for carbon cost pass-through | Sales + Legal | 30 days |
| 7 | Create automated carbon pricing data feed from ICAP price explorer | IT + Sustainability | 45 days |
| 8 | Establish carbon cost escalation clauses in client contracts | Legal + Sales | 60 days |

---

## Implementation Timeline

| March 2026 | CBAM reporting requirements in force | In force |

| January 2027 | CBAM financial obligations begin | Upcoming |

| 2026-2030 | EU ETS price projected to reach €100/tCO₂ | Upcoming |
| By 2030 | Singapore carbon tax increases to S$50-80/tCO₂ | Upcoming |
| 2030+ | India CCTS becomes world's largest carbon market | Projected |

---

## Open Questions

• **EU ETS 2 Allowance Pricing:** 

Then, the second emission trading scheme covering the road transport and buildings sectors should start operating in 2027, with uncertainties as to the value at which ETS2 allowances will be auctioned and traded. Although ongoing trilogue discussions are even considering postponing the ETS2 start to 2028, this analysis takes into account the objectives as they are currently enshrined.



• **India CCTS Price Formation:** Initial carbon credit certificate pricing mechanism and benchmark price levels for the October 2026 launch remain undefined. Market expects intensity-based system with modest initial targets, but specific pricing is pending regulatory guidance.

• **China ETS Expansion Timeline:** Specific dates and sectoral coverage details for steel, cement, and aluminum sectors beyond power sector inclusion await State Council approval.

• **CBAM Default Values:** European Commission still developing default carbon content values for countries without adequate carbon pricing systems, affecting CBAM cost calculations.

• **Brazil Carbon Market Development:** 

This year's ICAP Emissions Trading Worldwide Status Report finds that the momentum for emissions trading is particularly strong in emerging economies adopting innovative system designs, including in Brazil, India, Indonesia, Türkiye, and Vietnam.

 Implementation timeline and design details pending legislative approval.

For freight forwarders: (1) clients reporting under CSRD must classify their transport spend as Taxonomy-aligned or not, (2) forwarders offering low-carbon services (EV fleets, modal shift, ISO 14083 reporting) help clients increase their Taxonomy-aligned OpEx, (3) Taxonomy alignment is increasingly used in tender scoring — preferred suppliers demonstrate green credentials, (4) access to green finance and sustainability-linked loans requires Taxonomy-aligned activities.

fullBrief:
# EU Taxonomy for Sustainable Activities

**Regulatory Fact Document** | April 2026
**Primary source:** [Regulation (EU) 2020/852 on the establishment of a framework to facilitate sustainable investment](https://eur-lex.europa.eu/legal-content/EN/TXT/?uri=celex:32020R0852)

---

## What This Regulation Is and Why It Applies



Regulation (EU) 2020/852 of the European Parliament and of the Council of 18 June 2020 on the establishment of a framework to facilitate sustainable investment entered into force on 22 June 2020 and became applicable on 12 July 2020

. 

The Taxonomy Regulation establishes the basis for the EU taxonomy by setting out the 4 overarching conditions that an economic activity has to meet in order to qualify as environmentally sustainable

.



The EU Taxonomy is a classification system that establishes a list of environmentally sustainable economic activities using scientific criteria to create a 'green list' of economic activities by identifying and defining those that qualify as environmentally sustainable

. 

The Taxonomy Regulation establishes six climate and environmental objectives

 and provides that economic activities can be included within the Taxonomy as "environmentally sustainable" if they: make a substantial contribution to one or more environmental objectives; do no significant harm to any other environmental objectives; are carried on in compliance with certain safeguards; and comply with technical screening criteria set out in delegated legislation.

For freight forwarders, this regulation creates a new competitive landscape where Taxonomy alignment becomes a critical differentiator in client relationships and access to green finance. 

Companies subject to non-financial reporting obligations must include in their sustainability statements information on how and to what extent their activities are associated with economic activities that qualify as environmentally sustainable

. This means freight forwarders' clients reporting under the Corporate Sustainability Reporting Directive (CSRD) must classify their transport spend as Taxonomy-aligned or not, creating demand for services that help clients meet their Taxonomy alignment targets.



Investors and lenders are increasingly relying on the EU Taxonomy when assessing whether an investment is environmentally sustainable, and companies not meeting the Taxonomy requirements may face increasing difficulty in accessing new capital and financing

. For freight forwarders, offering Taxonomy-aligned services — including electric vehicle fleets, modal shift to rail or water transport, and ISO 14083-compliant emissions reporting — directly impacts their ability to win tenders and maintain client relationships as sustainability criteria become weighted more heavily in procurement decisions.

**Source:** Articles 3, 8, 9 of Regulation (EU) 2020/852; Delegated Regulation (EU) 2021/2139 (Climate Delegated Act)

---

## Issues Requiring Immediate Action

### Service Portfolio Classification and Client Communication



Under Article 8 of the Taxonomy Regulation, non-financial and financial undertakings are required to disclose alignment of their business activities (turnover, CapEx, OpEx) with sustainable economic activities as defined in the Taxonomy

. Freight forwarders must immediately assess which of their services qualify as Taxonomy-aligned and communicate this to clients for their CSRD reporting.

**Action Required — Confirm for Your Business:** Map your current service offerings against transport technical screening criteria. Identify which services qualify as Taxonomy-aligned (EV last-mile delivery, rail freight, short sea shipping with zero-emission vessels, modal shift consulting) versus those that do not qualify.

### Maritime Transport Technical Screening Compliance



The technical screening criteria require vessels to have zero direct (tailpipe) CO2 emissions as a starting point for all maritime transport activities

. 

However, vessels used for passenger water transport and freight water transport may still qualify if the activity is a transitional activity, provided it complies with remaining technical screening criteria

. 

A buffer period applies until 2025 to allow a certain amount of direct emissions, and vessels generally meet criteria if they derive a minimum of 25% of energy from zero direct emission fuels

.

**Action Required — Confirm for Your Business:** Audit your ocean freight carrier partners to determine which vessels and routes qualify as Taxonomy-aligned. Document the fuel types, emission levels, and transitional activity status of vessels used in your supply chains.

### Aviation Sustainable Fuel Requirements



For aviation, sustainable aviation fuel (SAF) share is calculated by dividing the total quantity of SAF purchased at fleet level by the total amount of aviation fuel used by the compliant aircraft, then multiplied by one hundred

. 

Aircraft used for private or commercial business aviation would be excluded unless the relevant aircraft had zero direct (tailpipe) CO2 emissions

.

**Action Required — Confirm for Your Business:** Engage with airline partners to obtain SAF usage data and calculate the Taxonomy alignment percentage of air freight services offered to clients.

### Data Collection System Implementation for CSRD Reporting Chain



CSRD reporting is expected to be subject to assurance requirements (initially limited assurance) — meaning data must be traceable, defensible, and stored with proper controls

. 

Companies will need to complete double materiality assessments, gap analyses, and implement quantitative data collection mechanisms by the end of 2026 in alignment with the new European Sustainability Reporting Standards (ESRS) and in an auditable manner

.

**Action Required — Confirm for Your Business:** Implement systems to track and report Taxonomy alignment data to clients. Ensure data quality meets limited assurance requirements, with documented methodologies and supporting evidence for all Taxonomy alignment claims.

---

## Operational Impact by Transport Mode

### Ocean Freight



The Climate Delegated Act Amendment impacts which shipping activities can be classified as "environmentally sustainable" and includes amendments to the shipping technical screening criteria

. 

Additional options for vessel qualification apply from 1 January 2026 and there is no time limit on their application

.

For ocean freight forwarders, the key operational impacts include:
- Carrier selection must prioritize Taxonomy-aligned vessels where available
- Service pricing should differentiate between Taxonomy-aligned and non-aligned options
- Client reporting must clearly segregate shipment volumes and costs by Taxonomy alignment status
- Contract negotiations must include Taxonomy alignment guarantees and associated surcharge mechanisms



Vessels dedicated to the transport of fossil fuels may never be considered to substantially contribute to environmental objectives

, creating absolute exclusions for certain trade lanes and cargo types.

### Air Freight



The European Commission recognizes that the aviation industry has the potential to play a significant role in reducing carbon emissions but at present the ability of the industry to make such progress is technologically constrained

. The technical screening criteria focus on sustainable aviation fuel usage and noise compliance.

Air freight forwarders must:
- Obtain SAF usage certificates from airline partners for Taxonomy alignment calculations
- Pass through SAF surcharges with transparent methodology to clients
- Document noise level compliance for aircraft used in their operations
- Exclude private aviation and business jet services from Taxonomy alignment claims unless zero-emission aircraft are used

### Road Transport

The Taxonomy includes technical screening criteria for road transport focusing on electric vehicles and alternative fuel vehicles. Road freight forwarders operating delivery fleets must:
- Prioritize electric vehicle deployment for last-mile delivery services
- Calculate and report the percentage of delivery volume completed by zero-emission vehicles
- Implement surcharge mechanisms to recover the higher costs of EV operations
- Provide clients with detailed Taxonomy alignment reporting for Scope 3 emissions calculations

### Customs and Reporting



EU Taxonomy reporting entities are now able to use new templates and apply the materiality threshold if they choose, though they also have the option to report using original templates

. 

A new materiality principle allows activities that cumulatively make up less than 10% of turnover, CapEx or OpEx to be excluded from KPI calculations as not financially material

.

Customs and trade compliance teams must:
- Classify shipments by Taxonomy alignment status for statistical reporting
- Maintain documentation supporting Taxonomy alignment claims for audit purposes
- Implement materiality thresholds in client reporting systems
- Ensure alignment between trade data and sustainability reporting requirements

---

## Open Questions

• **Technical screening criteria updates:** 

The European Commission highlighted that it will be necessary to further assess maritime shipping and establish technical screening criteria for maritime shipping applicable as of 2026 with reference to the status of zero emission vessels

 — specific criteria for 2026 onwards not yet published

• **Sector-specific transport criteria:** The extent to which freight forwarding as a service activity (versus asset ownership) will be subject to specific technical screening criteria remains unclear

• **Intermodal transport classification:** How to classify and report Taxonomy alignment for multimodal shipments involving both aligned and non-aligned transport modes requires further guidance

• **Third country equivalence:** 

The European Commission has committed to "a systematic and thorough review of the reporting requirements and all the technical screening criteria, in particular of all the DNSH criteria, with the aim of assessing ways to make them simpler, more usable and more aligned with EU legislation"

 — timeline and scope not specified

 — impact on freight forwarders operating below these thresholds unclear

fullBrief:
# GRI Standards 

**Regulatory Fact Document** | April 2026
**Primary source:** [GRI Standards](https://www.globalreporting.org/standards/)

---

## What This Regulation Is and Why It Applies

The GRI (Global Reporting Initiative) Standards are 

the world's most widely used sustainability reporting framework, representing global best practice for reporting publicly on a range of economic, environmental and social impacts

. 

The revised Universal Standards were published in October 2021 and came into effect for reporting on 1 January 2023

. 

The GRI Standards is a modular system of interconnected standards

 consisting of 

three series of Standards: the GRI Universal Standards, which apply to all organizations; the GRI Sector Standards, applicable to specific sectors; and the GRI Topic Standards, each listing disclosures relevant to a particular topic

.

While voluntary in nature, the GRI Standards have become the de facto global standard for ESG reporting, with organizations worldwide adopting them to demonstrate transparency and accountability. 

The GRI Standards are regularly reviewed to ensure they reflect global best practices for sustainability reporting, helping thousands of organizations around the world respond to emerging information demands from stakeholders and regulators

. Crucially for freight forwarders, 

given GRI and EFRAG's continued collaboration, GRI reporters can be assured they will be best prepared to meet future reporting rules, as further EU standards are developed

, particularly the EU Corporate Sustainability Reporting Directive (CSRD).

For international freight forwarding operations, GRI Standards matter because they directly govern how clients report their Scope 3 emissions from purchased transport services. GRI 305: Emissions is the specific standard requiring organizations to disclose their greenhouse gas emissions across all three scopes. 

Disclosure 305-3 includes Scope 3 emissions that encompass upstream and downstream sources not owned or directly controlled by the organization

, which includes freight transport services purchased from forwarders. This creates a direct data flow requirement: freight forwarders must provide emissions data to clients in GRI-compatible formats.

**Source:** GRI 305: Emissions 2016, Universal Standards 2021

---

## Issues Requiring Immediate Action

### ### Client Data Format Standardization



GRI 305-3 requires gross other indirect (Scope 3) GHG emissions in metric tons of CO2 equivalent

, with specific reporting requirements including 

gases included in the calculation; whether CO2, CH4, N2O, HFCs, PFCs, SF6, NF3, or all

 and 

source of the emission factors and the global warming potential (GWP) rates used, or a reference to the GWP source

. Freight forwarders must ensure their emissions calculation outputs meet these exact specifications.

**Action Required — Confirm for Your Business:** Verify that your current emissions data output includes all required GRI 305-3 elements: CO2e totals, gas breakdown, emission factor sources, and GWP rate references.

### ### CSRD-GRI Interoperability Preparation



A new GRI-ESRS Linkage Service launched in June, to provide advice and feedback to companies on how to align sustainability reports based on the GRI Standards with the ESRS requirements

. 

Companies that already disclose Scope 1, 2 and 3 GHG emissions using the GRI Standards will be well positioned to report information about GHG emissions in accordance with IFRS S2

. This interoperability means freight forwarders providing GRI-compatible data can serve both GRI and CSRD reporting requirements.

**Action Required — Confirm for Your Business:** Ensure your data format can support both GRI 305-3 and ESRS E1 requirements simultaneously for clients reporting under multiple frameworks.

### ### Transport Sector Standard Development



In 2024, the potential to expand the number of Sector Standards developed simultaneously will be assessed. This may result in the commencement of standards for Priority Group 2: Industrial sectors

. While a specific GRI Transport Sector Standard is under development (as referenced in the source material), no definitive publication date has been confirmed. Freight forwarders must monitor this development as it could introduce transport-specific disclosure requirements beyond the current Topic Standards.

**Action Required — Confirm for Your Business:** Monitor GRI's Standards development pipeline for transport sector-specific requirements that could affect your data reporting obligations.

### ### Updated Climate and Energy Standards Integration



Note: Requirement 1.2 and Disclosures 305-1 to 305-5 have been superseded by GRI 102: Climate Change 2025

 and 

GRI 103: Energy 2025, as of 1 January 2027

. These revisions will introduce new reporting requirements that affect how transport emissions are calculated and disclosed.

**Action Required — Confirm for Your Business:** Prepare for transition to updated GRI climate and energy standards effective January 2027, including potential changes to emission calculation methodologies.

---

## Operational Impact by Transport Mode

### ### Ocean Freight

GRI 305-3 requires Scope 3 emissions reporting for all purchased transport services. For ocean freight, this means clients need vessel-specific emissions data including fuel type, consumption, distance, and cargo allocation methodologies. 

The requirements for GHG emissions in this Standard are based on the requirements of the 'GHG Protocol Corporate Accounting and Reporting Standard' and the 'GHG Protocol Corporate Value Chain (Scope 3) Accounting and Reporting Standard'

. Ocean forwarders must provide data showing allocation methods (TEU-km, mass-distance, or revenue-based) and emission factors used.

Cost pass-through: Clients increasingly require GRI-compliant emissions reporting as part of their procurement processes. Ocean forwarders unable to provide compliant data face competitive disadvantage and potential contract loss.

### ### Air Freight

Air transport generates the highest per-kilogram emissions in freight, making accurate GRI 305-3 reporting critical for client compliance. 

Standards, methodologies, assumptions, and/or calculation tools used

 must be disclosed, requiring air forwarders to document their calculation approaches, emission factors, and data sources. This includes aircraft type, fuel burn rates, load factors, and distance calculations.

Cost pass-through: Premium air freight clients expect detailed emissions breakdowns for sustainability reporting. Non-compliance with GRI format requirements may result in lost business or required data verification costs passed to clients.

### ### Road Transport

Road freight emissions under GRI 305-3 require detailed vehicle type, fuel consumption, load efficiency, and route optimization data. 

Transportation of materials, products, waste, workers, and passengers: these emissions result from the combustion of fuels in mobile combustion sources owned or controlled by the organization, such as trucks, trains, ships, airplanes, buses, and cars

. Road forwarders must provide truck-specific data including vehicle class, fuel type, empty running percentages, and hub-to-destination breakdowns.

### ### Customs and Reporting Functions

GRI compliance affects customs operations through documentation requirements. 

Other indirect (Scope 3) GHG emissions categories and activities included in the calculation

 must be specified, requiring customs teams to maintain transport mode classifications and emission category mappings for each shipment. This creates additional data management requirements for customs documentation systems.

---

| Biodiversity standard update | 1 January 2026 | GRI 101: Biodiversity 2024 |
| Global adoption | 10,000+ organizations | Standard usage data |
| Geographic coverage | 100+ countries | Global implementation |

---

## Compliance Risk Register

| Risk | Severity | Likelihood | Deadline |
|---|---|---|---|

| Missing emission factor sources | High | Medium | Ongoing |
| Transport standard updates | Medium | Medium | TBD 2024-2025 |
| Climate standard transition | Medium | High | January 2027 |

---

## Recommended Actions

| Priority | Action | Owner | Timeframe |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Audit current emissions data output format against GRI 305-3 requirements | Sustainability | 30 days |
| 2 | Implement GWP rate source documentation in all emission calculations | Sustainability | 60 days |
| 3 | Develop CSRD-GRI dual-compliance data format | IT, Sustainability | Q2 2026 |
| 4 | Establish GRI transport standard monitoring process | Sustainability | 30 days |
| 5 | Create client education materials on GRI compliance benefits | Sales, Sustainability | 90 days |
| 6 | Update RFP response templates with GRI compliance statements | Sales | 60 days |
| 7 | Train customer service teams on GRI data requirements | Operations | 90 days |
| 8 | Implement automated GRI format validation in reporting systems | IT | Q3 2026 |

---

## Implementation Timeline

| Date | Milestone | Status |
|---|---|---|
| January 1, 2023 | GRI Universal Standards 2021 in effect | In force |
| June 2024 | GRI-ESRS Linkage Service launched | In force |
| January 1, 2026 | GRI 101: Biodiversity 2024 effective | Upcoming |
| TBD 2024-2025 | Transport Sector Standard publication | Pending |
| January 1, 2027 | GRI 102: Climate Change 2025 effective | Upcoming |

---

## Open Questions

- Publication date and specific requirements for GRI Transport Sector Standard — depends on GSSB work program prioritization and public consultation process
- Detailed interoperability requirements between GRI 305-3 and ESRS E1 transport emissions — depends on finalization of ESRS implementation guidance
- Specific changes to emission calculation methodologies in GRI 102: Climate Change 2025 — depends on final standard publication
- Integration requirements between GRI biodiversity reporting and transport impact disclosure — depends on GRI 101 implementation guidance
- Cost allocation methodologies for multi-modal transport chains under updated climate standards — depends on revised GRI guidance documents

Increasingly referenced in EU regulation (CSRD/ESRS, CountEmissions EU) and customer contractual requirements.

For freight forwarders: (1) clients include ISO 14083 compliance in RFP scoring criteria, (2) CSRD requires ISO 14083-aligned Scope 3 reporting, (3) the EU CountEmissions Regulation will make ISO 14083 the legally referenced methodology, (4) forwarders offering compliant calculations have a competitive advantage in tenders, (5) methodology requires carrier-specific emissions factors — not just industry averages.

Adopted by major shippers and logistics companies as the basis for supply chain emissions accounting.
whyMatters: GLEC Framework is the practical 'how-to' that translates ISO 14083 into operational reporting workflows. For freight forwarders: (1) most large customer carbon reporting requests reference GLEC Framework, (2) provides calculation methodologies for each transport mode with default emission factors where carrier data is unavailable, (3) enables consistent, auditable emissions reporting across multimodal supply chains, (4) competitive advantage for forwarders offering GLEC-aligned reporting in tenders.

whatIsIt: The GHG Protocol Corporate Value Chain (Scope 3) Standard provides the globally accepted methodology for measuring and reporting indirect value chain emissions. Published by WRI and WBCSD. Scope 3 Category 4 (Upstream Transport) and Category 9 (Downstream Transport) are the specific categories that cover freight forwarding emissions. Referenced by CSRD, ISSB/IFRS S2, SBTi, CDP, and virtually every corporate sustainability framework.
whyMatters: The GHG Protocol is WHY your clients ask for transport emissions data. Every sustainability questionnaire, CDP disclosure, and SBTi target your customers submit uses GHG Protocol as the accounting basis. For freight forwarders: (1) you are in your clients' Scope 3 — they need your emissions data to report, (2) inability to provide compliant data risks losing preferred-supplier status, (3) GHG Protocol requires calculation methodology aligned with ISO 14083 / GLEC Framework, (4) Scope 3 data requests are growing 30%+ annually as CSRD and ISSB expand.

Freight categories: Category 4 (Upstream Transport), Category 9 (Downstream Transport)
