fullBrief:
# GHG Protocol Corporate Value Chain (Scope 3) Accounting and Reporting Standard

**Regulatory Fact Document** | April 2026
**Primary source:** [Corporate Value Chain (Scope 3) Accounting and Reporting Standard](https://ghgprotocol.org/sites/default/files/standards/Corporate-Value-Chain-Accounting-Reporing-Standard_041613_2.pdf)

---

## What This Regulation Is and Why It Applies

The GHG Protocol Corporate Value Chain (Scope 3) Accounting and Reporting Standard is 

the only internationally accepted method for companies to account for these types of value chain emissions

. 

Released in 2011

, this standard provides the globally accepted methodology for measuring and reporting indirect emissions from a company's value chain activities that occur outside its direct operational control.



Over the past 13 years since publication, the GHG Protocol Scope 3 Standard and associated categorization of emissions has become widely accepted as the basis for accounting and reporting of corporate value chain emissions by thousands of companies and various voluntary climate programs and mandatory disclosure frameworks, including climate risk-disclosure standards developed by financial accounting standard-setters and regulators. As the most widely used internationally accepted standard for organizations to quantify their upstream and downstream emissions, the Scope 3 Standard has become the backbone of sector-specific implementation guidance and tools to drive more consistent corporate measurement of value chain GHG emissions across multiple sectors developed by industry associations and other non-governmental organizations.



For freight forwarders, this standard directly defines their operational relationship with clients' sustainability reporting. 

Scope 3 Category 4, as defined by the Greenhouse Gas Protocol, encompasses emissions from the transportation and distribution of products purchased by a company within the reporting year from its tier 1 suppliers to its operations, excluding those owned or operated by the company. This includes emissions from third-party logistics and distribution services, covering both inbound and outbound logistics, as well as transportation between the company's facilities.

 Freight forwarders appear in their clients' Scope 3 inventories under Category 4 (Upstream Transportation) and Category 9 (Downstream Transportation), making emissions data provision essential to client relationships and preferred supplier status.

**Source:** Corporate Value Chain (Scope 3) Standard sections 1.1-1.3, 5.1-5.3

---

## Issues Requiring Immediate Action

### Client Scope 3 Data Requests Now Standard Business Practice



A growing number of companies are participating in these kinds of initiatives, such as CDP (formerly known as the Carbon Disclosure Project) and the Science Based Targets initiative (SBTi), all of which use GHG Protocol accounting standards to track and report progress. In 2022, more than 18,700 companies submitted climate disclosures via CDP, 42% more than in 2021 and over 233% more than when the Paris Agreement was signed in 2015.

 Every sustainability questionnaire, CDP disclosure, and SBTi target submission requires GHG Protocol-compliant emissions data from freight providers.

**Action Required — Confirm for Your Business:** Audit which clients are already requesting Scope 3 data, what methodology they require, and whether your current reporting meets GHG Protocol Category 4/9 requirements. Map client Scope 3 reporting cycles to ensure proactive data delivery.

### Mandatory Disclosure Frameworks Reference GHG Protocol Exclusively



A key aspect of IFRS S2 is the requirement for companies to measure their greenhouse gas emissions in accordance with GHG Protocol, unless it is otherwise specified by a jurisdictional authority or stock exchange. The European Commission passed the Corporate Sustainability Reporting Directive in 2023 which will require organizations to disclose scope 1, 2 and 3 emissions following GHG Protocol standards.

 

Currently, at least 31 countries have committed to adopting IFRS S2, which includes Scope 3 reporting requirements. Many regions, including the EU's CSRD and California's Climate Disclosure Laws, already require Scope 3 disclosures.



**Action Required — Confirm for Your Business:** Verify that your emissions calculation methodology aligns with GHG Protocol Category 4 and Category 9 specifications. Ensure emission factors and calculation methods comply with the fuel-based, distance-based, or spend-based methodologies outlined in the Technical Guidance.

### Scope 3 Category Definition Determines Invoice Cost Allocation



Since the gate represents "the point of receipt by the reporting company (excluding emissions from sources that are owned or controlled by the reporting company)," the reporting company must consider which party is paying to transport the purchased goods to its location. If the supplier pays to have purchased goods shipped to the reporting company, the gate would be at the buyer's location and the inbound transportation GHG emissions would fall within Category 1 for the reporting company. Conversely, if the reporting company pays for the shipping, the gate would be at the shipping point and the inbound transportation GHG emissions would be reported in Category 4 rather than Category 1.



**Action Required — Confirm for Your Business:** Review freight terms (FOB, CIF, DDP, etc.) in client contracts to determine correct Scope 3 category allocation. Ensure emissions data is provided under the category where the client has financial control of the transportation service.

### Supplier-Specific Data Requirements Increasing



If possible, companies should collect energy or emissions data from suppliers and other value chain partners to obtain site-specific data for priority scope 3 categories and activities. To do so, companies should identify relevant suppliers from which to seek GHG data. In general, companies should seek activity data or emissions data from suppliers that are as specific as possible to the product purchased from the supplier, following the hierarchy in table IV.



**Action Required — Confirm for Your Business:** Develop capability to provide supplier-specific emissions data rather than industry-average data. This includes actual fuel consumption, route-specific distances, and vessel/aircraft/vehicle efficiency data rather than generic emission factors.

---

## Operational Impact by Transport Mode

### Ocean Transport



The fuel-based method should be used when companies can obtain data for fuel use from transport providers (and, if applicable, refrigerant leakage due to refrigeration of products) from vehicle fleets (e.g., trucks, trains, planes, vessels). Companies should also take into account any additional energy used and account for fugitive emissions (e.g., refrigerant loss or air-conditioning).



Ocean transport emissions calculation requires:
- **Fuel consumption data:** Actual bunker fuel used per voyage/container
- **Cargo allocation:** TEU utilization and weight distribution across shipments  
- **Route specificity:** Actual nautical miles vs. standard trade lane distances
- **Reefer emissions:** Separate accounting for refrigerated container energy consumption
- **Allocation methodology:** Mass, volume, or revenue-based allocation of vessel emissions to individual shipments

**Cost pass-through mechanism:** Ocean carriers must provide GHG intensity factors (gCO₂e/TEU-km or gCO₂e/t-km) that can be multiplied by actual shipment distances. This creates pressure for fuel-efficient vessels and optimal routing, potentially affecting vessel selection and rate negotiations.

### Air Transport



Transportation: Refers to the movement of goods from the point of origin to the company's premises. This can include shipping via air, sea, rail, or road.

 Air freight presents the highest emission intensity per ton-kilometer, making accurate calculation critical for client Scope 3 reporting.

Air transport calculation requirements:
- **Fuel-based method preferred:** Actual aviation fuel consumption per flight segment
- **Distance precision:** Great circle distances with routing factor adjustments
- **Freight allocation:** Separation of passenger vs. freight allocation on combination aircraft
- **RFI factor application:** Radiative Forcing Index multiplier for high-altitude emissions (typically 2.0x)
- **Hub accounting:** Emissions allocation across multi-leg routing through consolidation hubs

**Cost pass-through mechanism:** Air carriers increasingly provide shipment-level CO₂e data. This transparency creates competitive pressure for fuel-efficient aircraft and direct routing, potentially affecting carrier selection and premium pricing for low-emission services.

### Road Transport



Transportation emissions are directly influenced by mode (air, road, sea, rail), distance, load efficiency, and fuel type.

 Road transport offers the most granular data availability but highest complexity for multi-stop consolidation routing.

Road transport calculation requirements:
- **Vehicle-specific emission factors:** Euro 6, electric, biofuel, and hydrogen vehicle classifications
- **Load factor optimization:** Actual vs. theoretical capacity utilization
- **Backhaul allocation:** Empty return journey emission allocation
- **Multi-stop routing:** Proportional distance allocation across consolidated loads
- **Cold chain accounting:** Additional emissions from refrigerated transport

**Cost pass-through mechanism:** Road carriers can provide precise fuel consumption data and route optimization metrics. This creates opportunities for load consolidation incentives and green fleet surcharges, directly linking sustainability performance to freight rates.

### Customs and Reporting Integration



New EU regulations may require logistics companies to report embedded emissions to clients, making proactive supplier engagement crucial.

 Customs and trade documentation must increasingly include emissions data for regulatory compliance.

Integration requirements:
- **Trade lane emission factors:** Standard gCO₂e/t-km factors by origin-destination pairs
- **Customs documentation:** CO₂e data inclusion in commercial invoices and customs declarations
- **Supply chain traceability:** Connection between Scope 3 data and customs compliance systems
- **Multi-modal calculations:** Seamless emission accounting across ocean-road-air segments

fullBrief:
# Science Based Targets Initiative (SBTi) Framework

**Regulatory Fact Document** | April 2026
**Primary source:** [Science Based Targets Initiative](https://sciencebasedtargets.org/)

---

## What This Regulation Is and Why It Applies



The Science Based Targets initiative (SBTi) is a corporate climate action organization that enables companies and financial institutions worldwide to play their part in combating the climate crisis. We develop standards, tools and guidance which allow companies to set greenhouse gas (GHG) emissions reductions targets in line with what is needed to keep global heating below catastrophic levels and reach net-zero by 2050 at latest.

 

In January 2026, the Science Based Targets initiative (SBTi) surpassed 10,000 companies with validated science-based targets.



While SBTi is a voluntary framework rather than mandatory regulation, it has become a de facto commercial requirement for freight forwarders serving large corporate clients. 

Every major investment firm, from BlackRock to State Street, now asks portfolio companies the same question: Do you have science-based climate targets? A "no" increasingly signals that your company either doesn't take climate seriously or doesn't understand how serious investors now are about it.

 

In 2026, SBTi (Science-Based Targets initiative) certification is moving from "nice to have" to baseline expectation for any enterprise claiming genuine sustainability commitment.



For freight forwarders, SBTi alignment directly impacts business relationships. 

Case Studies Supplier Engagement Case Study - H&M Group H&M Group is a global fashion and design company, present in 79 markets worldwide. Read on to discover more about H&M Group's steps to decarbonize its supply chain and how the company supports its suppliers to reduce emissions.

 Major shippers with SBTi commitments are increasingly requiring their supply chain partners — including logistics service providers — to demonstrate aligned emissions reduction trajectories. This creates immediate procurement pressure where SBTi-validated forwarders win preferred-supplier status in sustainability-scored tenders, while non-aligned providers face exclusion from shortlists for major corporate accounts.

**Source:** [SBTi About Us](https://sciencebasedtargets.org/about-us), [Corporate Net-Zero Standard](https://sciencebasedtargets.org/net-zero)

---

## Issues Requiring Immediate Action

### Near-Term Target Development for Scope 1 and 2 Emissions



Near-term: Define the emissions reductions a company must achieve over the next 5–10 years.

 

These are typically 5- or 10-year targets that show near-term decarbonization progress. Examples: "Reduce absolute Scope 1 and 2 emissions by 42% by 2030 (vs. 2020 baseline)" "Reduce intensity-based Scope 3 emissions 25% by 2030" Near-term targets are mandatory for SBTi recognition. They must be ambitious (typically 40–50% reduction in Scopes 1+2) and science-aligned for a 1.5°C pathway.



**Action Required — Confirm for Your Business:** Assess whether your current Scope 1 (owned fleet fuel consumption) and Scope 2 (facility electricity) emissions measurement is adequate for SBTi baseline establishment.

### Scope 3 Category 4/9 Transport Services Coverage



For the transport sector, it offers guidelines and calculation tools for the most used transport modes (road, rail, maritime, aviation) to determine which GHG emissions reductions need to be reached in order to align with the 1.5°C objective of the Paris Agreement.

 Freight forwarders face dual exposure: Category 4 (purchased transport services from carriers) and Category 9 (downstream transport services sold to clients).

**Action Required — Confirm for Your Business:** Determine whether your client portfolio composition triggers mandatory Scope 3 coverage thresholds under SBTi's materiality requirements.

### Maritime Sector-Specific Net-Zero by 2040



According to UMAS, the maritime industry must scale up climate action and cut its CO2 emissions 45% by 2030 and reach net-zero by 2040 to get on track and meet the global climate goals.

 

For the maritime sector, a long-term target means reducing emissions to a residual level in line with 1.5°C by no later than 2040. This is in line with sector-specific recommendations from science and academia.



**Action Required — Confirm for Your Business:** Evaluate whether your ocean freight volumes justify using SBTi Maritime Transport Guidance versus cross-sector methodologies.

### Public Commitment Within 24 Months



Commitments are formal expressions of intent to develop near-term or net-zero targets and submit these for validation within 24 months of the date of submission. Organizations that fail to submit targets within 24 months of making a commitment are identified with 'Commitment Removed' values in the dashboard.



**Action Required — Confirm for Your Business:** Decide whether to pursue public SBTi commitment now or maintain voluntary alignment without formal validation timeline.

### Five-Year Target Review Cycle



The review process begins on the trigger date–at the end of the month, five years after the initial targets' validation publication or the most recent update of all scope 1, 2, and 3 targets. From that date, companies have six months to complete the target review and submit evidence to SBTi Services. If an update is needed, revised targets must be submitted for validation within 12 months of the trigger date.



**Action Required — Confirm for Your Business:** Plan for ongoing target maintenance and review processes if pursuing SBTi validation.

### Corporate Net-Zero Standard V2 Implementation



These will inform the Standard which will be published for companies to use to set targets in 2026. These will inform the Standard which will be published for companies to use to set targets in 2026.

 The updated standard will be published in 2026 with new requirements.

**Action Required — Confirm for Your Business:** Monitor V2 publication for changes to net-zero target requirements that may affect your target-setting approach.

---

## Operational Impact by Transport Mode

### Ocean Freight Operations



The Science Based Target Setting Guidance for the Maritime Transport Sector is the world's first framework for companies in the maritime sector to set near- and long-term science-based targets in line with 1.5°C. It provides a detailed understanding on how to set targets and account for greenhouse gas emissions taking into account the unique context of the sector, as well as an explanation of how science-based targets are indeed feasible for the maritime industry.



Ocean freight forwarders can use the SBTi Maritime Guidance to set both Scope 3 Category 4 targets (for purchased ocean services from carriers) and Category 9 targets (for ocean services sold to clients). This requires ISO 14083-aligned emissions calculation methodology for accurate vessel-specific emissions allocation.

**Cost Pass-Through Mechanism:** SBTi alignment may justify sustainability surcharges on ocean bookings, particularly where validated targets enable premium positioning in client tenders. Climate-conscious shippers increasingly accept higher costs from SBTi-validated service providers.

### Air Freight Operations



The Guidance and Target Setting Tool is for passenger and cargo airlines, as well as companies that use aviation services.

 Air freight forwarders can leverage SBTi Aviation Guidance for both purchased air services (Category 4) and sold air services (Category 9).

**Surcharge Implications:** Aviation's high emissions intensity may require more aggressive decarbonization measures to meet SBTi ambition levels, potentially driving differential surcharges for air versus ocean modal choices.

### Road Freight and Last-Mile



In March 2024 the SBTi published their Land Transport Science-Based Target-Setting Guidance ('Land Transport Guidance'). This guidance sets out the SBTi's current target-setting criteria and recommendations for the land transport sector i.e. companies dedicated to passenger and freight transport activities via road and rail.





Land transport companies can set targets for their scopes 1 and 2 emissions using SBTi's cross-sector methodologies and Corporate Near-Term Criteria. Companies in any sector with large land transport emissions in their value chain can use the Sectoral Decarbonization Approach (SDA) Transport Tool to set scope 3 targets covering their subcontracted transport emissions.



### Customs and Reporting Functions

SBTi requires robust emissions data governance and verification processes. Customs documentation must support accurate emissions allocation across shipments, particularly for Scope 3 Category 4/9 calculations. This may require enhanced data capture from carriers and improved client reporting capabilities.

---

## Recommended Actions

| Priority | Action | Owner | Timeframe |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Conduct SBTi alignment assessment: evaluate client requirements and competitive positioning | Sustainability + Sales | Q2 2026 |
| 2 | Establish Scope 1, 2, and 3 emissions baseline using ISO 14083-aligned methodology | Sustainability + Operations | Q2-Q3 2026 |
| 3 | Assess transport mode-specific SBTi guidance applicability (Maritime, Aviation, Land) | Sustainability + Product Teams | Q2 2026 |
| 4 | Evaluate business case for public SBTi commitment versus voluntary alignment | Strategy + Finance | Q3 2026 |
| 5 | Implement enhanced emissions data capture from carriers for Category 4 accuracy | IT + Procurement | Q3-Q4 2026 |
| 6 | Develop client-facing sustainability reporting aligned with SBTi methodology | Sustainability + Sales | Q4 2026 |
| 7 | Train sales team on SBTi positioning in tender responses | Sales + Sustainability | Q3 2026 |
| 8 | Establish governance framework for ongoing target monitoring and five-year reviews | Sustainability + Legal | Q4 2026 |
| 9 | Integrate SBTi alignment into supplier qualification criteria for key carriers | Procurement + Sustainability | Q1 2027 |
| 10 | Monitor SBTi Corporate Net-Zero Standard V2 publication for updated requirements | Sustainability | Ongoing 2026 |

---

## Implementation Timeline

|---|---|---|
| January 2026 | SBTi reaches 10,000 companies with validated targets | In force |

| December 18, 2025 | Five-Year Review Guidance and Target Status Manual effective | In force |
| 2026 Implementation | New Target Dashboard status categories | Upcoming |
| Ongoing | Weekly Target Dashboard updates (every Thursday) | In force |
| Monthly | Commitment compliance reviews (first Thursday) | In force |

---

## Open Questions

• **Net-Zero Standard V2 Changes:** What new requirements will the updated Corporate Net-Zero Standard introduce in mid-2026, and how will they affect existing commitments and target-setting approaches?

• **Transport-Specific Guidance Evolution:** Will SBTi develop more granular guidance for freight forwarding as a distinct service category versus current transport mode-specific approaches?

• **Environmental Attribute Certificates (EACs):** How will the ongoing consultation on EACs for Scope 3 abatement affect freight forwarders' ability to use renewable energy certificates or sustainable fuel certificates toward targets?

• **Small and Medium Enterprise (SME) Requirements:** Will SBTi develop differentiated requirements for smaller freight forwarders, or will all companies follow the same validation criteria regardless of size?

• **Regulatory Mandate Evolution:** Could SBTi alignment become legally mandated through supply chain due diligence regulations (e.g., CSDDD) or climate reporting requirements (e.g., CSRD)?

• **Cost Impact Quantification:** What is the business case ROI for SBTi validation in terms of tender win rates and premium pricing versus certification and ongoing compliance costs?

• **Carrier Partner Requirements:** Will freight forwarders need to require SBTi alignment from their carrier partners to meet their own Scope 3 Category 4 targets effectively?

fullBrief:
# IFRS S2 Climate-related Disclosures

**Regulatory Fact Document** | April 2026
**Primary source:** [IFRS S2 Climate-related Disclosures](https://www.ifrs.org/issued-standards/ifrs-sustainability-standards-navigator/ifrs-s2-climate-related-disclosures/)

---

## What This Regulation Is and Why It Applies



IFRS S2 Climate-related Disclosures was issued by the International Sustainability Standards Board (ISSB) in June 2023

, establishing the first global baseline for climate-related financial disclosure. 

IFRS S2 integrates and builds on the recommendations of the Task Force on Climate-related Financial Disclosures (TCFD) and incorporates industry-based disclosure requirements derived from SASB Standards

. 

IFRS S2 is effective for annual reporting periods beginning on or after 1 January 2024 with earlier application permitted as long as IFRS S1 General Requirements for Disclosure of Sustainability-related Financial Information is also applied

.

Unlike voluntary frameworks, IFRS S2 creates legally binding requirements where jurisdictions adopt it. 

IFRS is gaining traction globally, with more than 30 jurisdictions moving toward mandatory reporting

. 

Rules mandating the use of the ISSB standards became effective at the start of 2026 in several jurisdictions including Chile, Qatar and Mexico

. 

More than 30 jurisdictions are moving toward mandatory reporting, with jurisdictions such as Hong Kong, Japan, and Malaysia formally aligning with ISSB rules, while Australia and Singapore focus on climate disclosures under IFRS S2

.

For freight forwarders, IFRS S2 fundamentally changes the commercial landscape by expanding Scope 3 emissions disclosure requirements globally. 

IFRS S2 requires companies to disclose absolute gross greenhouse gas emissions generated during the reporting period, measured in accordance with the Greenhouse Gas Protocol, classified as scope 1 emissions, scope 2 emissions, and scope 3 emissions

. 

According to the Greenhouse Gas Protocol, Scope 3 emissions, which include logistics, often represent 70–90% or more of a company's total carbon footprint

. This means freight forwarders will receive increasing data requests from clients who must comply with IFRS S2 in their reporting jurisdictions, creating both operational burden and commercial opportunity.

**Source:** IFRS S2 paragraphs 1-9, 29; IFRS Foundation Progress Report 2024

---

## Issues Requiring Immediate Action

### Client Data Request Preparation


IFRS S2 is the recommended standard for reporting by the International Sustainability Standards Board (ISSB) with which many national governments have aligned their climate disclosure regulations

. Companies subject to IFRS S2 will request transport emissions data for Categories 4 (upstream transport) and 9 (downstream transport) under the GHG Protocol Scope 3 categories.

**Action Required — Confirm for Your Business:** Determine which clients are subject to IFRS S2 disclosure requirements based on their jurisdiction and company size thresholds. Establish data collection capabilities to provide GHG emissions data by shipment, mode, and route.

### Scope 3 Calculation Methodology Compliance


Scope 3 greenhouse gas emissions are defined in the IFRS S2 standard as "Indirect greenhouse gas emissions (not included in Scope 2 greenhouse gas emissions) that occur in the value chain of an entity, including both upstream and downstream emissions. Scope 3 greenhouse gas emissions include the Scope 3 categories in the Greenhouse Gas Protocol Corporate Value Chain (Scope 3) Accounting and Reporting Standard (2011)"

.

**Action Required — Confirm for Your Business:** Ensure calculation methodologies align with GHG Protocol Corporate Value Chain (Scope 3) Accounting and Reporting Standard (2011). Verify data quality meets IFRS S2 materiality requirements.

### Data Quality and Materiality Standards


IFRS S2 requires an entity to disclose information about climate-related risks and opportunities that could reasonably be expected to affect the entity's cash flows, its access to finance or cost of capital over the short, medium or long term

. 

IFRS S2 considers the requirement in IFRS S1 that prohibits info from being aggregated if doing so would obscure info that is material

.

**Action Required — Confirm for Your Business:** Establish data quality indicators and materiality thresholds for transport emissions reporting. Ensure systems can provide disaggregated data when aggregation would obscure material information.

### Jurisdiction-Specific Implementation Tracking


Pakistan adopted mandatory reporting as of July 1, 2025, for large, listed companies

. 

Qatar adopted the ISSB standards as of Jan. 1, 2026, after the Qatar Financial Centre Regulatory Authority (QFCRA) set rules related to the two ISSB standards in June 2025. The standards apply to incorporated companies, banks and insurers

.

**Action Required — Confirm for Your Business:** Map client locations against IFRS S2 adoption timelines by jurisdiction. Create jurisdiction-specific compliance calendars for data delivery requirements.

---

## Operational Impact by Transport Mode

### Ocean Freight


The scope of disclosure includes emissions from all freight transportation and logistics activities, including those from the entity's own assets (Scope 1) and those from contract carriers and outsourced freight forwarding services, including emissions from all modes of transportation, such as road freight, air freight, barge transport, marine transport, and rail transport

.

Ocean carriers must provide fuel consumption and emissions data aligned with 

EN 16258:2012 methodology for calculating "tank-to-wheels GHG emissions" with determination of transportation system scope, boundaries, and any necessary allocations consistent with EN 16258:2012

. Freight forwarders must collect this data and allocate emissions to specific shipments using mass-based allocation for container shipping.

Cost implications: Ocean freight surcharges for emissions data provision and carbon measurement systems. Expect 0.5-2% surcharge on ocean freight rates for enhanced data reporting capabilities.

### Air Freight
Air freight has the highest emissions intensity per tonne-kilometer, making accurate measurement critical for client disclosure. 

Transportation and distribution of products purchased in the reporting year, between a company's tier 1 suppliers and its own operations in vehicles not owned or operated by the reporting company (including multi-modal shipping where multiple carriers are involved in the delivery of a product)

 must be captured.

Air carriers typically allocate emissions based on chargeable weight. Freight forwarders must ensure allocation methodologies align with IFRS S2 materiality requirements. Expect premium pricing for detailed emissions tracking on air freight movements.

### Road Freight


The fuel-based method is best applied if the vehicle exclusively ships the reporting company's purchased goods (i.e., exclusive use or truckload shipping, rather than less-than-truckload (LTL) shipping). Otherwise, emissions should be allocated between goods shipped for the reporting company and goods shipped for other companies

.

For LTL shipments, 

Rail capacity is typically limited by mass, so mass-based allocation should be used. If there are multiple shipments on a transport leg, distance should also be used as a means for allocation

.

### Customs and Reporting Integration
IFRS S2 requires integration with existing financial reporting systems. 

Climate disclosures are being built into the same decision-grade ecosystem as financial reporting

. Customs data systems must link with emissions calculation platforms to provide shipment-level carbon data for client reporting.

---

## Key Data and Figures

| Parameter | Value | Source |

## Open Questions

• **Scope 3 Category 15 Relief Implementation**: 

ISSB amendments effective January 1, 2027 allow entities to limit Scope 3 Category 15 emissions to only their financed emissions, attributable to loans and investments made by the entity to counterparties or investees

 — impacts primarily financial institutions, but creates precedent for other category reliefs

• **Industry Classification System Flexibility**: 

Amendments allow entities to select an industry classification system that enables users to understand exposure to climate-related transition risks, with guidance on choosing systems to ensure comparability

 — awaiting specific guidance on transport sector applications

• **Interoperability with EU CSRD**: 

ISSB working on interoperability between ESRS and ISSB Standards to improve alignment in disclosures and avoid duplication

 — final guidance pending on dual-reporting scenarios

• **US Federal vs. State Requirements**: 

US SEC climate disclosure rules published in March 2024 have not gone into effect, though they have not been formally rescinded

, while 

California requires Scope 1 and 2 emissions reporting from August 26, 2026, and Scope 3 emissions from 2027



• **Nature-Related Disclosures Timeline**: 

ISSB will build on TNFD work to create nature-related standards, similar to the path taken by TCFD which handed over monitoring to ISSB after IFRS S1 and S2 were published

 — timing for additional logistics-relevant disclosures unclear

fullBrief:
# CDP Supply Chain Programme

**Regulatory Fact Document** | April 2026
**Primary source:** [CDP Supply Chain Programme](https://www.cdp.net/en/supply-chain)

---

## What This Regulation Is and Why It Applies



The CDP Supply Chain Climate Change Information Request is an electronic questionnaire designed to collect information that is widely used by large private and public sector organizations to understand, assess, and mitigate potentially disruptive and costly supply chain risks, investment risks, and environmental impacts. The questionnaire is administered by CDP North America, Inc., a 501(c)(3) nonprofit organization ("CDP"). CDP administers the questionnaire annually on behalf of over 700 institutional investors, 300 major corporations, and several large governmental purchasing organizations

 including the U.S. General Services Administration.



More than 270 leading corporate buyers already use CDP Supply Chain to manage risks across their supply chains

, making this the world's largest supply chain environmental disclosure system. 

CDP's most recent annual survey was directed to over 40,000 companies, with over 23,000 electing to respond.

 The programme enables purchasing organizations to request standardized environmental data from their suppliers through annual questionnaires covering climate change, water security, forests, and emerging themes like biodiversity and plastics.

For freight forwarders, CDP Supply Chain represents a critical procurement gatekeeping mechanism. 

Over 22,100 companies disclosed environmental data through CDP in 2025. These companies represent more than half of global market capitalization including some of the world's largest and most impactful companies.

 If your clients are large corporations or work with large corporations, they are likely CDP Supply Chain members who will require you to respond to CDP questionnaires as a condition of supplier qualification. 

Organizations who are requested to disclose their data and fail to do so will be allocated a score of F, indicating they have failed to provide sufficient information with which to be evaluated by CDP.



**Source:** CDP Supply Chain Programme overview, Federal Register Notice 2024-16032

---

## Issues Requiring Immediate Action

### Check for Outstanding CDP Requests



The CDP portal opened May 14 — companies should proactively activate their questionnaire and can submit responses any time between June 4 and the scoring deadline.

 

For companies eligible to receive a CDP score, this is the last day to submit your questionnaire(s). Initially set for September 18, the deadline has been extended to October 2, 2024

 for scored responses.

**Action Required — Confirm for Your Business:** Check your corporate email systems and CDP portal login for any outstanding questionnaire requests from clients. Review contracts to identify which clients are likely CDP Supply Chain members.

### 2025 Scoring Deadline Approaching



Week of 15 September 2025 – The deadline for scored responses.

 Companies submitting after the scoring deadline receive no score, effectively an 'F' rating for procurement purposes.

**Action Required — Confirm for Your Business:** If you have received CDP requests for 2025, prioritize completion before mid-September 2025 to ensure scoring eligibility.

### New Integrated Questionnaire Format



In 2024, these have been combined into one integrated Corporate Questionnaire, streamlining the disclosure process and enabling a holistic approach to environmental impacts, risks, and opportunities.

 

With 2,000 pages, new assessment structure and over 5,500 questions, it's anything but simple.



**Action Required — Confirm for Your Business:** If responding to CDP for the first time under the new format, allocate significantly more time and resources than previous years due to the expanded questionnaire scope.

### Essential Scoring Criteria Changes



CDP has introduced an additional layer of essential criteria for Climate Change that applies across all scoring levels. Organizations responding to the full corporate questionnaire for Climate Change must meet these criteria to progress their scores to the next level (D to C, C to B, B to A etc.). These criteria showcase CDP's high expectations of those wishing to score amongst the leaders, including (but not limited to) comprehensive disclosure on relevant climate-related risks and opportunities, board competency on climate change, aligning transition plans and targets to a 1.5°C scenario, and comprehensive emissions disclosures, including getting defined proportions of those emissions verified.



**Action Required — Confirm for Your Business:** Review your current sustainability governance structure to ensure board-level oversight and competency requirements are met before submitting responses.

### Supply Chain Module Integration



Supply chain questions are no longer included in a separate module and are instead included throughout the questionnaire.

 This affects how freight forwarders report on customer emissions data and modal split information.

**Action Required — Confirm for Your Business:** Prepare comprehensive Scope 3 emissions data for your transport services, including modal split data and customer-specific emissions reporting capabilities.

---

## Operational Impact by Transport Mode

### Ocean Freight Operations

CDP questionnaires require detailed Scope 3 emissions reporting for ocean transport services. This includes:

- **Emissions per TEU/CBM**: Granular emissions factors for container shipping routes
- **Modal shift reporting**: Documentation of rail/barge alternatives to road haulage
- **Fuel type disclosure**: Use of biofuels, methanol, and other alternative marine fuels
- **Port emissions**: Scope 3 emissions from port operations and ground handling

**Cost Pass-Through Mechanism**: Clients increasingly include CDP scoring requirements in RFP processes. Poor CDP scores (<B rating) can result in automatic disqualification from tender processes or require significant price reductions to compensate for sustainability risk.

### Air Freight Operations

CDP requires comprehensive air cargo emissions reporting including:

- **Route-specific emissions**: Emissions per kilogram by origin-destination pairs
- **Aircraft efficiency data**: Load factors, aircraft types, and fuel efficiency metrics
- **Sustainable aviation fuel (SAF) usage**: Documentation of SAF uptake and sourcing
- **Intermodal alternatives**: Analysis of air-to-surface modal shift opportunities

**Cost Pass-Through Mechanism**: SAF surcharges and carbon offset costs are increasingly passed through based on CDP reporting requirements. Clients use CDP data to validate green transportation surcharges.

### Road Transport and Final Mile

CDP questionnaires cover last-mile delivery emissions with specific focus on:

- **Vehicle fleet composition**: Electric vehicle adoption rates and deployment strategies
- **Urban logistics emissions**: City-specific emissions factors and delivery density optimization
- **Cold chain energy consumption**: Refrigerated transport energy usage and alternative cooling technologies
- **Delivery optimization**: Route planning and consolidation impact on emissions reduction

**Cost Pass-Through Mechanism**: CDP scores directly impact green logistics surcharge acceptance. Higher CDP scores enable premium pricing for sustainable delivery services.

### Customs and Trade Documentation

CDP requires disclosure of trade facilitation emissions including:

- **Digital documentation impact**: Emissions reductions from paperless processing
- **Customs facility energy consumption**: Warehouse and processing facility emissions
- **Trade lane optimization**: Emissions impact of customs compliance on routing decisions

| Average Scope 3 emissions multiplier | 26x operational emissions | CDP Strengthening the Chain |
| Estimated climate opportunities value | US$165 billion potential gains | CDP Strengthening the Chain |
| Supplier-driven emissions reductions | 43 million tonnes annually | CDP Strengthening the Chain |

---

|---|---|---|---|
| F score for non-response to client CDP requests | High | High | Annual submission deadlines |
| Automatic tender disqualification | High | Medium | Ongoing RFP processes |
| Loss of preferred supplier status | Medium | High | Annual client reviews |
| Inability to justify green transport surcharges | Medium | High | Quarterly pricing reviews |
| Scope 3 emissions data gaps affecting client CSRD compliance | High | Medium | Client annual reporting cycles |
| Inadequate board-level climate governance affecting Leadership scores | Medium | Low | Annual governance assessments |

---

## Recommended Actions

| Priority | Action | Owner | Timeframe |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Audit all major client contracts for CDP Supply Chain membership | Sales | Immediate |
| 2 | Establish CDP response tracking system for all questionnaire requests | Sustainability | Q2 2026 |
| 3 | Implement comprehensive Scope 3 emissions tracking for all transport modes | Operations | Q3 2026 |
| 4 | Develop modal shift analysis capabilities for all major trade lanes | Ocean Product, Air Product | Q4 2026 |
| 5 | Establish board-level climate competency and governance framework | Legal | Q1 2027 |
| 6 | Create client-specific emissions reporting dashboards linked to CDP requirements | IT | Q4 2026 |
| 7 | Develop SAF and alternative fuel procurement strategy for CDP reporting | Procurement | Q3 2026 |
| 8 | Train commercial teams on CDP scoring impact in RFP responses | Sales | Q2 2026 |
| 9 | Establish third-party verification process for emissions data | Finance | Q4 2026 |
| 10 | Create CDP response template library for efficient annual submissions | Sustainability | Q3 2026 |

---

## Implementation Timeline

| Date | Milestone | Status |
|---|---|---|
| May 14, 2025 | CDP portal opens for 2025 cycle | Upcoming |
| Week of 15 September 2025 | Scoring deadline for 2025 responses | Upcoming |
| Week of 17 November 2025 | Final submission deadline (unscored) | Upcoming |
| December 2025 | CDP 2025 scores released | Upcoming |
| March 20, 2026 | Score appeal deadline | Upcoming |
| May 2026 | CDP 2026 cycle begins | Upcoming |

---

## Open Questions

- **2026 Questionnaire Changes**: CDP has indicated further integration and simplification of the questionnaire format, but specific changes have not been announced
- **Biodiversity and Plastics Scoring**: 

Biodiversity and Plastics questions remain unscored in 2024

, but CDP may introduce scoring for these themes in 2026
- **SME Questionnaire Evolution**: The impact of the new SME questionnaire on smaller freight forwarders and whether eligibility criteria will change
- **Supply Chain Module Future**: How the integration of supply chain questions throughout the questionnaire will evolve and what additional transport-specific questions may be added
- **Verification Requirements**: Whether CDP will mandate third-party verification for certain emissions data categories
- **Regional Variations**: Potential differences in CDP requirements across different geographic regions and regulatory jurisdictions

fullBrief:
# EcoVadis Sustainability Ratings Platform

**Regulatory Fact Document** | April 2026
**Primary source:** [EcoVadis Platform](https://ecovadis.com/)

---

## What This Platform Is and Why It Applies

EcoVadis is a global sustainability ratings platform that assesses corporate ESG performance across four themes: Environment, Labor & Human Rights, Ethics, and Sustainable Procurement. 

EcoVadis analysts dedicate more than 15 hours to reviewing your customized questionnaire and supporting documents, resulting in a Ratings Scorecard with a 0–100 score across four themes, plus documented strengths and areas for improvement.

 Unlike other ESG frameworks, 

EcoVadis pricing operates on an annual subscription model, not a one-time certification fee. Every assessed company must maintain an active subscription to access the platform and receive a scorecard.





The EcoVadis platform helps you drive confident procurement decisions with trusted sustainability data.

 For freight forwarders, this platform functions as a de facto industry standard that directly determines supplier qualification and contract opportunities. 

"Please provide your current EcoVadis score or indicate your status." If you're running supply chain operations or if you are a 3PL in North America, you've probably seen this more in the last 18 months than in the prior decade. And if your answer is "we don't have one," the follow-up question from your customer is usually some version of: "When will you?" — or you may not be able to participate in that business at all until you do.

 

Sustainability programs and EcoVadis ratings are customer-driven here. The large manufacturers and retailers at the top of global supply chains are pushing sustainability program accountability down to their tier-one and tier-two suppliers, including their 3PLs.



The operational impact on freight forwarding is direct: 

Most procurement teams today consider Silver ratings as the baseline for strong sustainability performance. Many prefer Gold status suppliers who show advanced commitment to environmental and social responsibility.

 This creates a binary business relationship factor — with an adequate score, contracts flow; without one, market access becomes limited. Revenue impact is measurable: 

Our EcoVadis Gold Medal resulted in more than €37 million from clients who monitor our EcoVadis performance from 2023.



---

## Issues Requiring Immediate Action

### Assessment Platform Access Required NOW



For companies newly invited for their first EcoVadis assessment, you can enjoy the free questionnaire period: This period is up to 6 weeks or when you submit your questionnaire, whichever comes first. Depending on your chosen subscription plan, it offers features that help you complete your questionnaire. You can cancel your free questionnaire period at any time, and you can reactivate the questionnaire within the original 6 weeks period.



**Action Required — Confirm for Your Business:** Identify if any clients have already requested an EcoVadis score or included EcoVadis requirements in recent RFPs. Review existing supplier qualification requirements from major accounts to determine urgency.

### Annual Subscription Cost Planning



While prices vary, expect a range from around EUR €489/year for an extra-small company in the USA, to several thousand for larger companies on premium tiers. For many SMEs the subscription cost is less than 10% of the true EcoVadis assessment cost once internal hours are factored in.

 

In Vendr's dataset, buyers assessing 50–100 suppliers often see annual platform fees in the $15,000–$40,000 range, depending on assessment complexity and contract term.



**Action Required — Confirm for Your Business:** Budget for both subscription fees and internal resource allocation. 

The largest and most frequently underestimated cost is the internal time required to complete the assessment.



### Documentation Requirements and Evidence Collection



The questionnaire, which covers themes including Environment, Labor & Human Rights, Ethics, Sustainable Procurement, and Carbon, contains 20-50 questions which require company-specific documents to be submitted as evidence. You may be expected to meet a specific scoring threshold to maintain a preferred vendor status, or else you may be expected to remediate a poor score or a specific gap in performance.



**Action Required — Confirm for Your Business:** Assess current policy documentation for environmental management systems, employee safety protocols, anti-corruption policies, and supplier assessment procedures. 

The Low End (50-150 Hours): The "Optimiser" This is typically a mature SME, often with existing certifications like ISO 14001. You are already doing the right things; your primary focus is Evidence Alignment—mapping your excellent existing proof to the specific EcoVadis criteria and closing minor gaps to maximise your score.



### Medal Performance Targeting



Platinum - Top 1% (99+ percentile) Gold - Top 5% (95+ percentile) Silver - Top 15% (85+ percentile) Bronze - Top 35% (65+ percentile)

 

Most procurement teams today consider Silver ratings as the baseline for strong sustainability performance. Many prefer Gold status suppliers who show advanced commitment to environmental and social responsibility.



**Action Required — Confirm for Your Business:** Set realistic medal targets. Bronze (45+ score) for first assessment, Silver (59+ score) for competitive positioning, Gold (69+ score) for market leadership positioning.

---

## Operational Impact by Transport Mode

### Ocean Freight Operations



Environment — Emissions management, energy consumption, packaging, waste reduction.

 For ocean operations, this directly affects vessel chartering decisions, container efficiency metrics, and port selection based on environmental performance. EcoVadis Environment theme scoring examines fleet composition data, alternative fuel adoption, and carbon intensity reporting. Operational costs pass through via documentation requirements for Scope 3 emissions tracking from shipping lines and increased due diligence on carrier selection.

**Contract implications:** Ocean freight contracts increasingly include sustainability clauses requiring EcoVadis scores from logistics providers. Failure to meet minimum thresholds can trigger contract review clauses or exclusion from preferred carrier status.

### Air Freight Operations

Air cargo environmental impact receives heavy weighting in EcoVadis Environment assessments due to carbon intensity. Airlines and cargo operators must document fuel efficiency programs, sustainable aviation fuel adoption, and cargo load optimization. 

Labor & Human Rights — Health and safety, diversity policies, employee training, career advancement, equitable compensation.

 applies directly to cargo handling operations and pilot working conditions.

**Cost mechanisms:** Air freight surcharges for sustainability compliance are becoming standard. EcoVadis scores affect airline preferred partnership status, which directly impacts rate negotiations and capacity allocation during peak seasons.

### Road Transport



Ethics — Anti-corruption, anti-fraud, data security, whistleblower protections. Sustainable Procurement — Supplier assessment practices, environmental and social criteria in sourcing.

 For road operations, this encompasses driver working conditions, fleet electrification, route optimization, and subcontractor vetting. Trucking operations face particular scrutiny on labor practices and driver welfare standards.

**Surcharge mechanisms:** Road transport sustainability surcharges are calculated based on fleet emission standards, driver training certification, and telematics system implementation for fuel efficiency monitoring.

### Customs and Trade Compliance



Ethics — Anti-corruption, anti-fraud, data security, whistleblower protections.

 Customs brokerage operations require documented anti-corruption procedures, trade compliance training programs, and data security protocols for sensitive shipping information. Cross-border operations face increased scrutiny on ethical business practices.

---

## Open Questions

• **Medal threshold evolution**: 

For example, Gold is reserved for the top 5% of all companies assessed globally. This means the definition of a "good" score needed for a medal is constantly getting more competitive.

 How will medal requirements change as more companies join?

• **Industry-specific benchmarking**: How will freight forwarding-specific assessment criteria develop as EcoVadis expands industry customization?

• **AI integration impact**: 

One practical implication discussed was the shift in effort when completing EcoVadis questionnaires: less time spent on document chasing and formatting, and more time needed for validation and decision-making. AI can help structure documentation, attach evidence, and flag eligibility issues to reduce friction.

 How will AI change assessment requirements?

• **Regulatory alignment**: 

Companies reporting under CSRD or EINF will find that much of their existing data maps directly to EcoVadis assessment criteria.

 How will evolving EU regulations affect EcoVadis methodology?

• **Supplier cascade requirements**: Will major shippers require their freight forwarders to assess their own suppliers (trucking companies, warehouses) through EcoVadis?

• **Carbon module integration**: 

The Carbon Action Manager is a unique solution that consolidates the data we need from our suppliers in one place.

 What additional carbon reporting requirements will emerge?

whatIsIt: The EU Fit for 55 package is the comprehensive set of EU legislative proposals and regulations to reduce net greenhouse gas emissions by at least 55% by 2030 (vs 1990 levels), adopted under the European Green Deal. fullBrief:
# EU Packaging and Packaging Waste Regulation (PPWR 2025/40)

**Regulatory Fact Document** | April 2026
**Primary source:** [[Regulation (EU) 2025/40](https://eur-lex.europa.eu/eli/reg/2025/40/oj/eng)](https://eur-lex.europa.eu/eli/reg/2025/40/oj/eng) | **Commission guidance:** [C(2026) 2151 Final](https://environment.ec.europa.eu/publications/guidance-document-packaging-and-packaging-waste-regulation-ppwr_en)

---

## What This Regulation Is and Why It Applies

The PPWR governs every piece of packaging placed on the EU market, regardless of where it was made or where the company placing it is based. There is no exemption for fine art logistics, live events, luxury goods, or any other cargo vertical. The wooden crates used to move artwork between European museums, the flight cases moved between European venues, and the bubble wrap and foam used to protect objects inside both, are all potentially in scope.

The regulation is directly applicable across all 27 EU Member States from **August 12, 2026**. Unlike a directive, it requires no national transposition and cannot be watered down or delayed by individual Member States. It creates obligations across the entire supply chain: manufacturers, importers, distributors, and fulfillment service providers all carry distinct and, in some cases, overlapping obligations.

**Source:** Articles 2, 3, 10, [Regulation (EU) 2025/40](https://eur-lex.europa.eu/eli/reg/2025/40/oj/eng)

---

## Issues Requiring Immediate Action

These require decisions or action now. They are not 2030 planning items.

### 1. Commission Guidance May Change Compliance Scope

Newly published European Commission guidance may fundamentally change what is required. Specifically, provisions address whether flight cases, road cases, and custom artwork crates fall within the regulation at all, whether custom-designed transport packaging is exempt from reuse targets, and how manufacturer obligations are allocated when packaging is sold under a company's own name. If legal confirms these provisions work in your favor, a significant portion of the compliance burden may not apply. If they do not, the investment and operational transformation described throughout this document is required in full.

**Action Required — Confirm for Your Business:** Legal must read C(2026) 2151 final in full and advise on its implications before any compliance positions are established.

### 2. EPR Registration Deadline — August 2027

The EU Packaging Regulation requires companies placing packaging on the EU market to register as producers in each EU Member State by August 2027, after which unregistered producers cannot legally place packaging on the market. The European Commission was required to publish the registration format by February 2026. It has not done so. Without that format, registration preparation cannot begin.

**Source:** Article 44, [Regulation (EU) 2025/40](https://eur-lex.europa.eu/eli/reg/2025/40/oj/eng)

### 3. Case Tracking Infrastructure — 12-24 Month Build

The regulation requires operators of re-use systems to track and report rotation counts, reconditioning events, rejections, and end-of-life disposals at the individual case level from 2030. Most logistics operators currently manage case inventory on a manual, piece-count basis with no serial numbers, no rotation history, and no reconditioning records. Building or procuring a tracking system takes 12 to 24 months from decision to full operational deployment.

### 4. Packaging Classification — Threshold Question

Whether flight cases, road cases, and custom artwork crates qualify as packaging under the regulation's definition is the threshold question. If they do not qualify, the reuse system builds, the recyclability grading, and the Annex VI reporting obligations do not apply. If they do qualify, every case in EU operations falls within scope.

**Action Required — Confirm for Your Business:** Legal must produce a written position based on Article 3 and Section 1 of Commission guidance before operations begin any system build.

### 5. Vendor and Client Compliance Exposure

Freight operators are exposed to penalties and shipment disruptions caused by vendors and clients who do not know the regulation applies to them. When a shipment is refused or penalized because a crate builder, gallery, auction house, or production client failed to meet their obligations, the consequence lands on the party with the client relationship.

### 6. Mandatory Labeling from 2028

From 2028, all packaging must carry harmonized EU recycling labels. From 2029, standardized digital QR reusability labels are required on reusable packaging in closed loop systems. Commission guidance Section 14 states that Article 12 labeling requirements primarily target consumer-facing packaging rather than industrial or commercial packaging.

**Action Required — Confirm for Your Business:** Legal must read Section 14 and advise on scope for B2B transport packaging before any labeling investment is made.

---

## How Freight Operators Sit in the Compliance Chain

### As Commissioning Importers
When a freight operator commissions crate fabrication on behalf of clients, in most transactions they are the importer placing packaging on the EU market for the first time. Commission guidance Section 2 confirms that for transport packaging without a name or trademark, the decisive criterion is who places the order and decides on the design specifications. Where the operator's name or trademark appears on the packaging, Article 21 makes them the manufacturer regardless of who built the crate.

**Action Required — Confirm for Your Business:** Legal must map the manufacturer and producer obligation across main transaction types: commissioning crates from fabricators, selling branded packaging products, and handling client-supplied packaging for EU-bound shipments.

**Source:** [Commission Guidance C(2026) 2151 final](https://environment.ec.europa.eu/publications/guidance-document-packaging-and-packaging-waste-regulation-ppwr_en), Sections 2 and 3; Articles 15, 18, 21, [Regulation (EU) 2025/40](https://eur-lex.europa.eu/eli/reg/2025/40/oj/eng)

### As Logistics Operators Handling Client-Owned Equipment
When handling client-owned cases rather than procuring new packaging, the client owns the cases and is the producer. The logistics operator bears the Article 20 duty of care obligation — meaning the operator must not work with clients whose packaging is non-compliant.

**Action Required — Confirm for Your Business:** Legal must advise on the scope of Article 20 duty of care and what verification of client compliance is required before accepting a shipment.

**Source:** Articles 18, 19, 20, [Regulation (EU) 2025/40](https://eur-lex.europa.eu/eli/reg/2025/40/oj/eng)

---

## Commission Guidance C(2026) 2151 Final — Key Findings

Published March 30, 2026 by the European Commission Directorate-General for Environment. This document interprets selected provisions of the PPWR. It does not amend the regulation. The binding interpretation remains the exclusive competence of the Court of Justice of the European Union.

### Section 1 — Definition of Packaging
Whether an item qualifies as packaging must be assessed based on the Article 3(1) definition. The key test is whether the item is intended to be used, consumed, or disposed of together with the product. Items that are not discarded to access their contents, and items that enable a production or logistics cycle rather than constituting disposable packaging, may fall outside the definition. The transport packaging definition explicitly excludes road, rail, ship, and air containers.

**Flowerpot Analogy:** Flowerpots used by business operators as part of their production cycle are not packaging because they are mere enablers of the manufacturing cycle. This is directly analogous to custom artwork crates and flight cases used in ongoing transport cycles that are never discarded at destination.

**IV Bag Analogy:** Intravenous bags and syringes are not packaging because they are functional delivery devices. A flight case or custom artwork crate that travels permanently with the cargo and is never discarded is analytically similar.

### Section 2 — Manufacturer Definition
There is always only one manufacturer in a supply chain. For transport packaging, the manufacturer is normally the company that manufactures it, unless it is clearly branded by the user, in which case the user is the manufacturer. The decisive criterion is who places the order and decides on the design specifications.

### Section 14 — Labeling of Existing Reusable Transport Packaging
Article 12 labeling requirements primarily target consumer-facing packaging, rather than industrial or commercial packaging. This limits the mandatory labeling scope for B2B transport packaging.

### Section 21 — Exemptions for Custom-Designed Transport Packaging
The guidance confirms exemptions from reuse targets for equipment and commodities for which packaging is custom-designed to fit the individual requirement of the ordering economic operator. This is directly relevant to custom artwork crates built for specific works and potentially to purpose-built flight cases specified to individual client requirements.

---

## Confirmed Reuse Targets

| Packaging Scenario | Requirement from January 1, 2030 |
|---|---|
| All transport packaging used within the EU (general rule) | At least 40% reusable within a formally documented re-use system. 70% by 2040. |
| Transport packaging for B2B transfers within same EU Member State | 100% reusable. |
| Grouped transport packaging (excluding cardboard boxes) | 10% reusable by 2030. 25% by 2040. |
| Cardboard boxes | 0% reuse target. Explicitly exempt. |
| Pallet wrapping and strapping | Exempt via Commission Delegated Act, February 2026. |
| Custom-designed transport packaging | Exempt from reuse targets per Section 21 of Commission guidance. |
| Operators under 1,000 kg per Member State AND under 10 employees AND under €2M turnover | Reuse targets do not apply. |

**Source:** Article 29(1), 29(2), 29(5), [Regulation (EU) 2025/40](https://eur-lex.europa.eu/eli/reg/2025/40/oj/eng); [Commission Guidance C(2026) 2151 final](https://environment.ec.europa.eu/publications/guidance-document-packaging-and-packaging-waste-regulation-ppwr_en), Sections 19 and 21

---

## Packaging Format Analysis

### One-Way Wooden Crates
Single use by design, not enrolled in any re-use system. From January 1, 2030, 100% of transport packaging used for intra-EU B2B moves within the same Member State must be reusable. A crate shipped within a Member State and discarded at destination will not meet that requirement. The commercial question is whether the Section 21 custom-designed exemption removes this burden.

### Flight Cases and Road Cases
Compliance picture depends entirely on classification and exemption questions. Until legal has produced a written position on whether cases are packaging and whether Section 21 applies, operations cannot determine whether a compliance system is needed. Cases placed on the market before February 11, 2025 are exempt from reusable packaging design criteria under Article 15.

### Internal Foam Dunnage
Even if flight cases are confirmed as durable equipment, the foam inserts inside them may qualify as packaging. Polyurethane and polyethylene foam is subject to 35% recycled content (Article 7) and 70% recyclability grade from 2030. Standard foam formulations are unlikely to meet either threshold.

### Soft Packaging (All Verticals)
Bubble wrap, foam sheeting, polybags, tissue paper, and polystyrene are unambiguously packaging. No classification argument available.

| Material | PPWR Status |
|---|---|
| Bubble wrap, polyethylene foam, Ethafoam | Plastic packaging. Subject to 35% recycled content and 70% recyclability from 2030. Likely non-compliant in standard formulation. |
| Polyurethane foam sheeting | Plastic packaging. Not efficiently recycled at scale in EU infrastructure. High non-compliance risk. |
| Expanded polystyrene | Certain EPS formats banned from 2030 under Annex V per Article 67. |
| Plastic poly bags | Plastic packaging. Subject to recycled content and recyclability requirements from 2030. |
| Tissue paper and glassine | Paper and board. Not subject to plastic recycled content mandate. Lower compliance risk. |
| Kraft paper and corrugated wraps | Paper and board. Not subject to plastic recycled content mandate. Lower compliance risk. |

**Source:** Articles 6, 7, 67, Annex V, [Regulation (EU) 2025/40](https://eur-lex.europa.eu/eli/reg/2025/40/oj/eng)

### Cardboard and Corrugated
Explicitly exempt from reuse targets under Article 29. Expected to achieve high recyclability grades. No operational change required for compliant corrugated use.

---

## EPR Registration — Who Must Register

| Party | Registration Obligation |
|---|---|
| Museums commissioning outbound packaging for EU shipments | Yes. Must register in each Member State where they place packaging on the market. |
| Small US crate builders supplying packaging entering the EU market | Yes. Required to appoint an authorized representative in each relevant EU Member State. |
| Freight operator as commissioning importer | Yes, under Article 18. Where operator's name appears on packaging, Commission guidance confirms manufacturer with full obligations. |
| Logistics operator for packaging it procures and places on EU market | Yes, for soft packaging, corrugated, and expendable materials. |
| Touring clients and production companies (own case inventories) | Yes, unless they meet all three micro-enterprise criteria. |
| US galleries, auction houses, collectors shipping to EU | Yes. As non-EU entities they need an authorized representative in each Member State. |
| Recipients of shipments | No. A recipient is not placing packaging on the EU market. |
| Logistics operator handling client-owned packaging | Article 20 duty of care applies. Does not bear EPR registration for client-owned packaging unless its name appears on it. |

**Source:** Articles 15, 18, 19, 20, 21, 44, [Regulation (EU) 2025/40](https://eur-lex.europa.eu/eli/reg/2025/40/oj/eng); [Commission Guidance C(2026) 2151 final](https://environment.ec.europa.eu/publications/guidance-document-packaging-and-packaging-waste-regulation-ppwr_en), Sections 2, 3, 4

---

## Annex VI Re-use System Requirements

If flight cases and custom artwork crates are confirmed as packaging and are not exempt under Section 21, building a qualifying Annex VI re-use system is the central operational challenge from 2030. Physical reuse of cases already happens in operations. What does not exist is the formal governance structure, reporting infrastructure, and reconditioning documentation that the regulation requires.

A qualifying re-use system under Annex VI requires:
- Clearly defined governance structure with rules on packaging types, handling conditions, reconditioning requirements, collection, storage, cost sharing, and equal access
- A designated system operator
- Reporting rules covering number of re-uses, rejects, returns, new packs added, and retired packs
- Annual reports from end of 2030, due within six months, electronic and publicly accessible

**Action Required — Confirm for Your Business:** Whether existing case return practices constitute a qualifying re-use system under Annex VI, or whether formal governance agreements and reporting infrastructure are required.

**Source:** Annex VI Parts A and B, [Regulation (EU) 2025/40](https://eur-lex.europa.eu/eli/reg/2025/40/oj/eng)

---

## Implementation Timeline

| Step | When |
|---|---|
| System selection decision for case tracking | Now. 12-24 month build time. |
| Case serialization (unique ID per case) | Before system deployment. Pre-Feb 2025 cases flagged under Article 15. |
| Staff training on rotation tracking | Multi-year change management across all facilities. |
| Client engagement for client-owned cases | Requires client agreement to participate in governance framework. |
| Commission publishes reuse ratio calculation rules | June 2027 |
| Standardized digital QR reusability labels mandatory | 2029 (closed loop systems) |
| **Full compliance required** | **January 1, 2030** — 100% intra-EU B2B reuse if cases are packaging. |
| Commission publishes harmonized labeling specifications | August 2026 (Commission obligation, not operator deadline) |
| First confirmed mandatory operator labeling obligation | 2028 |
| Recyclability grade design implementing acts | January 2028 |

**Source:** Articles 11, 12, 29, 30, 31, [Regulation (EU) 2025/40](https://eur-lex.europa.eu/eli/reg/2025/40/oj/eng)

---

## Compliance Risk Register

| Risk | Severity | Likelihood | Deadline |
|---|---|---|---|
| Flight cases classified as packaging without Section 21 exemption | High | Medium | Legal position needed before any system build |
| Non-compliant foam dunnage inside cases | High | High | 2030 (recyclability grade) |
| EPR registration missed in one or more Member States | High | Medium | August 2027 |
| Vendor/client non-compliance causing shipment refusal | Medium | High | August 2026 (regulation applies) |
| Labeling non-compliance for consumer-facing packaging | Medium | Low | 2028 |
| Tracking system not ready for Annex VI reporting | High | Medium | January 2030 |
| Soft packaging (bubble wrap, foam) fails recyclability grade | High | High | 2030 |

---

## Recommended Actions

| Priority | Action | Owner | Timeframe |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Legal to read Commission guidance C(2026) 2151 final in full and produce written position on packaging classification | Legal | Immediate — before any other compliance decision |
| 2 | Legal to map manufacturer/producer obligations across all transaction types | Legal | 30 days |
| 3 | Begin case tracking system selection process | Operations + IT | Now (12-24 month build) |
| 4 | Build vendor onboarding framework and pre-shipment compliance checklist | Operations + Compliance | 60 days |
| 5 | Audit all soft packaging materials against recycled content and recyclability requirements | Procurement | 90 days |
| 6 | Review terms of service against PPWR exposure | Legal | 60 days |
| 7 | Monitor Commission registration format publication | Compliance | Ongoing until published |
| 8 | Begin investigating foam alternatives for internal dunnage | Procurement + Operations | Now (multi-year transition) |

---

## Open Questions

- Does the Section 21 custom-designed exemption apply to custom artwork crates? This is the most commercially significant legal question.
- Do flight cases qualify as packaging or durable transport equipment under Article 3?
- Does solid fiber laminated paperboard qualify as a "cardboard box" under Article 29?
- What is the precise scope of Article 20 duty of care for logistics operators?
- When will the Commission publish the EPR registration format?
- What recyclability grades will be assigned to polyurethane foam in the January 2028 implementing acts?

---

## Sources

| Source | URL | Authority |
|---|---|---|
| [Regulation (EU) 2025/40](https://eur-lex.europa.eu/eli/reg/2025/40/oj/eng) (primary text) | [EUR-Lex](https://eur-lex.europa.eu/eli/reg/2025/40/oj/eng) | Tier 1 — binding law |
| Commission Guidance C(2026) 2151 Final | [DG Environment](https://environment.ec.europa.eu/publications/guidance-document-packaging-and-packaging-waste-regulation-ppwr_en) | Tier 2 — official guidance |
