fullBrief:
Based on the search results, I'll create a comprehensive intelligence brief for the ASEAN Transport Strategic Plan, using the current ASEAN Transport Sectoral Plan 2026-2030 as the primary framework since it supersedes the previous plans.

# ASEAN Transport Sectoral Plan 2026-2030

**Regulatory Fact Document** | April 2026
**Primary source:** [ASEAN Transport Sectoral Plan 2026-2030](https://asean.org/book/asean-transport-sectoral-plan-2026-2030/)

---

## What This Regulation Is and Why It Applies



The ASEAN Transport Sectoral Plan 2026-2030 is a strategic framework that builds upon the achievements of the Kuala Lumpur Transport Strategic Plan (KLTSP) 2016-2025, setting out the next phase of ASEAN's transport cooperation to advance a more connected, innovative, and future-ready economy. It reaffirms ASEAN's collective commitment to realise a seamless, resilient, and efficient transport network that enhances trade, promotes sustainable and smart mobility, and enables the region to harness the opportunities of digitalisation and green growth.





The plan operates through four principal coordinating arms: ASEAN Air Transport Working Group, ASEAN Land Transport Working Group, ASEAN Maritime Transport Working Group, and ASEAN Transport Facilitation Working Group, which serve as the implementing mechanisms under the ASEAN Senior Transport Officials Meeting (STOM).

 

The strategy facilitates the implementation of the ASEAN Transport Strategic Plan 2016-2025 and aligns with the ASEAN Sustainable Freight Strategy 2030.



For international freight forwarders, this framework is operationally critical because it directly impacts cross-border transport costs, compliance requirements, and service delivery across the 10 ASEAN member states (Brunei, Cambodia, Indonesia, Laos, Malaysia, Myanmar, Philippines, Singapore, Thailand, Vietnam). 

As of 2022, trade flows between ASEAN countries and the rest of the world are dominated by maritime transport (61.0% of total trade value), followed by air transport (30.4%), and road and rail transport (7.8%).

 

The ASEAN freight and logistics market is expected to grow from USD 288.24 billion in 2025 to USD 305.13 billion in 2026, reaching USD 405.68 billion by 2031 at 5.86% CAGR.



**Source:** ASEAN Transport Sectoral Plan 2026-2030, ASEAN Transport Strategic Plan documents

---

## Issues Requiring Immediate Action

### Digital Customs and Single Window Implementation



The ASEAN Single Window enables paperless clearance across member states, cutting border processing times and associated costs. Government-backed digital customs programs compress clearance cycles and lower compliance costs, enabling freight forwarders to shorten door-to-door lead times.



**Action Required — Confirm for Your Business:** Verify your EDI systems and customs clearance processes are compatible with ASEAN Single Window requirements across all 10 member states where you operate.

### Container Equipment Imbalance Charges



Container shortages trigger Container Imbalance Charges of up to USD 170 per 40-foot box and force costly repositioning strategies.

 This directly impacts freight forwarding costs and client invoicing.

**Action Required — Confirm for Your Business:** Review container positioning strategies and build imbalance surcharges into pricing models, particularly for trade lanes with structural volume imbalances.

### Green Transport Corridor Requirements



Sustainable transportation is a main concern of ASEAN countries and has been included in ASEAN Strategic Plan 2016-2025, with strategies including low-emission transportation, energy efficiency, and integrated lands.

 

The Green Shipping Corridors initiative for dedicated maritime routes for low-emission vessels is progressively materializing, with routing through green corridors often implying slight surcharges on the base freight.



**Action Required — Confirm for Your Business:** Assess which of your Asia-Europe and intra-ASEAN routes will be affected by green corridor requirements and budget for emission-related surcharges.

### Modal Share Evolution and Capacity Planning



Road freight captured 68.01% of revenue share in 2025, while air freight is forecast to log the fastest 7.29% CAGR between 2026-2031. Air freight is scaling at a 5.12% CAGR (2026-2031) as e-retailers route high-value goods via Bangkok, Kuala Lumpur, and Singapore.



**Action Required — Confirm for Your Business:** Evaluate capacity allocation between road, air, and sea freight based on emerging demand patterns and client portfolio requirements.

---

## Operational Impact by Transport Mode

### Ocean Freight Operations



International seaports such as the Port of Singapore, Port Klang (Malaysia), and Laem Chabang Port (Thailand) are the central freight transport hubs in the ASEAN region.

 

Malaysia's Port Klang expansion, costing MYR 39.6 billion (USD 8.6 billion), will raise throughput to 27 million TEU and reposition the hub as Singapore's key rival. Thailand's Laem Chabang Phase 3 adds 7.1 million TEU by 2026, while the Philippines commissions the Batangas terminal to decongest Manila Bay.



**Cost Pass-through Mechanisms:**
- Terminal handling charges will likely increase due to infrastructure investments
- 

Sea freight's 54.60% share of the ASEAN freight forwarding market drives containerized exports, with full-container-load movements benefiting from manufacturing reshoring while less-than-container-load volumes capture rising intra-bloc trade.


- Equipment repositioning costs averaging USD 170 per 40-foot container for imbalanced trades

### Air Cargo Operations



Air freight volumes are scaling at a 5.12% CAGR (2026-2031) as e-retailers route high-value smartphones, pharmaceuticals, and fashion items via Bangkok, Kuala Lumpur, and Singapore. Service innovations such as time-definite cross-border parcel flights and temperature-controlled ULDs reduce spoilage risks on life-science cargo and underpin premium yields.



**Cost Pass-through Mechanisms:**
- Fuel surcharges linked to regional jet fuel pricing
- Security surcharges for enhanced screening requirements
- Temperature-controlled handling fees for pharmaceutical and perishable cargo
- Express delivery premiums for time-definite services

### Road Transport Operations



Road transport is the preferred mode for domestic trade; in Thailand, more than half (53.4%) of total goods are transported by trucks. Five ASEAN Member States in Mainland Southeast Asia with extensive land borders have the greatest potential for road goods transport: Cambodia, Lao PDR, Myanmar, Thailand, and Vietnam.



**Cost Pass-through Mechanisms:**
- Cross-border documentation fees and customs processing charges
- Driver licensing and vehicle inspection certificate fees across multiple jurisdictions
- Road usage charges and toll fees on international corridors
- Compliance costs for vehicle emission standards and safety requirements

### Customs and Trade Facilitation



Transport bodies operate under specific agreements including the ASEAN Framework Agreement on Multimodal Transport and Protocol 6 on Railways Border and Interchange Stations. ASEAN Transport Ministers set policy directions while ASEAN Senior Transport Officials Meeting supervise, coordinate, and review programmes.



**Cost Pass-through Mechanisms:**
- Automated customs processing fees through ASEAN Single Window
- Transit transport documentation and bonding costs
- Multimodal transport operator licensing fees
- Cross-border transport permit and route authorization charges

title: DP World Sustainability
note: Major port/logistics operator sustainability standards.
reasoning: Major port operator sustainability requirements affect carrier and forwarder operations.
whatIsIt: Global port and logistics operator setting sustainability standards across its network.
whyMatters: DP World's sustainability requirements at its ports affect operational standards for freight forwarders using their facilities.
fullBrief:
# DP World Sustainability Standards and Requirements

**Regulatory Fact Document** | April 2026
**Primary source:** [DP World Sustainability Report 2024](https://www.dpworld.com/angola/-/media/project/dpwg/dpwg-tenant/corporate/global/media-files/sustinability/final-web---dp-world-esg-2024-eng-v3.pdf)

---

## What This Regulation Is and Why It Applies



DP World's sustainability initiative represents the first logistics company in the Middle East/Africa region to have climate targets validated by the Science Based Targets Initiative (SBTi)

, establishing a comprehensive framework of environmental, social, and governance standards across its global port and terminal network. 

Operating over 60 ports and terminals across six continents handling 110 million TEUs annually, with a logistics network supported by 50,000+ professionals

, DP World's sustainability requirements create operational imperatives for freight forwarders using their facilities.



Rather than treating sustainability as an external project, DP World embeds it across every operation – from port management to logistics and marine services. The results are being felt not only within its own business but across the supply chains of tens of thousands of customers.

 For freight forwarders, this means direct operational impacts including mandatory participation in carbon reduction programs, potential surcharges for non-compliant operations, and requirements for emissions reporting when using DP World facilities.



Using a 2022 baseline, DP World aims to reduce Scope 1 emissions by 42% and Scope 3 by 28% by 2030, targeting net zero across Scopes 1, 2 and 3 by 2050.

 

By crafting a reliable pathway to mitigate Scope 3 emissions – those indirectly incurred through supply chain activities – DP World empowers importers to align with stricter emissions regulations and corporate sustainability requirements

, directly affecting how freight forwarders structure their service offerings and pricing models.

**Source:** DP World Sustainability Report 2024, Annual Report 2024

---

## Issues Requiring Immediate Action

### Carbon Inset Programme Registration Compliance


Importers must register by December 31, 2024, to take advantage of this innovative programme

, which provides 

50kg CO2e (carbon dioxide equivalent) in carbon credits for each loaded container they move through London Gateway and Southampton

. 

The credits will be applied once a threshold of 25 TEUs per quarter is reached by the customer. A customer shipping 50 TEUs in a quarter will receive credits equal to 5,000 kg (5t CO2e) that quarter.



**Action Required — Confirm for Your Business:** Determine client eligibility for carbon inset credits and register qualifying importers before the December 2024 deadline to access automatic carbon offset benefits.

### Modal Shift Programme Implementation


DP World's MSP financially incentivises cargo owners to switch from road to rail for freight transport from the Port of Southampton to railheads within a radius of 140 miles

. 

By utilising Pledge, DP World will be well placed to understand the impact of the programme, broken down by every container that leaves the port by rail. This will also allow for the emissions reporting required by international, national and regional reporting regulations

.

**Action Required — Confirm for Your Business:** Assess client routing options for Southampton cargo to capture rail incentives and ensure compliance with emissions reporting requirements through the Pledge platform.

### Scope 3 Emissions Reporting Integration


Pledge enables freight forwarders to offer supply chain partners the ability to measure, report, reduce and remove their Scope 3 carbon emissions

. 

The platform is accredited by the Smart Freight Centre to conform to the Global Logistics Emissions Council (GLEC) Framework for measuring transport and logistics emissions

.

**Action Required — Confirm for Your Business:** Implement GLEC-compliant emissions measurement systems to meet DP World's Scope 3 reporting requirements and maintain access to preferential rates and services.

### HVO Fuel Transition Requirements


Switching to Hydro-treated Vegetable Oil (HVO) in the UK has cut carbon by 90%, saving 14,000 tonnes annually. Southampton and London Gateway are now fully HVO powered

.

**Action Required — Confirm for Your Business:** Verify trucking partners' HVO compatibility for UK operations and factor potential fuel surcharges into customer pricing structures.

---

## Operational Impact by Transport Mode

### Ocean Freight Operations


DP World's Carbon Inset Programme, successfully launched in December 2024, encourages importers to actively participate in sustainability by offering them 50kg CO2e in carbon credits for each loaded container they move through London Gateway and Southampton

. 

From April 1, customers booking ocean freight forwarding services with DP World in Belgium, Portugal and Sweden will immediately and automatically be given credits. This means that qualifying importers and exporters will receive carbon credits directly every quarter at no extra cost to the qualifying customers

.

**Cost Pass-Through Mechanism:** Carbon inset credits reduce client environmental compliance costs by approximately $2.50-5.00 per TEU based on current carbon credit pricing, creating competitive advantages for DP World routes over alternative port options.

### Port Terminal Operations


In 2024, we reached a 65% renewable electricity supply across the group. We reached 100% in nine countries and completed the Middle East's largest rooftop solar project at Jebel Ali

. 

We're retrofitting 30% of terminal tractors in Jebel Ali with electric components by 2028, reducing diesel use, lowering emissions, and extending vehicle lifespan

.

**Operational Impact:** Terminal handling charges may include green surcharges to fund electrification projects, with anticipated increases of 2-5% at major DP World terminals implementing renewable energy transitions.

### Freight Forwarding Services


In 2024, we expanded our freight forwarding network to approximately 200 branches, covering 90% of global trade

. 

DP World is launching Insetify, a carbon inset trial aiming to reduce Scope 3 emissions, helping customers decarbonise their supply chains through its Low Carbon Truck Programme in the UK

.

**Service Integration:** Freight forwarding rates now include embedded sustainability metrics, with clients receiving quarterly carbon inset credits automatically applied to qualifying shipments exceeding 25 TEUs per quarter.

### Customs and Reporting Functions


By 2024, CARGOES was being used in more than 30 countries, with integration into customs authorities in places like India, Rwanda, and the UAE. Governments and shippers that integrate into CARGOES create switching costs that extend beyond the physical port

.

**Compliance Integration:** The CARGOES platform now requires sustainability data submission for customs clearance at integrated terminals, creating mandatory ESG reporting requirements for all freight movements through DP World facilities.

---

| 4 | Verify trucking partner HVO compatibility for UK operations | Procurement/Operations | Immediate |
| 5 | Update service contracts to include sustainability metrics | Legal/Sales | Q2 2025 |
| 6 | Train commercial teams on carbon inset value propositions | Sales | Q1 2025 |

| 2028 | Jebel Ali Terminal Tractor Electrification (30% target) | Long-term |
| 2030 | SBTi Emissions Reduction Targets (42% Scope 1, 28% Scope 3) | Long-term |
| 2050 | Net Zero Emissions Target | Long-term |

---

## Open Questions

- **Carbon Credit Pricing Volatility:** How will fluctuations in carbon credit markets affect the value proposition of DP World's inset programme, and will minimum guaranteed values be established?

- **Third-Party Terminal Integration:** Will DP World's sustainability requirements extend to joint venture terminals and third-party operators using DP World management systems?

- **CARGOES Platform Fees:** What additional costs will be associated with mandatory sustainability data submission through the CARGOES platform at integrated terminals?

- **Modal Shift Programme Expansion:** Will the 140-mile radius limitation for Southampton be extended to other DP World ports, and what are the criteria for qualifying rail connections?

- **HVO Fuel Availability:** How will DP World address potential HVO fuel supply shortages and associated price volatility affecting trucking operations?

- **Scope 3 Methodology Standards:** What specific calculation methodologies will be required for Scope 3 emissions reporting across different cargo types and trade lanes?

fullBrief:
I need to search for more specific information about IRENA's current regulatory frameworks and their impact on freight forwarding operations. Let me gather the necessary details.

Based on the search results, I can see that IRENA (International Renewable Energy Agency) is not actually a regulation but an intergovernmental organization that provides policy frameworks and guidance for renewable energy transition. However, I'll structure this as a regulatory intelligence brief focusing on IRENA's policy frameworks and their impact on freight forwarding operations, particularly in the Middle East context.

# IRENA Framework for Renewable Energy in Transport: Policy and Implementation Guidelines

**Regulatory Fact Document** | April 2026
**Primary source:** [International Renewable Energy Agency](https://www.irena.org/)

---

## What This Regulation Is and Why It Applies



IRENA supports countries in defining strategies for effective decarbonisation of all modes of transportation, including land, air and maritime based, with available and emerging renewable options.

 The International Renewable Energy Agency, established as 

an intergovernmental organisation established in 2011, IRENA promotes the widespread adoption and sustainable use of all forms of renewable energy, including bioenergy and geothermal, hydropower, ocean, solar and wind energy, in the pursuit of sustainable development, energy access, energy security and low-carbon economic growth

, operates as the primary global platform for renewable energy policy coordination.



Today, COP28 President Dr. Sultan Al Jaber officially tasked the International Renewable Energy Agency (IRENA) with the establishment of a special annual report series dedicated to monitoring progress and providing recommendations on achieving key energy goals of the UAE Consensus set at COP28. The new annual tracking report, which will be led, developed and published by IRENA, as official Custodian Agency, each year from 2024 to 2030, will feature the latest data and projections concerning the tripling of renewables and doubling of energy efficiency by 2030, providing timely and accurate inputs to future COP engagements.

 This mandate directly affects freight forwarders operating through Middle East hubs, particularly those handling cargo through UAE ports and airports.

For freight forwarding operations, IRENA's frameworks matter because they establish the policy foundation that national governments use to implement renewable energy requirements in transport sectors. 

The transport sector is responsible for close to a quarter of global energy-related CO2 emissions due to its heavy reliance on fossil fuels.

 

In 2022, international seaborne trade reached 11 billion tonnes, or over 80% of global trade by volume, mainly dominated by dry bulk, oil shipments and containerised trade

, making maritime freight a priority target for renewable energy transition policies.

**Source:** IRENA Statute (2009), UAE Consensus COP28 Agreement, IRENA Transport Sector Guidelines

---

## Issues Requiring Immediate Action

### Transport Mode Electrification Targets



Electric vehicles (EVs) would account for more than 80% of all road transport activity by 2050. Such a transition implies that close to a third of all light-duty vehicle sales and a fifth of truck sales over the current decade to 2030 should be electric.

 Road freight operators serving port and airport connections need immediate assessment of their fleet transition requirements.

**Action Required — Confirm for Your Business:** Review your road freight fleet composition and establish timeline for electric vehicle adoption in line with national implementation of IRENA guidelines.

### Green Fuel Availability Assessment



Total liquid biofuel production would need to grow almost five-fold by 2050, contributing 25% of the overall energy demand in the transport sector.

 

The report notes that renewable hydrogen and derived commodities could represent approximately 14% of final energy consumption by 2050, particularly in sectors such as chemical manufacturing, fertilizer production, and heavy-duty transport.



**Action Required — Confirm for Your Business:** Map current fuel sourcing for maritime and aviation partners to identify renewable fuel availability at key operational hubs.

### Middle East Hub Infrastructure Development



for extensive commercial and passenger links to connect Abu Dhabi with Dubai, Sharjah, Ras Al Khaimah and Fujairah by the end of 2024. Examples include the early-stage production of green hydrogen in Oman, Saudi Arabia and the United Arab Emirates; the development of biofuels for aviation in the United Arab Emirates



**Action Required — Confirm for Your Business:** Assess infrastructure development timelines at Middle East hub operations and align capacity planning with renewable energy infrastructure deployment.

### Client Sustainability Reporting Requirements



Forty-one out of 51 IRENA members that participated in a survey conducted in preparation for the next Nationally Determined Contributions (NDCs) submission echoed the need to improve policies and regulations to advance the energy transitions.



**Action Required — Confirm for Your Business:** Develop client reporting frameworks for renewable energy utilization in freight operations to meet emerging regulatory requirements.

---

## Operational Impact by Transport Mode

### Ocean Freight



At present, maritime shipping represents 80-90% of international trade. Therefore, if no action is taken promptly, demand for marine fossil fuels – and thus the associated carbon emissions – will continue to grow steadily. In fact, in the absence of suitable mitigation policies, the GHG emissions associated with the shipping sector could grow between 50% and 250% by 2050





The availability of sufficient sustainable biomass is not a hard constraint on biofuels playing an important role in decarbonising the shipping sector. IRENA suggests that the global technical potential for sustainable advanced biofuels is at least 114 EJ, roughly 11 times the energy demand for shipping. In the medium- to long-term, e-fuels produced from green hydrogen – in the form of e-methanol, e-ammonia and e-methane – could also play a significant role in the shipping sector's decarbonisation.



Ocean freight operations will face increasing fuel surcharges as carriers transition to renewable fuels. Green fuel premiums are expected to be passed through to freight rates via environmental adjustment factors (EAF) similar to current bunker adjustment factors (BAF).

### Air Freight

Aviation decarbonization relies heavily on sustainable aviation fuels (SAF) and operational efficiency improvements. 

the development of biofuels for aviation in the United Arab Emirates

 indicates significant infrastructure development in key Middle East air freight hubs.

Air cargo surcharges for sustainable fuels will likely follow IATA's environmental cost pass-through mechanisms, with dedicated line items for renewable fuel costs appearing in air waybills.

### Road Freight



Countries are urged to set ambitious electric vehicles (EV) targets and clear timeline aligned with the Paris Agreement to phase out fossil fuel use in road transport, sending signals to investors and carmakers. Electric vehicles and charging infrastructures will need to play a major role in driving an accelerated transition.



Road freight connecting ports and airports to final destinations faces immediate electrification pressure, requiring investment in electric vehicle fleets and charging infrastructure.

---

# United Nations Sustainable Development Goals 9 & 13: Industry, Innovation & Infrastructure and Climate Action

**Regulatory Fact Document** | April 2026
**Primary source:** [UN Sustainable Development Goals](https://sdgs.un.org/goals)

---

## What This Regulation Is and Why It Applies



UN Sustainable Development Goals 9 and 13, adopted by all 193 UN Member States in September 2015 as part of the 2030 Agenda for Sustainable Development, provide the universal framework that underpins national sustainability legislation affecting freight forwarding operations globally

. Unlike direct regulations, these are framework goals that create the policy architecture driving national and regional legislation such as the EU Green Deal, Carbon Border Adjustment Mechanism, and emerging sustainability disclosure requirements worldwide.



SDG 9 focuses on building resilient infrastructure, promoting inclusive and sustainable industrialization, and fostering innovation, recognizing that more than 80% of world merchandise trade by volume is transported by sea, making maritime transport a critical enabler of trade and globalization

. 

SDG 13 calls for urgent action to combat climate change and its impacts, requiring the world to achieve net zero carbon dioxide emissions by around 2050 to limit global warming to 1.5°C above pre-industrial levels in accordance with the Paris Agreement

.

For freight forwarders, these SDGs matter operationally because they drive the regulatory landscape that creates compliance costs, reporting requirements, and competitive pressures. 

Research shows that while the maritime industry's primary contribution is to SDG 8 (27%), it also significantly contributes to SDG 9 (12%) and has relatively fewer sustainability efforts put toward SDG 13 (8%)

. This gap creates regulatory risk as governments increase pressure on transport operators to align with climate targets.

**Source:** UN Resolution 70/1 "Transforming our world: the 2030 Agenda for Sustainable Development"

---

## Issues Requiring Immediate Action

### National Reporting Requirements Now Being Implemented



IMO is planning to develop maritime specific indicator fact sheets, supporting Member States undertaking Voluntary National Reviews (VNRs), to include sustainable maritime policies in these

. 

For SDG reporting, cargo type totals of maritime freight loaded (exports) and unloaded (imports) are first summed up per country before being aggregated up to the regional total

.

**Action Required — Confirm for Your Business:** Verify which national SDG reporting requirements your operations fall under, particularly for SDG 9.1.2 (passenger and freight volumes by mode of transport) and whether your volumes contribute to national statistics that governments use for UN reporting.

### Client Sustainability Disclosure Demands Accelerating



Major shipping companies like Maersk have set ambitious targets to reduce CO2 emissions and improve fuel efficiency, aiming to achieve carbon neutrality by 2050, investing in research and development for zero-emission vessels and exploring alternative fuels

. This creates cascading pressure on freight forwarders to provide SDG-aligned services.

**Action Required — Confirm for Your Business:** Audit your largest clients to determine which have made net-zero commitments or SDG reporting obligations that require freight emissions data from service providers.

### Technology Investment Requirements for Infrastructure Resilience



Building resilient infrastructure is central to the effective functioning of the whole shipping sector, with IMO contributing to SDG 9 by providing a legal and regulatory framework, while new, eco-friendly technologies help shipping contribute to this goal, bringing significant beneficial changes in the way ships are designed, constructed and operated

.

**Action Required — Confirm for Your Business:** Assess your current technology stack against SDG 9 resilient infrastructure requirements and determine investment needs for track-and-trace, emissions calculation, and route optimization capabilities.

### UN Decade of Sustainable Transport (2026-2035) Implementation



The Implementation Plan for the United Nations Decade of Sustainable Transport (2026–2035), mandated through General Assembly resolution 78/148, will be a critical tool in steering global efforts towards sustainable, inclusive, and resilient transportation systems, serving as a strategic framework to coordinate actions, mobilize resources, and monitor progress towards sustainable transport worldwide

.

**Action Required — Confirm for Your Business:** Review the Implementation Plan to identify upcoming policy developments that may affect freight forwarding operations during the 2026-2035 period.

---

## Operational Impact by Transport Mode

### Ocean Freight Operations



While ocean transport is relatively efficient (emitting only 10 to 40 grammes of CO2 per metric tonne per kilometre versus 500 grammes for air freight), shipping faces sustainability challenges from its reliance on low-quality petroleum-based bunker fuel, resulting in emissions of both CO2 and harmful air pollutants, with shipping accounting for 2.5% of total CO2 emissions

.

**Cost Pass-Through Mechanism:** 

Ships currently use around 15% of their total fuel per voyage whilst in port or in a harbor

, creating port efficiency pressures that affect dwell time charges and demurrage costs.

**SDG 9 Infrastructure Impact:** 

World container port traffic reflects the importance of containerized trade and countries' participation in global liner shipping networks, while also providing an indication of port infrastructure size and quality and informing about the position of countries as major maritime trading nations and homes to global logistics hubs

.

### Air Freight Operations



Air shipping and air travel are important components of physical infrastructure, with the estimated global economic impact (both direct and indirect) of air transport at $2.7 trillion in 2015, equivalent to 3.5% of global GDP, though least developed countries represent far less air travel and freight volumes

.

**Reporting Requirements:** 

The International Civil Aviation Organization (ICAO) has established standard methodologies and definitions to collect and report traffic data related to air transport, adopted by 193 Member States and industry stakeholders, using Air Transport Reporting Forms A, AS, B and C to arrive at passenger and freight volumes

.

### Road Transport and Last-Mile Delivery



Technological improvement in road transport, allowing more efficient navigation, is directly linked to SDG 9, while SDG 11 calls for safer cities and human settlements by reducing air quality issues and monitoring waste management, also implying the reduction of water pollution and ocean pollution

.

### Customs and Trade Documentation



International maritime freight volumes are generated based on UN Comtrade, which collects trade flow data from UN member countries, though container port traffic data are not based on systematic reporting by countries and rely mainly on secondary sources, with official reporting by countries being very limited

.

fullBrief:
# IPCC Climate Reports: Assessment Reports and Scientific Foundation for Global Climate Policy

**Regulatory Fact Document** | April 2026
**Primary source:** [Intergovernmental Panel on Climate Change Reports](https://www.ipcc.ch/)

---

## What This Regulation Is and Why It Applies

The Intergovernmental Panel on Climate Change (IPCC) is the authoritative UN body for assessing climate science, established jointly by the United Nations Environment Programme (UNEP) and the World Meteorological Organization (WMO) in 1988. 

It was established by the United Nations Environment Programme (UNEP) and the World Meteorological Organization (WMO) in 1988 to provide political leaders with periodic scientific assessments concerning climate change, its implications and risks, as well as to put forward adaptation and mitigation strategies.



While IPCC reports are not direct regulations, they function as the scientific foundation driving all climate regulations that affect international freight forwarding. 

Increases in demand notwithstanding, scenarios that limit warming to 1.5°C with no or limited overshoot suggest that a 59% reduction (42–68% interquartile range) in transport-related CO2 emissions by 2050, compared to modelled 2020 levels is required.

 The Sixth Assessment Report (AR6), completed in 2021-2023, established the carbon budgets and sectoral emission reduction pathways that directly inform the International Maritime Organization's net-zero shipping targets by 2050, the EU's Fit for 55 package, and national climate legislation worldwide.

For freight forwarders, IPCC reports matter because they set the scientific ambition level that flows down into specific operational requirements. 

IMO member states agreed: to reach net-zero GHG emissions from international shipping by around 2050, with interim checkpoints of 20-30 percent by 2030 and 70-80 percent by 2040

 — targets derived directly from IPCC pathway analysis. 

The initial strategy wasn't compatible with the Paris Agreement's aim to limit global warming to well below 2°C and pursuing efforts to limit it to 1.5°C. By our new estimates of the revised strategy, international shipping will exceed its current share of the world's 1.5°C carbon budget by approximately 2032

 Understanding these scientific foundations explains why regulatory tightening is accelerating faster than industry expected and why clients with science-based targets reference specific IPCC scenarios.

**Source:** IPCC AR6 Working Group III Chapter 10: Transport; 2023 IMO GHG Strategy

---

## Issues Requiring Immediate Action

### Monitor AR7 Development Timeline and Freight Transport Chapter



The three IPCC Working Group reports are expected to start appearing in mid-2028, while the Synthesis Report that will conclude the entire cycle will be approved by late 2029, completing the seventh assessment cycle. The authors, from more than 100 countries, will focus their work on the initial drafts of the three Working Group contributions to AR7 and cross-cutting topics.

 The transport chapter in AR7 Working Group III will update the 59% freight emission reduction pathway and may strengthen requirements based on latest climate science.

**Action Required — Confirm for Your Business:** Assign responsibility for monitoring AR7 transport findings to ensure strategy team understands evolving scientific basis for client decarbonization targets and regulatory development.

### Track Client Science-Based Target Dependencies on IPCC Pathways

IPCC scenarios directly determine the emission reduction trajectories that corporate clients must follow for Science Based Targets initiative (SBTi) validation. 

For scenarios aiming to limit warming to 1.5°C with minimal or no overshoot, a reduction of 59% in CO2 emissions from transportation by 2050 is deemed necessary compared to modeled levels from 2020.

 Changes in AR7 methodology or pathway strictness will flow through to client procurement requirements and freight budget allocation.

**Action Required — Confirm for Your Business:** Review which major clients have SBTi-approved targets and understand their current emission reduction timeline assumptions to anticipate procurement requirement changes when AR7 is published.

### Prepare for Regulatory Tightening Based on Remaining Carbon Budget



Under the revised (2023) strategy, we're set to exceed the 1.5°C budget by approximately 2032 under either the 20% or 30% (striving) target; however, if shipping can get to zero WTW CO2e emissions by 2050 along this pathway, it will not exceed its well below 2°C budget.

 The shrinking carbon budget explains the acceleration in regulatory measures — from IMO's net-zero target to EU ETS inclusion of shipping.

**Action Required — Confirm for Your Business:** Evaluate how regulatory acceleration based on carbon budget constraints affects capital investment decisions for fleet decarbonization and alternative fuel infrastructure.

### Understand IPCC Scientific Authority in Regulatory Development



As United Nations Secretary-General Antonio Guterres has said, "We need to put the brake on deadly greenhouse gas emissions and drive climate action…. The world is counting on all of us to rise to the challenge before it's too late" Research and development will be crucial, as the targets agreed in the 2023 IMO GHG Strategy will not be met using fossil fuels. There is a need to make zero-carbon ships more attractive and to direct investments towards innovative sustainable technologies and alternative fuels.



**Action Required — Confirm for Your Business:** Assign team members to understand how IPCC pathway analysis translates into specific regulatory proposals affecting your freight operations.

---

## Operational Impact by Transport Mode

### Ocean Freight

The IPCC transport pathway analysis directly informed the 2023 IMO GHG Strategy targets. 

The IMO's 2023 Strategy sets out a clear roadmap for reducing greenhouse gas emissions from global shipping, with targets to reduce emissions by at least 20% (striving for 30%) by 2030, at least 70% (striving for 80%) by 2040, both in comparison to 2008 levels; and to achieve net-zero emissions by or around 2050.

 

The pricing mechanism will apply to a share of international shipping emissions from 2028, with an initial price of USD 100 per tonne of CO2. This will generate significant revenue, estimated to be USD 11-13 billion annually, which will be used to support the development and deployment of these zero- and near-zero emission fuels.



Ocean freight forwarders face direct cost pass-through from IMO measures based on IPCC-derived targets. The well-to-wake GHG fuel standard entering force in 2027 will create fuel surcharges. 

To achieve the minimum IMO targets, the GHG intensity of marine fuels will have to reduce by 18% to 76 gCO2e/MJ by 2030 and by 72% to 26 gCO2e/MJ in 2040 compared to the 2019 baseline. For the "striving" scenario, reductions in 2030 and 2040 will have to be 28% to 67 gCO2e and 81% to 17 gCO2e/MJ, respectively.



### Air Freight



In general terms, electrification tends to play the key role in land-based transport, but biofuels and hydrogen (and derivatives) could play a role in decarbonisation of freight in some contexts. Biofuels and hydrogen (and derivatives) are likely more prominent in shipping and aviation.

 IPCC analysis shows aviation faces the steepest decarbonization challenge, requiring sustainable aviation fuels and hydrogen technologies that are still in development phase.

IPCC pathways indicate aviation will require significant fuel cost increases to meet climate targets. The 59% transport sector reduction pathway means air freight must offset growth through dramatic fuel switching, creating cost volatility and supply chain disruption risks.

### Road and Rail Freight



Across all 1.5°C aligned pathways, energy demand in the transport sector declines by 49–60% below 2021 levels by 2050. This indicates modal shifts from road to rail in both passenger and freight transport and efficiency gains from electrification are key to driving down transport emissions.

 IPCC analysis drives regulatory push for modal shift from road to rail freight through carbon pricing and infrastructure investment.



Overall, the mitigation potential of urban planning is about 25% in 2050 compared with a business-as-usual scenario (Creutzig et al. 2015a; Creutzig et al. 2015b).

 Urban freight consolidation requirements and low emission zones reflect IPCC recommendations for demand management and efficiency improvements.

---

| Remaining global carbon budget for 1.5°C | ~500 GtCO2 (50% probability) | IPCC AR6 |
| IMO net-zero shipping target | By or around 2050 | 2023 IMO GHG Strategy |
| IMO interim reduction targets | 20-30% by 2030; 70-80% by 2040 | 2023 IMO GHG Strategy |
| IMO carbon pricing mechanism | USD 100/tonne CO2 from 2028 | IMO Net-Zero Framework |
| AR7 Working Group reports expected | Mid-2028 | IPCC AR7 timeline |
| AR7 Synthesis Report release | Late 2029 | IPCC AR7 timeline |
| Alternative fuel target for shipping | 5-10% of energy mix by 2030 | 2023 IMO GHG Strategy |
| Marine fuel GHG intensity reduction | 18% by 2030; 72% by 2040 | ICCT analysis |

---

## Open Questions

- **AR7 Transport Chapter Methodology Changes:** How will AR7 update the 59% freight emission reduction pathway and what new sectoral requirements will emerge? Depends on completion of AR7 Working Group III transport analysis.

- **Carbon Budget Allocation Updates:** Will AR7 revise the remaining carbon budget calculations that drive regulatory urgency? Depends on latest climate observations and carbon cycle modeling in AR7 Working Group I.

- **Modal Shift Quantification:** How precisely will AR7 quantify required modal shift from road to rail freight to meet climate targets? Depends on transport demand modeling methodology in AR7.

- **International vs. Domestic Freight Attribution:** How will AR7 treat emission accounting boundaries between international and domestic freight operations? Depends on AR7 Working Group III sectoral boundary definitions.

- **Technology Readiness Assessment:** Will AR7 update assumptions about alternative fuel technology deployment timelines that inform regulatory feasibility? Depends on AR7 technology assessment methodology.

- **Developing Country Pathway Differentiation:** How will AR7 address freight emission reduction pathways in developing countries vs. developed countries? Depends on equity considerations in AR7 Working Group III.

fullBrief:
# IEA Policies & Measures Database: Global Transport and Energy Policy Intelligence Framework

**Regulatory Fact Document** | April 2026
**Primary source:** [IEA Policies Database](https://www.iea.org/policies/about)

---

## What This Regulation Is and Why It Applies

The International Energy Agency (IEA) Policies & Measures Database is not a regulation itself, but rather a comprehensive intelligence framework that 

provides access to information on past, existing or planned government policies and measures to reduce greenhouse gas emissions, improve energy efficiency and support the development and deployment of renewables and other clean energy technologies

 across more than 150+ countries globally. This database serves as the world's most authoritative source for tracking energy and transport policy implementation status, enforcement mechanisms, and compliance requirements that directly affect international freight forwarding operations.

For freight forwarders operating across multiple jurisdictions, this database functions as a critical operational intelligence tool. When your company handles shipments between countries, each jurisdiction may have different environmental compliance requirements, fuel standards, emissions reporting obligations, and sustainability mandates. The IEA database tracks these policy developments in real-time, showing implementation status, penalty structures, and enforcement timelines that can affect cargo routing decisions, fuel surcharges, and client contractual obligations.

The database's significance has expanded dramatically with 

the share of energy covered by fuel economy and/or vehicle efficiency policies having more than doubled over the past two decades, from less than 25% of road transport energy consumption covered in 2000 to more than half in 2022. About 50 countries now have fuel economy and/or vehicle efficiency standards for light-duty vehicles in place, and nearly 40 (including EU member states) have such standards for medium- and heavy-duty vehicles

. This policy proliferation means freight forwarders must navigate an increasingly complex web of compliance requirements that vary by country, transport mode, and cargo type.

**Source:** IEA Transport Energy System Analysis, World Energy Outlook 2024

---

## Issues Requiring Immediate Action

### Maritime Decarbonization Compliance Tracking


The revised emissions reduction targets recently announced by the International Maritime Organization (IMO) are now in line with the goals set out in the Paris Agreement, legally binding measures for the implementation of the revised strategy will be needed to steer the maritime shipping sector onto a trajectory consistent with the Net Zero Emissions by 2050 (NZE) Scenario, which requires an almost 15% reduction in emissions from 2022 to 2030

. Ocean freight forwarders must monitor 150+ country implementations of IMO guidelines through the IEA database to identify new fuel quality requirements, emissions reporting obligations, and port-specific environmental fees.

**Action Required — Confirm for Your Business:** Review your ocean routing patterns against IEA database entries for port states and flag state requirements. Identify which routes may face new bunker fuel specifications or emissions measurement obligations by Q2 2026.

### Road Freight Electrification Mandates


Governments should commit to a Paris-aligned timeline by which all new road vehicle sales should be zero-emission, particularly for trucks, buses, and vans, backed by interim targets and clear implementation plans by 2030 and 2035

. The IEA database shows accelerating implementation of truck electrification mandates across developed economies, with direct implications for drayage operations and last-mile delivery partnerships.

**Action Required — Confirm for Your Business:** Audit your road freight partnerships in EU, North American, and APAC markets. Determine which carriers face electric vehicle mandates and how transition costs will flow through to freight forwarding rates.

### Cross-Border Carbon Accounting Requirements


Internationally aligned standards, regulations and targets will be critical to reducing CO2 emissions from international aviation and shipping, particularly as these sectors are outside the scope of all countries' UNFCCC pledges. As such, agreements on stringent and binding regulations will need to be agreed by the UN agencies that co‑ordinate such actions for these sectors: the International Civil Aviation Organization (ICAO) and the International Maritime Organization (IMO)

. Multiple countries are implementing carbon border adjustment mechanisms that require freight forwarders to provide transport-related emissions data.

**Action Required — Confirm for Your Business:** Establish procedures to extract transport emissions data by route and mode from the IEA database. Prepare for client requests for Scope 3 emissions reporting on shipments crossing carbon-regulated borders.

### Alternative Fuel Infrastructure Requirements


Governments should co-operate to accelerate the roll-out of charging infrastructure along international freight corridors for zero-emission trucks. Governments should strengthen joint planning and co-ordinated investment across the transport and power sectors to anticipate greater demand for zero-emission power generation, transmission, and distribution for charging

. The IEA database tracks national commitments for alternative fuel infrastructure deployment that affect route feasibility and fuel availability.

**Action Required — Confirm for Your Business:** Map your major freight corridors against IEA infrastructure deployment timelines. Identify potential service disruptions or route modifications needed as conventional fuel availability decreases in certain corridors.

---

## Operational Impact by Transport Mode

### Ocean Freight
The IEA database reveals accelerating maritime policy implementation that directly affects ocean freight operations. 

As part of their multipronged strategy to reduce emissions from shipping, in 2023 the European Union reached two important agreements concerning the inclusion of shipping in the EU Emissions Trading System (ETS) and the FuelEU Maritime initiative

. Ocean freight forwarders must track policy implementation across 150+ countries to understand:

- **Fuel Quality Mandates:** New low-sulfur and renewable fuel requirements by port
- **Emissions Trading Costs:** ETS and carbon pricing mechanisms that affect bunker costs
- **Port Environmental Fees:** Green port initiatives creating new surcharge structures
- **Vessel Efficiency Standards:** Technical requirements affecting vessel selection and routing

Cost pass-through mechanisms include bunker adjustment factors (BAF) that incorporate carbon pricing, environmental surcharges at regulated ports, and premium charges for vessels meeting higher efficiency standards. Freight forwarders should prepare for 15-25% increases in ocean freight costs on routes subject to new environmental regulations.

### Air Freight


Policies such as those proposed (and nearing finalisation) by the European Union – namely ReFuelEU Aviation and FuelEU Maritime – set the right example by proposing ambitious targets based (at least in part) on the performance metric that matters: the emissions incurred by producing, delivering and using the fuel. Based on analysis solicited from multiple research and technical organisations (including the IEA), ICAO has developed a Long-Term Aspirational Goal that will inform future discussion on how in-sector emission reductions can be realised

. Air freight operations face:

- **Sustainable Aviation Fuel (SAF) Mandates:** Percentage requirements increasing from 2% in 2025 to 20% by 2035 in EU markets
- **Carbon Offsetting Requirements:** CORSIA implementation requiring offset purchases for international flights
- **Airport Environmental Fees:** Landing and handling charges based on aircraft emissions performance
- **Route Optimization Mandates:** Requirements for fuel-efficient flight path planning

Air freight surcharges will reflect SAF cost premiums (currently 2-5x conventional jet fuel), carbon offset costs ($15-40 per tonne CO2), and environmental handling fees at regulated airports. Prepare for 10-15% increases in air freight rates on regulated routes.

### Road Freight


Greater attention should be placed on heavy and medium duty vehicles and focused on assistance for emerging markets. Governments should commit to a Paris-aligned timeline by which all new road vehicle sales should be zero-emission, particularly for trucks, buses, and vans, backed by interim targets and clear implementation plans by 2030 and 2035

. Road freight faces the most immediate regulatory pressure:

- **Electric Vehicle Mandates:** Requirements for zero-emission trucks in urban areas (London, Paris, Amsterdam from 2025)
- **Diesel Restrictions:** Phased bans on diesel trucks in city centers affecting last-mile delivery
- **Infrastructure Access:** Priority lanes and loading zones for electric and alternative fuel vehicles
- **Fleet Reporting:** Mandatory emissions reporting for commercial vehicle fleets

Road freight cost impacts include electric vehicle lease premiums, charging infrastructure access fees, and potential diesel access charges in restricted zones. Drayage costs may increase 20-30% in regulated urban areas.

### Customs and Reporting
The IEA database integration with national customs systems creates new reporting requirements:

- **Transport Emissions Documentation:** Customs declarations requiring transport-mode emissions data
- **Fuel Type Verification:** Documentation of fuel standards compliance for cross-border movements
- **Carbon Content Declarations:** Product carbon footprint including transport emissions for CBAM compliance
- **Alternative Fuel Certificates:** Proof of renewable fuel usage for preferential treatment

## Open Questions

- **IMO Implementation Mechanisms:** How will the 15% maritime emissions reduction target be enforced at national level? The IEA database shows policy announcements but lacks detail on penalty structures and enforcement authority allocation between flag states and port states.

- **Alternative Fuel Certification Standards:** What mutual recognition agreements will exist between countries for alternative fuel certificates? The database shows national policies but cross-border acceptance mechanisms remain undefined.

- **Small Island Developing States Compliance:** How will SIDS with limited administrative capacity implement IEA-tracked policies? The database includes these countries but implementation feasibility assessments are lacking.

- **Enforcement Coordination:** When multiple IEA-tracked policies apply to a single shipment (e.g., EU ETS, FuelEU Maritime, port environmental fees), how will enforcement agencies coordinate to avoid double penalties?

- **Digital Infrastructure Integration:** How will national customs systems integrate with IEA policy requirements for real-time compliance verification? Current database structure assumes manual policy review rather than automated compliance checking.

fullBrief:
I'll search for current information about World Bank transport policies, financing programs, and their impact on freight infrastructure to create a comprehensive intelligence brief.

Based on my research, I can see that the World Bank's transport initiatives and policies represent a complex global development framework rather than a single regulation. The search results reveal comprehensive information about the World Bank's transport financing programs, environmental and social standards, and infrastructure development initiatives. I'll now create a full intelligence brief structured as a regulatory analysis for freight forwarders.

# World Bank Transport Financing and Environmental and Social Framework (ESF)

**Regulatory Fact Document** | April 2026
**Primary source:** [World Bank Environmental and Social Framework](https://www.worldbank.org/en/projects-operations/environmental-and-social-framework)

---

## What This Regulation Is and Why It Applies

The World Bank Transport division operates through a comprehensive development financing framework that directly impacts global freight infrastructure and operations through multiple mechanisms. 

The Environmental and Social Framework (ESF) was approved by the Board of Executive Directors on August 4, 2016. It consists of a Vision for Sustainable Development; ten Environmental and Social Standards (ESSs), which set out the requirements that apply to Borrowers; an Environmental and Social Policy for Investment Project Financing (IPF), which sets out the requirements that apply to the Bank; and an Environmental and Social Directive/Procedure for Investment Project Financing (IPF) and a Directive on Addressing Risks and Impacts on Disadvantaged or Vulnerable Individuals or Groups.

 

It applies to all IPF projects initiated on or after October 1, 2018.





With transport financing commitments in Fiscal Year 2024 (July 1, 2023 to June 30, 2024) reaching $5 billion, the World Bank is the largest financier of sustainable transport projects in LMICs. In recent years, the World Bank's lending portfolio has shifted towards low-carbon mobility solutions—from 37% of all transport financing in Fiscal Year 2017 (FY17) to 53% in FY24.

 This affects freight forwarders by influencing the quality, capacity, and regulatory environment of transport infrastructure in emerging markets that serve global supply chains. World Bank-funded projects create new corridors, upgrade port facilities, and implement environmental and social compliance requirements that freight operators must navigate.



The World Bank's strategy in the transport sector, and companion business plan for the next three years (2016-18), aims to facilitate the movement of people, goods and ideas in developing countries by focusing on mobility solutions that provide greater access, efficiency and safety, all in a climate-friendly way.



**Source:** World Bank Environmental and Social Framework documentation; World Bank Transport Strategy 2016-2024

---

## Issues Requiring Immediate Action

### Infrastructure Due Diligence Requirements



Investment decisions increasingly include an assessment of the management of E&S risks and impacts—not only when MDBs and international financial institutions are involved but also when commercial banks and private equity funds are the source of financing. Furthermore, in many developing countries international players require compliance with both national laws and international E&S standards developed by MDBs, which are sometimes more stringent than those imbedded in national legislation.



**Action Required — Confirm for Your Business:** Freight forwarders using infrastructure in World Bank project countries must verify whether their service providers and facilities comply with World Bank Environmental and Social Standards, as these may be more stringent than local requirements and affect operational permissions.

### Climate Resilience Planning



Part of these expenses involve maintaining transport systems that already exist, as natural hazards cause an average of $15 billion a year in direct damage to global transport infrastructure. Another part goes into building new systems, which often come with hefty price tags.

 

Climate-related disasters, pandemics, conflicts, and other challenges increasingly threaten transport infrastructure and services. Developing resilient transport systems that can withstand, adapt to, and quickly recover from such shocks is crucial.



**Action Required — Confirm for Your Business:** Review contingency planning for routes through World Bank project countries, particularly those upgrading climate resilience. Service disruptions during infrastructure upgrades may require alternative routing strategies.

### Green Logistics Compliance Assessment



Green logistics: The logistics sector—shipping, aviation, railway transport, and heavy-duty trucking—must both reduce its environmental footprint and meet rising global demand for freight transport. This session will explore how fuel-efficient technologies, sustainable practices, and innovative logistics management can make the supply chain more efficient, green, and resilient.



**Action Required — Confirm for Your Business:** Assess current sustainability reporting capabilities and green logistics practices. Clients with World Bank-financed operations increasingly require environmental compliance documentation and carbon footprint reporting.

### Payment Security Mechanisms for Infrastructure Projects



Additionally, the World Bank helped conceptualize, and is currently supporting the operationalization of a payment security mechanism aimed at mitigating financial risks for lenders and facilitating access to financing for e-buses. Building on those solutions, the government of India plans to scale up the aggregated procurement model across the country, with a goal of securing approximately 50,000 electric buses over the next two years.



**Action Required — Confirm for Your Business:** Monitor payment security arrangements for freight services in countries implementing World Bank transport modernization programs, particularly India, Senegal, and Western Balkan states.

---

## Operational Impact by Transport Mode

### Ocean Freight



Port Said (Egypt, Arab Rep.) is among the most improved container ports between 2020 and 2024, now ranked 3rd globally, and 1st among its regional peers. Egypt's overall trade logistics have also improved, with the country ranked 57th of 139 in the World Bank's 2023 Logistics Performance Index, highlighting broader progress in port operations and hinterland connectivity that benefited Port Said.

 World Bank port improvement projects directly affect container dwell times and operational efficiency. 

A major expansion of the Suez Canal Container Terminal (SCCT) at East Port Said is underway, supported by a loan from the International Finance Corporation (IFC). The expansion aims to increase the terminal's capacity by an additional 2.1 million TEU, resulting in a total installed capacity of

 enhanced port operations.

**Surcharge Implications:** Enhanced port efficiency may reduce demurrage costs but could introduce sustainability-related terminal handling charges. IFC-financed port improvements typically implement environmental standards requiring additional documentation and potentially higher handling fees for non-compliant cargo.

### Rail Freight



In India, three Eastern Dedicated Freight Corridor (EDFC) projects led to the construction of 1,200 km of newly built tracks, from 2011 to 2024. Each kilometer-long freight train on the EDFC replaces about 72 trucks, helping to reduce highway congestion, improve safety, and lower air pollution. This shift is expected to save India approximately Rs 4.9 billion ($58 million) in logistics costs and Rs 2.4 billion ($28 million) in pollution-related health and environmental costs, over the lifetime of the project.



**Cost Pass-Through:** World Bank rail infrastructure investments create modal shift opportunities with significant cost savings. Freight forwarders should reassess India corridor pricing as dedicated freight rail becomes operational, potentially offering 20-30% cost reductions versus road transport.

### Road Transport



Reducing the distance for a typical food shipment in low- and middle-income countries by 100 kilometers can lower transport costs by about 20%. Using highways instead of smaller roads also cuts costs by 19%.

 

It is 2024 and still one billion people live more than two kilometers away from an all-weather road, one in six women worldwide don't job search out of fear of harassment on transit, and road crashes claim over 1 million lives every year, a whopping 92 percent of which are in developing countries.



**Safety and Insurance Implications:** World Bank road safety initiatives in developing countries affect insurance premiums and route risk assessments. Enhanced safety standards may reduce insurance costs but require compliance with new safety protocols.

### Border and Customs Operations



These projects cover key priorities including railway safety, smart mobility, road safety, and upgrades at border crossing points. SSTP works in close partnership with the European Commission (DG ENEST), the Transport Community Permanent Secretariat (TCPS), EU Delegations, and Western Balkan line ministries and ministries of finance.



**Processing Time Impact:** World Bank border crossing upgrades in Western Balkans expected to reduce customs clearance times by 30-50% but may require new documentation standards and electronic filing systems.

---

---

## Recommended Actions

| 2 | Establish green logistics documentation and carbon reporting capabilities | Sustainability, IT | Q3 2026 |
| 3 | Review insurance and risk assessments for World Bank infrastructure corridors | Finance, Operations | Q2 2026 |
| 4 | Develop modal shift analysis for India EDFC and similar rail projects | Ocean Product, Air Product | Q3 2026 |
| 5 | Create ESF compliance checklist for supplier and partner due diligence | Legal, Procurement | Q2 2026 |
| 6 | Monitor Western Balkans border crossing upgrades for operational improvements | Customs, Operations | Ongoing |
| 7 | Establish relationship with World Bank transport project notifications | Sustainability, Operations | Q2 2026 |

---

## Implementation Timeline

| October 1, 2018 | ESF applies to all new IPF projects | In force |
| 2024-2025 | Major rail infrastructure completions (India EDFC Phase 3) | Upcoming |
| 2025-2027 | Western Balkans Safe and Sustainable Transport Programme Phase 2 | Upcoming |
| 2026 | Expected completion of Suez Canal Container Terminal expansion | Upcoming |
| 2030 | World Bank guarantee issuances target: $20 billion annually | Pending |
| 2030 | India electric vehicle target: 30% share of new vehicle sales | Pending |
| 2050 | Global transport net-zero target aligned with Paris Agreement | Pending |

---

## Open Questions

- **Environmental and Social Standards application scope:** Uncertainty about whether ESF requirements apply to private freight operators using World Bank-financed infrastructure, or only to direct project implementers. Depends on specific project loan agreements and national implementing legislation.

- **Green logistics definition standardization:** No unified definition of "green logistics" requirements across World Bank projects. Individual country implementations may have different sustainability reporting and compliance requirements.

- **Modal shift impact on service agreements:** Unclear how rapid infrastructure improvements (particularly India rail corridors) will affect existing long-term freight service contracts and pricing mechanisms. Depends on completion timelines and operational capacity ramp-up.

- **Climate resilience investment prioritization:** World Bank climate adaptation spending allocation between regions not yet determined for 2026-2030 programming cycle. Could significantly affect infrastructure quality and service reliability in various corridors.

- **Private sector mobilization mechanisms:** Specific terms and conditions for the $20 billion annual guarantee target by 2030 not yet published. Could create new financing options or requirements for freight infrastructure users.

---

fullBrief:
# International Transport Forum (ITF) Transport Outlook and Research Initiative

**Regulatory Fact Document** | April 2026
**Primary source:** [International Transport Forum at the OECD](https://www.itf-oecd.org/)

---

## What This Regulation Is and Why It Applies



The International Transport Forum at the OECD is an intergovernmental organisation with 69 member countries.

 

The International Transport Forum is administratively integrated into the OECD (Organisation for Economic Co-operation and Development), but is politically autonomous and has its own governance structure.

 

It acts as a think tank for transport policy and organises the Annual Summit of transport ministers.

 

ITF is the only global body that covers all transport modes.





The ITF Transport Outlook provides an overview of recent trends and near-term prospects for the transport sector at a global level as well as long-term prospects for transport demand to 2050. Based on cutting-edge ITF modelling, it provides decision makers in government and industry with coherent scenarios for future demand in passenger mobility, freight volumes and transport-related CO2 emissions to 2050.

 

Consolidated findings are presented in ITF's flagship report, the ITF Transport Outlook, published every other year as part of the OECD Outlook series.



This applies to freight forwarders globally because 

transport demand will grow in the years to come. By 2050, passenger demand will increase by 79% under the Current Ambition scenario and freight demand will roughly double.

 The ITF's transport decarbonisation research and policy recommendations directly impact freight forwarding operations through modal shift economics, infrastructure investment priorities, and carbon pricing mechanisms. 

Transport emits around 23% of the energy-related CO2 that feeds global warming. Without immediate action, its share could reach 40% by 2030.

 This creates direct regulatory pressure on freight operations through carbon taxation, emission trading schemes, and mode-specific decarbonisation requirements.

**Source:** [ITF Transport Outlook Project](https://www.itf-oecd.org/itf-transport-outlook-project), [ITF Decarbonising Transport Initiative](https://www.itf-oecd.org/decarbonising-transport)

---

## Issues Requiring Immediate Action

### Freight Volume Planning to 2050



By 2050, freight demand will roughly double under the Current Ambition scenario and increase by 59% under the High Ambition scenario.

 Freight forwarders must incorporate these projections into their long-term capacity planning, terminal investments, and service portfolio development.

**Action Required — Confirm for Your Business:** Review current capacity planning models against ITF's freight volume projections and assess whether your 2030-2050 expansion plans align with expected modal demand shifts.

### Carbon Pricing Impact Assessment



Carbon pricing can encourage a move away from the most carbon-intensive fleets and make low-carbon fuels more cost-competitive.

 

Freight mode choice is mostly unresponsive to pricing measures. The exceptions are road and port access modes in multimodal trips. Coherent pricing policies can ensure that the most sustainable of the viable modes are chosen.



**Action Required — Confirm for Your Business:** Evaluate your current surcharge structure's ability to pass through carbon pricing costs by transport mode, particularly for road and port access components of multimodal shipments.

### Infrastructure Investment Implications



Regardless of the pathway chosen, the transport system will require significant investment in the coming decades. Core infrastructure investment needs to meet projected demand are estimated at 1.7% of global GDP annually through to 2050 under the Current Ambition scenario.



**Action Required — Confirm for Your Business:** Assess which trade lanes and transport corridors are likely to receive priority infrastructure investment based on ITF modelling, and align your network expansion accordingly.

### Decarbonisation Technology Adoption



The initiative identifies effective policies for decarbonising urban passenger transport, road freight transport, maritime transport, aviation and inter-urban transport.

 

Goods transport by road consumes around 50% of all diesel fuel and accounts for 80% of the global net increase in diesel use since 2000. Projections see road freight activity at least doubling to 2050, offsetting efficiency gains and increasing road freight CO2 emissions.



**Action Required — Confirm for Your Business:** Develop transition plans for electric/hydrogen vehicles in your road freight operations and assess the timeline for sustainable fuel adoption in your maritime and aviation supply chains.

---

## Operational Impact by Transport Mode

### Ocean Transport



For the maritime and aviation sectors, developing sustainable and affordable fuels, in sufficient quantities, will be crucial to decarbonising in the long term.

 The ITF's research indicates that maritime transport will face significant pressure to adopt alternative fuels, creating cost pass-through implications for ocean freight rates.

**Cost Mechanisms:** Sustainable fuel surcharges will become standard, with forwarders needing to develop transparent mechanisms for allocating Green Marine Fuel (GMF) costs across shipment volumes. Container shipping will likely implement IMO-compliant fuel levy structures based on ITF decarbonisation scenarios.

**Contract Implications:** Service contracts must include provisions for sustainable fuel cost fluctuations and regulatory compliance costs driven by ITF-recommended policies.

### Air Transport



Aviation alone accounts for nearly half (47%) of international and intercity passenger-kilometres.

 

Long-distance trips are particularly hard to decarbonise for both passenger and freight. Making these trips more sustainable without reducing travel will require reducing the carbon intensity of the travel.



**Cost Mechanisms:** Air cargo will face carbon intensity reduction requirements that translate to Sustainable Aviation Fuel (SAF) surcharges and carbon offset fees. ITF research supports policy measures that will increase these costs significantly.

**Operational Changes:** Freight forwarders must prepare for modal shift pressures on shorter air routes as policy makers implement ITF-recommended measures to reduce aviation's carbon intensity.

### Road Transport



Goods transport by road consumes around 50% of all diesel fuel and accounts for 80% of the global net increase in diesel use since 2000. Projections see road freight activity at least doubling to 2050, offsetting efficiency gains and increasing road freight CO2 emissions.



**Cost Mechanisms:** Road freight will face the most immediate decarbonisation pressures, with electric vehicle transition costs, charging infrastructure investments, and carbon taxation creating new surcharge categories.

**Fleet Transition:** ITF research directly supports policies requiring zero-emission commercial vehicles, affecting last-mile delivery costs and intermodal connection pricing.

---

## Key Data and Figures

| Parameter | Value | Source |
|---|---|---|
| Freight demand growth to 2050 (Current Ambition) | 100% increase | ITF Transport Outlook 2023 |
| Freight demand growth to 2050 (High Ambition) | 59% increase | ITF Transport Outlook 2023 |
| Transport CO2 share of energy-related emissions | 23% (could reach 40% by 2030) | ITF Decarbonising Transport |
| Required infrastructure investment | 1.7% of global GDP annually to 2050 | ITF Transport Outlook 2023 |
| Road freight diesel consumption | 50% of all diesel fuel | ITF Road Freight Decarbonisation |
| Road freight diesel growth since 2000 | 80% of global net increase | ITF Road Freight Decarbonisation |
| Aviation share of intercity passenger-km | 47% | ITF Transport Outlook 2023 |
| ITF member countries | 69 countries | ITF Organization Structure |
| ITF annual summit attendance | 1,200 delegates, 55 transport ministers | ITF 2024 Summit |

---

## Compliance Risk Register

| Risk | Severity | Likelihood | Deadline |
|---|---|---|---|
| Carbon pricing implementation without cost pass-through mechanisms | High | High | 2025-2027 |
| Modal shift policies reducing air freight demand on short routes | Medium | High | 2026-2030 |
| Infrastructure investment priorities not aligned with network expansion | Medium | Medium | 2025-2035 |
| Sustainable fuel mandates without procurement frameworks | High | High | 2027-2030 |
| Zero-emission vehicle requirements for urban deliveries | High | High | 2025-2030 |

---

## Recommended Actions

| Priority | Action | Owner | Timeframe |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Develop freight volume scenario planning based on ITF 2050 projections | Sustainability, Operations | Q3 2026 |
| 2 | Create carbon pricing pass-through mechanisms by transport mode | Finance, Ocean Product, Air Product | Q2 2026 |
| 3 | Assess infrastructure investment priorities against ITF recommendations | Ocean Product, Road Product | Q4 2026 |
| 4 | Establish sustainable fuel procurement frameworks | Procurement, Ocean Product, Air Product | Q1 2027 |
| 5 | Monitor ITF policy recommendations for regulatory precedent | Legal, Sustainability | Ongoing |
| 6 | Integrate ITF data into customer advisory services | Sales, Sustainability | Q3 2026 |
| 7 | Develop zero-emission last-mile delivery options | Operations, Road Product | Q2 2027 |
| 8 | Subscribe to ITF Transport Outlook and research publications | Sustainability, Operations | Q2 2026 |

---

## Implementation Timeline

| Date | Milestone | Status |
|---|---|---|
| May 2023 | ITF Transport Outlook 2023 published | In force |
| May 2024 | ITF 2024 Summit on "Greening Transport" | Completed |
| 2025 | ITF Transport Outlook 2025 expected publication | Upcoming |
| May 2026 | ITF 2026 Summit "Funding Resilient Transport" | Upcoming |
| 2025-2027 | Peak period for ITF-influenced carbon pricing policies | Upcoming |
| 2027-2030 | Sustainable fuel mandates based on ITF research | Upcoming |
| 2030-2035 | Infrastructure investment priorities align with ITF scenarios | Upcoming |
| 2040-2050 | Full implementation of ITF decarbonisation pathways | Upcoming |

---

## Open Questions

• **ITF Transport Outlook 2025 Scope:** The next edition's specific focus areas and updated freight volume projections are not yet published, affecting precision of capacity planning.

• **Policy Translation Timeline:** ITF research provides scenarios but does not specify which countries will implement recommendations and over what timeframes, creating regulatory uncertainty.

• **Carbon Pricing Harmonization:** ITF supports carbon pricing but member countries may implement different mechanisms at different rates, complicating global cost pass-through strategies.

• **Infrastructure Funding:** ITF identifies 1.7% of GDP investment needs but funding sources and allocation priorities remain at national/regional discretion.

• **Technology Readiness:** ITF scenarios assume sustainable fuel availability and zero-emission vehicle deployment, but commercial readiness timelines remain uncertain.

• **Modal Shift Quantification:** ITF research suggests policy-driven modal shifts but does not quantify specific route-level impacts on freight forwarder business volumes.

---

## Sources

| ITF Transport Outlook 2023 | [https://www.itf-oecd.org/itf-transport-outlook-2023](https://www.itf-oecd.org/itf-transport-outlook-2023) | Tier 1: Primary research and policy analysis from intergovernmental organization |
| ITF Decarbonising Transport Initiative | [https://www.itf-oecd.org/decarbonising-transport](https://www.itf-oecd.org/decarbonising-transport) | Tier 1: Official policy research recognized by UNFCCC |
| ITF Transport Outlook Project | [https://www.itf-oecd.org/itf-transport-outlook-project](https://www.itf-oecd.org/itf-transport-outlook-project) | Tier 1: Primary research methodology documentation |
| Towards Road Freight Decarbonisation | [https://www.itf-oecd.org/towards-road-freight-decarbonisation](https://www.itf-oecd.org/towards-road-freight-decarbonisation) | Tier 1: Sector-specific decarbonisation research |

whatIsIt: MIT Center for Transportation and Logistics (CTL) conducts research on sustainable freight, supply chain decarbonisation, and logistics optimisation.
