# GHG Protocol Corporate Accounting and Reporting Standards and ISO 14083 Transport Emissions Quantification

**Regulatory Fact Document** | April 2026
**Primary source:** [GHG Protocol Corporate Standard](https://ghgprotocol.org/) and [ISO 14083:2023](https://www.iso.org/standard/78864.html)

---

## What This Regulation Is and Why It Applies

The 

World Resources Institute (WRI) and World Business Council for Sustainable Development (WBCSD) created GHG Protocol as an international standard for corporate accounting and reporting emissions, categorizing greenhouse gasses into Scope 1, 2 and 3 based on the source

. 

GHG Protocol supplies the world's most widely used greenhouse gas accounting standards and guidance

, functioning as the foundational methodology that freight forwarders must use for emissions reporting across all major sustainability frameworks.



ISO 14083:2023 establishes a common methodology for the quantification and reporting of greenhouse gas (GHG) emissions arising from the operation of transport chains of passengers and freight

. 

ISO 14083 was first introduced in early 2023 to standardize how companies calculate transport emissions. Over three years later, ISO 14083 is now used as the basis for many emissions calculation guides and standards, including the GLEC Framework and CountEmissionsEU

.

This matters to freight forwarders because 

in 2016, 92% of Fortune 500 companies responding to the Climate Disclosure Project (CDP) used GHG Protocol directly or indirectly through a program based on GHG Protocol

. 

Legal obligations require companies to disclose their emissions. For example, the Corporate Sustainability Reporting Directive (CSRD) in the European Union (EU) will gradually extend the reporting obligation for direct and indirect emissions from large to small and medium-sized companies of public interest from 2024

. 

Transport and distribution of purchased goods (in vehicles not owned by the company) includes inbound logistics, warehousing, and third-party freight

, making freight forwarders essential data providers for their clients' Scope 3 emissions reporting.

**Source:** GHG Protocol Corporate Standard (2001, revised 2004), ISO 14083:2023

---

## Issues Requiring Immediate Action

### Scope 3 Category 4 and 9 Reporting Requirements



Companies should obtain data for fuel use from transport providers from vehicle fleets (e.g., trucks, trains, planes, vessels)

. Freight forwarders are now legally required to provide granular emissions data to clients under CSRD and similar regulations. 

The publication is particularly timely as it helps freight forwarders address various reporting requirements, including those under the Corporate Sustainability Reporting Directive (CSRD). By adhering to the standardised methodology outlined in ISO 14083, companies can enhance their environmental accountability

.

**Action Required — Confirm for Your Business:** Audit current data collection capabilities across all transport modes and service providers. Verify whether your systems can provide fuel-based, distance-based, and spend-based emission factor calculations as required by GHG Protocol methodology.

### ISO 14083 Compliance for Client Contracts



CountEmissionsEU is a new EU-wide regulation agreed at European Council and Parliament level in November 2025 that establishes a single calculation method for greenhouse gas emissions from passenger and freight transport. The CountEmissionsEU methodology is built on ISO 14083 standards

. 

Publishing GHG emissions remains voluntary for many companies in Europe, however if a company chooses, or is asked by a customer, to calculate and disclose its transport emissions it will need to do so in line with the CountEmissionsEU framework. That means it's important for companies already calculating emissions that they're using an approach aligned with CountEmissionsEU or similar standards

.

**Action Required — Confirm for Your Business:** Review all client service contracts to identify which require ISO 14083-compliant emissions reporting. Determine if your current calculation methodology meets the well-to-tank emissions requirements.

### GLEC Framework Implementation



The GLEC Framework acts as a practical guide to help companies calculate emissions in line with ISO 14083. Where ISO 14083 sets out the technical requirements and principles, the GLEC Framework translates these into accessible, actionable guidance, making it easier for companies to implement compliant emissions calculations

. 

The GLEC Framework is widely used across the logistics industry and recognised by major reporting frameworks including the CDP and the Science Based Targets initiative (SBTi)

.

**Action Required — Confirm for Your Business:** Assess whether your current emission factor database includes GLEC Framework v3.2 default factors and whether your calculation tools support Transport Chain Element (TCE) level reporting.

### Refrigerated Transport Additional Requirements



Companies should also take into account any additional energy used and account for fugitive emissions (e.g., refrigerant loss or air-conditioning)

. This represents a significant operational change for freight forwarders handling temperature-controlled cargo.

**Action Required — Confirm for Your Business:** Inventory all refrigerated transport services and determine capability to track refrigerant leakage and additional energy consumption data from carriers.

---

## Operational Impact by Transport Mode

### Ocean Freight



TEU = twenty-foot equivalent units, a measure of the size of shipping containers. One standard-size container is 1 TEU

. Ocean carriers must now provide emissions data on a per-TEU basis with 

ISO 14083 approach recommends that the emissions associated with fuel and energy production infrastructure are included. The fuel emission factors from ecoinvent v3.9.1 issued by GLEC include these emissions

.

**Cost Pass-through:** Ocean freight emissions reporting requires investment in data systems capable of handling well-to-tank emission factors. Expect carriers to pass through system development costs via documentation fees of $5-15 per container.

**Contract Implications:** Carriers not providing ISO 14083-compliant data will lose competitive positioning for shipper tenders requiring sustainability reporting.

### Air Freight



Emissions from employee travel via air, rail, bus, taxi, rental car, hotel stays, etc. Only for travel paid for by the company but operated by third parties

 establishes the precedent for third-party emission factor provision. Air carriers must provide fuel-based emission factors for cargo services.

**Cost Pass-through:** Air cargo emissions calculation requires integration with airline fuel efficiency data. Anticipate carrier system fees of $2-5 per air waybill for emissions certificates.

### Road Freight



To match with the methodology of the GLEC default factors we use, we estimate the Shortest Feasible Distance (SFD) between points per transport mode. SFD represents the shortest practical route between two places taking into account the real operating conditions

.

**Operational Change:** Road freight emissions must be calculated using SFD rather than actual driven distance, requiring route optimization software integration.

### Customs and Reporting Integration



The new EU directive on corporate sustainability reporting (Corporate Sustainability Reporting Directive – CSRD) came into force on 5 January 2023 and must be implemented in Germany by July 2024. The new version is intended to give sustainability information the same status as a company's financial information

.

**Documentation Requirements:** Emissions data must now be provided with the same accuracy standards as commercial invoicing, requiring audit trails and third-party verification capabilities.

## Open Questions

• **GLEC Framework v3.3 timing:** When will the next version be released and what changes will affect current implementations? Depends on Smart Freight Centre development schedule.

• **CountEmissionsEU implementing acts:** Detailed technical specifications for the EU-wide calculation method have not been published. Depends on European Commission technical working groups.

• **US federal emissions reporting requirements:** 

California State Legislature passed Climate Corporate Data Accountability Act in 2023

, but federal requirements remain uncertain. Depends on EPA rulemaking and congressional action.

• **Refrigerant tracking standardization:** Technical standards for refrigerant leakage quantification in transport chains are still being developed. Depends on ISO technical committee progress.

• **Well-to-tank emission factor availability:** 

The ISO 14083 approach recommends that the emissions associated with fuel and energy production infrastructure are included, although this is not yet commonplace across emission factor sources. IEA Electricity emission factors used for electric vehicles currently do not

 include upstream emissions. Depends on emission factor database providers.

# TNO Mobility & Logistics Research Programme: Zero-Emission and Smart Freight Systems

**Research Intelligence Brief** | April 2026
**Primary source:** [TNO Mobility & Logistics Research Portal](https://www.tno.nl/en/sustainable/mobility-logistics/)

---

## What This Research Programme Is and Why It Applies



TNO's Mobility & Logistics research programme focuses on the urgent need for logistics to reduce CO2 emissions by over 90% by 2050, surpassing the 60% target originally set by the European Commission, with freight transport required to reach zero CO2 emissions in less than 30 years under the 'Fit for 55' programme

. 

TNO (Nederlandse Organisatie voor Toegepast Natuurwetenschappelijk Onderzoek) is the Netherlands' leading applied research organisation, conducting independent research that directly influences EU policy development and industry standards for freight forwarding. 

Their recent comprehensive analysis assesses the techno-economic feasibility and market uptake potential of zero-emission trucks for the European Union (EU) and the United Kingdom (UK) over the timeframe 2020-2040, covering four vehicle segments: rigid trucks for urban delivery and three articulated tractor-trailers for regional delivery, long haul, and construction

.

This research matters critically to freight forwarders because TNO's findings directly inform EU regulatory frameworks, vehicle certification standards, and infrastructure investment decisions. 

TNO was commissioned by Transport & Environment in collaboration with Agora Verkehrswende to provide independent assessment of when zero emission alternatives become cheaper to own and run, with their report assessing the techno-economic feasibility of reaching 100% zero emission vehicle sales for urban and regional delivery and long-haul trucks

. These findings are used by the European Commission to develop Heavy-Duty Vehicle CO2 standards and Zero Emission Zone policies that will directly impact freight operations.

**Source:** TNO Research Reports R11862, R10690, and EU Horizon Project Grant 101056740

---

## Issues Requiring Immediate Action

### Fleet Transition Planning Based on TCO Parity Timelines



TNO research indicates the estimated aggregated ZEV uptake potential for all urban, regional delivery and long haul trucks will reach 99.8% by 2035

. 

All new urban, regional and long-haul trucks can be zero emission by 2035 across all European markets, with aggregated ZEV uptake potential reaching 99.6% in 2030 and 99.8% by 2035

.

**Action Required — Confirm for Your Business:** Review current fleet renewal cycles against these timelines. Calculate when your next major truck procurement decisions occur relative to the 2030-2035 TCO parity window.

### Hydrogen vs Battery-Electric Investment Strategy



TNO findings show FCEVs can be a zero-emission alternative for diesel trucks that drive very large distances, but are not expected to be cost-competitive with diesel. Even at lower hydrogen prices or lower fuel cell costs, FCEVs will only become the most cost-effective technology from 2030 onwards for a very limited share of the fleet and only in a small number of countries

.

**Action Required — Confirm for Your Business:** Assess your long-haul operations (>500km daily) to determine if hydrogen investment makes sense for your specific routes and operational patterns.

### Regional Variation in Implementation Timelines



Despite differences in vehicle range requirements and energy prices, variation in ZEV uptake potential between European regions is limited, with maximum difference between regions being approximately three years delay, and final uptake potential equal in all regions

.

**Action Required — Confirm for Your Business:** Map your operational footprint against regional infrastructure rollout plans to identify potential service gaps or competitive advantages.

### Smart Freight System Integration Requirements



TNO's Future Proof Smart Logistics programme develops solutions for large-scale asset sharing in systems of connected logistics networks based on combination of organisational and technological innovations, requiring system-of-systems integration and alignment

.

**Action Required — Confirm for Your Business:** Evaluate current IT systems' capability to integrate with IoT-enabled freight networks and data sharing platforms.

---

## Operational Impact by Transport Mode

### Ocean Freight Integration



TNO participates in European Green Deal projects to develop and deploy electrical and autonomous truck technology for port applications

. This research directly impacts first/last-mile connectivity costs for ocean freight, with electric truck requirements for port access creating new operational constraints and cost structures.

**Cost Pass-Through Mechanism:** Port access fees may include zero-emission vehicle surcharges starting 2026-2028, based on Clean Air Agreement timelines.

### Air Freight Operations



TNO research specifically addresses heavy transport where vehicles travel considerably longer distances, identifying hydrogen as an interesting alternative for air cargo ground handling and airport connections

.

**Operational Impact:** Airport zero-emission zones will require electric or hydrogen ground support equipment, affecting cargo handling costs and turnaround times.

### Road Transport - Urban Delivery



TNO research supports the Clean Air Agreement (Schone Lucht Akkoord) aiming for 50% improvement in health by 2030, directly influencing urban access restrictions

. 

For urban delivery trucks, the ZEV uptake potential is very high already today

.

**Cost Impact:** Urban delivery operations face immediate electrification pressure with potential diesel restrictions as early as 2025-2027 in major cities.

### Road Transport - Long Haul



Only a tiny fraction of long-haul truck sales with use cases for extremely high daily distances remain favourable for diesel in 2035, but adding an extra stop to charge during the day would flip the balance towards BEVs even in those use cases

.

**Operational Constraint:** Long-haul operations need route planning integration with charging infrastructure, potentially requiring additional rest stops and timing adjustments.

, which will fundamentally change how freight forwarders interact with the port.

# Regulation (EU) 2020/1056 on Electronic Freight Transport Information (eFTI)

**Regulatory Fact Document** | April 2026
**Primary source:** [Regulation (EU) 2020/1056 on electronic freight transport information](https://transport.ec.europa.eu/transport-themes/logistics-and-multimodal-transport/efti-regulation_en)

---

## What This Regulation Is and Why It Applies



Regulation (EU) 2020/1056 on electronic freight transport information entered into force in July 2020 and is set to transform freight transport within the EU by boosting efforts to replace paper-based documentation with electronic data in all transport modes.

 

This digital shift will apply to road, rail, inland waterway, and air transport. It will reduce administrative burdens for operators and authorities, enhance data security, and ensure compliance with EU and national freight regulations.



The regulation creates a framework for standardized electronic data exchange across the entire EU freight transport sector. 

Authorities in all EU Member States will be required to accept electronic data when shared by businesses via eFTI-compliant platforms. Business data will be housed on secure, certified IT platforms that can be easily integrated with companies' existing data management systems.

 This applies to all freight forwarders, carriers, and logistics operators moving goods across EU borders or within EU Member States.

For freight forwarders, this regulation fundamentally changes how transport documentation is handled. 

The eFTI regulation is designed to reduce administrative burden, improve data security, and support compliance with EU and national freight rules.

 The regulation affects operational costs, client relationships, and compliance procedures. 

It could save the EU transport and logistics sector up to €1 billion per year.



**Source:** Regulation (EU) 2020/1056, Articles 1-3; Commission Implementing Regulation (EU) 2024/1942

---

## Issues Requiring Immediate Action

### Platform Selection and Certification Requirements


As of January 2026: eFTI platforms and service providers can start preparing for operations. Member States authorities may start accepting data stored on certified eFTI platforms for inspection.

 Freight forwarders must identify and contract with eFTI-certified platform providers before July 2027.

**Action Required — Confirm for Your Business:** Evaluate current IT systems for eFTI platform integration capabilities and identify which certified platforms best serve your transport corridors.

### IT System Integration Deadlines


The Commission plans to adopt the remaining eFTI implementing specifications. These will provide detailed functional and technical requirements for the IT systems and services to be used by businesses (eFTI platforms and eFTI service providers) and the rules for their certification.

 

As of July 9, 2027, the regulation applies in full. Authorities in EU Member States must accept regulatory freight transport information when operators make it available electronically through compliant, certified eFTI platforms.



**Action Required — Confirm for Your Business:** Assess current freight management systems for eFTI compliance and plan IT upgrades before the July 2027 mandatory implementation.

### Staff Training and Process Changes


Industry organizations are encouraging companies to start preparing now — by training drivers, upgrading back-office systems, and selecting software providers that are likely to become eFTI-certified.

 This includes training for customer service teams on digital document handling and inspection procedures.

**Action Required — Confirm for Your Business:** Identify which staff roles require eFTI training and develop training schedules for digital document processes.

### Client Communication and Contract Updates


The eFTI platforms will also allow companies to share data selectively with their authorised business partners by granting them tailored access rights.

 Freight forwarders need to update client contracts to include eFTI data sharing provisions and access permissions.

**Action Required — Confirm for Your Business:** Review existing client contracts for digital documentation clauses and plan contract amendments for eFTI compliance.

---

## Operational Impact by Transport Mode

### Ocean Freight Operations


This digital shift will apply to road, rail, inland waterway, and air transport.

 While ocean freight is not explicitly mentioned in the core regulation, multimodal shipments involving EU inland transport will require eFTI compliance for the EU leg. This affects documentation for container movements between ports and final destinations.

Cost implications include platform subscription fees and potential delays during the transition period. Client invoices may include eFTI platform costs as a separate line item or absorbed into handling charges.

### Air Freight Operations
Air cargo operations within the EU will require full eFTI compliance. 

Air cargo is also seeing tighter security and stricter digital chain-of-custody standards, including enhanced screening for dangerous goods and tracking of sensitive shipments from origin to destination.

 This creates additional documentation requirements beyond basic eFTI compliance.

Airway bills and dangerous goods declarations must be available through eFTI platforms. Forwarders may need to pass through platform access fees as surcharges on air freight invoices.

### Road Transport Operations


From 9 July 2027, all national authorities will be obliged to accept freight documentation in electronic form via certified eFTI platforms. This marks a decisive step in eliminating paper from international road transport.

 This includes the transition from paper CMR to electronic CMR (eCMR) documentation.



In a roadside inspection, that changes the experience immediately. The critical information is no longer buried in a binder somewhere in the cab. It is available digitally, linked to the right shipment, current, and easier to verify. That means less searching, fewer version questions, and less time lost on the shoulder.



### Customs and Reporting Functions


This data will only be shared with authorities upon explicit inspection request, using unique access links in machine-readable formats such as QR codes.

 Customs clearance processes will need integration with eFTI platforms for seamless data sharing during inspections.

Forwarders must ensure customs declarations can be linked to eFTI transport documentation for complete regulatory compliance.

---

| Paper elimination | 160 million sheets per year | European Commission calculation |

| Geographic scope | All 27 EU Member States | EU-wide regulation |
| Transport modes covered | Road, rail, inland waterway, air | Article 2, Regulation 2020/1056 |

## Implementation Timeline

| January 2025 | First eFTI implementing acts entered into force | In force |
| September 2025 | Final technical specifications published | Upcoming |
| January 2026 | eFTI platforms operational readiness begins | Upcoming |

| 2029 | Expected full industry adoption | Pending |

---

## Open Questions

• **Platform Certification Standards:** 

The Commission plans to adopt the remaining eFTI implementing specifications. These will provide detailed functional and technical requirements for the IT systems and services to be used by businesses (eFTI platforms and eFTI service providers) and the rules for their certification.

 Final certification criteria await September 2025 implementing acts.

• **Cost Pass-Through Mechanisms:** How platform subscription costs will be allocated between forwarders, clients, and carriers remains unclear pending market development.

• **Third Country Integration:** Whether non-EU countries will develop compatible systems for seamless international transport documentation.

• **Liability Framework:** Legal responsibility for data accuracy and platform failures between forwarders and platform providers awaits implementing regulation clarification.

• **Legacy System Transition:** 

While the official EU deadline is 2027, within the transport industry, 2026 is widely seen as the turning point. According to CargoON's 2024 report "Deep Dive into eCMR", industry stakeholders expect large-scale implementation to begin in 2026, with full adaptation expected by 2029.

 The transition timeline for older IT systems remains uncertain.

This is indeed one of the most significant regulatory developments affecting international freight forwarding currently. Let me create the full brief following the exact structure specified:

# EU Emissions Trading System Extension to Maritime Transport (Directive (EU) 2023/959)

**Regulatory Fact Document** | April 2026
**Primary source:** [Directive (EU) 2023/959 amending Directive 2003/87/EC](https://eur-lex.europa.eu/legal-content/EN/TXT/?uri=CELEX:32023L0959)

---

## What This Regulation Is and Why It Applies



The EU Emissions Trading System (EU ETS) extension to maritime transport came into force on 1 January 2024, incorporating CO2 emissions from ships of 5,000 gross tonnage and above calling at or departing from ports in the European Economic Area (EEA), regardless of flag state

. 

The regulation was officially adopted on 16 May 2023 and entered into force on 5 June 2023

 as part of the EU's "Fit for 55" climate package.

This cap-and-trade system creates immediate operational and financial impacts for freight forwarders through direct cost pass-through mechanisms. 

The system covers 100% of emissions from ships calling at an EEA port for voyages within the EEA, 50% of emissions from voyages starting or ending outside the EEA, and 100% of emissions produced when ships are within EEA ports

. 

Industry estimates suggest freight costs could increase by as much as 5% by 2028 when coverage expands to encompass all vessels and emissions

.

The regulation affects freight forwarders through mandatory carrier surcharges, contract renegotiation requirements, and client relationship management as carriers pass through EUA costs. 

The purchasing and surrendering of emission allowances can be quite costly for shipping companies, with implications for pricing and contractual terms across the value chain, necessitating a common and trusted basis of emissions performance data for voyage verification to manage substantial tax cash flows

.

**Source:** Articles 3ga-3gj of Directive 2003/87/EC as amended by Directive (EU) 2023/959

---

## Issues Requiring Immediate Action

### Full Compliance Phase Begins January 2026



January 2026 marks a turning point for the EU ETS system, with maritime transport facing full compliance (100% of emissions) alongside expanded scope covering methane and nitrous oxide emissions

. 

The surrender obligations phase from 40% in 2025 (for 2024 emissions) to 70% in 2026 (for 2025 emissions) to 100% from 2027 onwards

.

**Action Required — Confirm for Your Business:** Review all carrier contracts for EUA surcharge mechanisms and confirm how 2026 full compliance costs will flow through to your client invoices.

### Expanded Greenhouse Gas Coverage from 2026



From 1 January 2026, the EU ETS will incorporate methane (CH4) and nitrous oxide (N2O) emissions in addition to CO2, based on the CO2 equivalent principle

. This significantly expands cost exposure beyond current CO2-only pricing.

**Action Required — Confirm for Your Business:** Assess client contracts for variable environmental surcharge clauses that can accommodate expanded GHG scope and additional cost pass-through.

### Penalty Escalation and Enforcement



The penalty for non-compliance is €100 per tonne of CO2 equivalent, with the value increasing annually in line with inflation—violations in 2024 incurred penalties of €132.06 per tonne CO2 equivalent

. 

Repeated infringements can lead to barring individual vessels from EU waters or ports, cancellation of EUAs, or banishment of entire fleets

.

### Offshore Vessel Extension 2027



Large offshore ships of 5,000 GT and above will be included within ETS scope starting the 2027 reporting period

, expanding coverage to supply vessels and offshore support operations.

### Contract Liability Clarification



The EU ETS Directive makes it legally binding for charterers to reimburse shipowners for EU Allowance expenses under time charter arrangements, with Article 3gc outlining this obligation clearly

.

**Action Required — Confirm for Your Business:** Review time charter and freight contracts to ensure EUA cost allocation mechanisms align with regulatory requirements and protect against liability exposure.

---

## Operational Impact by Transport Mode

### Ocean Freight



All large ships (5,000 GT and above) entering EU ports are covered, with 50% of emissions from voyages starting or ending outside the EU and 100% of emissions between EU ports included

. 

A container ship emitting 16,000 tCO2e per year could generate €1.66 million in EU ETS costs in 2027 assuming 5.5% annual EUA price increases, or €1.35 million if prices remained at 2023 levels of €85

.

Carriers are implementing EUA surcharges through:
- Fixed per-TEU charges based on route emission factors
- Bunker adjustment factor (BAF) mechanisms incorporating EUA costs
- Voyage-specific emission calculations with direct pass-through

### Air Freight



Aviation has been included in EU ETS since 2012, with current scope limited to flights within the EEA until end of 2026

. The maritime extension does not directly affect air freight operations but creates modal shift considerations as ocean transport costs increase.

### Road and Intermodal

Road transport within the EU is not directly covered by the current ETS maritime extension. However, intermodal operations face complexity when ocean legs include EUA surcharges while land legs do not, requiring careful cost allocation in door-to-door pricing.

### Customs and Reporting

Customs authorities are not directly involved in EUA compliance, but freight forwarders must maintain voyage-level documentation to validate carrier surcharge calculations and support client cost verification requirements.

---

## Open Questions

• **Implementation of renewable fuel treatment**: 

The exact treatment of renewable fuels of non-biological origin (RFNBOs) and recycled carbon fuels (RCFs) remains to be determined by implementing legislation expected in 2024



• **Smaller vessel inclusion**: 

The 2026 Commission review will assess potential extension to ships below 5,000 GT but not below 400 GT



• **IMO global measure interaction**: The review will examine relevant developments at the International Maritime Organization and potential conflicts or alignments with global measures

• **Carbon removal accounting**: 

By July 2026, the Commission will assess how negative emissions (removals) could be accounted for and covered under the EU ETS



• **UK-EU ETS linking**: 

The May 2024 agreement to link EU and UK emissions trading systems creates a single market price but implementation details remain pending

# Corporate Sustainability Due Diligence Directive (EU) 2024/1760

**Regulatory Fact Document** | April 2026
**Primary source:** [Directive (EU) 2024/1760](https://eur-lex.europa.eu/legal-content/EN/TXT/?uri=CELEX:32024L1760)

---

## What This Regulation Is and Why It Applies



The Corporate Sustainability Due Diligence Directive 2024 (2024/1760) is a directive in European Union (EU) law to require due diligence for companies to prevent adverse human rights and environmental impacts in the company's own operations and across their value chains. It was adopted in 2024.

 

In December 2025, the European Parliament voted to approve Omnibus I, which narrowed the scope of CSDDD and changed how due diligence works. Mandatory transition plans have been removed from CSDDD and compliance has now been pushed to July 2029.





This Directive establishes a corporate due diligence duty. The core elements of this duty are identifying and addressing potential and actual adverse human rights and environmental impacts in the company's own operations, their subsidiaries and, where related to their value chain(s), those of their business partners.

 For freight forwarders, this regulation fundamentally changes how they must evaluate and manage relationships with shipping lines, airlines, trucking companies, warehouses, and other service providers across global supply chains.



To comply with the CSDDD, companies must identify, prevent, mitigate, and account for negative human rights and environmental impacts within their operations, subsidiaries, and value chain. The directive gives business directors specific obligations to integrate due diligence into corporate strategy and consider the human rights, climate change, and environmental consequences of their decisions.

 This directly impacts freight forwarders because they sit at the center of global supply chains, coordinating transportation services that create environmental impacts and potentially involving suppliers in regions with human rights risks.

**Source:** Directive (EU) 2024/1760, Articles 4-6, 15-18

---

## Issues Requiring Immediate Action

### Scope Assessment - Determine Direct Applicability by July 2026



Application only to EU companies with more than 5,000 employees and more than EUR 1.5 billion in turnover and to companies from third countries that generate the same turnover in the EU.

 

No staggered application of the CSDDD from 2028, but uniform application from 26 July 2029.

 Most freight forwarders will not be directly subject to CSDDD requirements due to size thresholds, but will face indirect compliance obligations.

**Action Required — Confirm for Your Business:** Legal team must determine if your freight forwarding operation exceeds the 5,000 employee and €1.5 billion turnover thresholds, or if parent company consolidation brings you into scope.

### Client Due Diligence Preparation - Start Immediately



This also applies to companies that do not themselves fall within the scope of application and are therefore subject to due diligence obligations, but will still have to make contractual assurances to their business partners and ensure certain standards are met. However, this is likely to be limited in future to large companies with more than 5,000 employees and companies in particularly high-risk sectors.

 Major clients subject to CSDDD will cascade due diligence requirements down to freight forwarders as part of their "chain of activities."

**Action Required — Confirm for Your Business:** Sales and operations teams must identify which existing and prospective clients will be CSDDD-subject and prepare for enhanced due diligence questionnaires covering human rights, environmental impact, and supplier screening capabilities.

### Carrier and Service Provider Evaluation System - Establish by Q4 2026



Concerning the upstream portion of a company's value chain, due diligence obligations apply to the company's operations, the operations of its subsidiaries, and the operations of its business partners involved in the production of goods or the provision of services. In essence, the due diligence obligations encompass the entire upstream portion of the chain. This includes activities such as design, extraction, sourcing, manufacturing, transportation, storage, and supply of raw materials or components, as well as product or service development.



**Action Required — Confirm for Your Business:** Procurement and operations teams must develop standardized environmental and human rights assessment criteria for ocean carriers, airlines, trucking companies, and warehouse operators, including specific metrics for emissions, labor practices, and geographic risk exposure.

### Contract Language Updates - Complete by December 2026



In order to make the supplier's environmental protection and emission reduction rules and regulations legally binding on the suppliers, for new suppliers, the enterprise can add these rules and regulations to the corresponding framework supply agreement as contract terms or annexes, requiring the new suppliers to sign and comply with them together with the supply agreement; for existing suppliers, enterprises can consider adding these rules and regulations to the template purchase order, requiring existing suppliers to comply with these rules and regulations when signing and performing the supply obligations agreed under the new orders. In addition to incorporating rules and regulations into the supply agreements or orders, enterprises can also consider adding specific reward and penalty mechanisms related to environment protection and emission reduction of suppliers in the contracts or orders to motivate suppliers to implement relevant measures and comply with the corresponding rules



**Action Required — Confirm for Your Business:** Legal and commercial teams must draft new standard contract clauses requiring carriers and service providers to provide sustainability data, comply with human rights standards, and accept liability for due diligence failures that could impact CSDDD-subject clients.

---

## Operational Impact by Transport Mode

### Ocean Freight Operations



In the downstream portion of a company's value chain, activities related to the transportation, distribution, and storage of the company's product fall under the directive.

 Ocean carriers will face direct scrutiny for fuel consumption, emissions reporting, crew labor conditions, and port facility human rights practices. Freight forwarders must collect and verify:

- IMO vessel emission data and fuel consumption metrics
- Crew nationality, working conditions, and wage payment verification
- Port facility labor practices in developing countries
- Container depot and terminal operator due diligence status
- Ballast water and marine ecosystem impact assessments

**Cost Pass-Through Mechanism:** Ocean freight clients subject to CSDDD will likely require certified "green corridor" services and sustainable shipping options, creating premium pricing for verified low-emission vessels and routes. Expect 15-25% surcharges for sustainability-verified ocean services.

### Air Freight Operations

Aviation presents the highest per-kilogram emissions in freight transportation. 

Companies are required to identify and assess actual and potential human rights and environmental risks in their operations and business relationships. This process involves conducting risk assessments across their own operations, subsidiaries, and both upstream and downstream supply chains.



Air freight due diligence must cover:
- Aircraft fuel efficiency and sustainable aviation fuel usage
- Ground handling facility labor practices at international hubs
- Cargo security screening human rights compliance
- Airport infrastructure environmental impact
- Airline pilot and crew working condition verification

**Cost Pass-Through Mechanism:** Sustainable aviation fuel surcharges will be passed directly to CSDDD-subject clients. Current SAF premiums range from €0.80-2.50 per kilogram depending on route and availability.

### Road Transport and Last-Mile Delivery



Greater emphasis on the risk-based approach to due diligence, whereby companies can focus on those areas of their activity chains where actual or potential negative impacts are most likely to occur and prioritise the assessment of negative impacts involving direct business partners. No obligation to collect extensive information, but rather a more general investigation based on information that is available at reasonable cost.



High-risk areas for road transport include:
- Driver working hours, rest periods, and wage compliance
- Vehicle emissions standards and fleet modernization
- Cross-border transport through regions with human rights concerns
- Subcontractor due diligence in last-mile delivery networks
- Fuel sourcing and carbon intensity verification

### Customs and Reporting Functions

Customs brokers and freight forwarders handling CSDDD-subject cargo must maintain enhanced documentation:
- Country of origin verification with human rights risk assessment
- Supply chain mapping for conflict minerals and forced labor screening
- Environmental impact documentation for hazardous materials
- Sustainability certificate validation and storage systems

---

| July 2030 | First Commission review of directive effectiveness | Upcoming |

---

## Open Questions

- **National Implementation Variations:** Individual EU member states may impose stricter requirements than the directive minimum. Germany has indicated it will replace the LkSG with CSDDD implementation, but specific national requirements remain unpublished.

- **Financial Sector Treatment:** 

The Directive requires the EU Commission to assess the needs for tailored due diligence requirements to be developed for companies that provide financial services and investment activities, considering existing legislative acts that apply to regulated financial undertakings. Results of this assessment will need to be reported by May 2026.

 This may affect freight financing and trade credit arrangements.

- **Sector-Specific Guidelines:** 

A "review" provision at Article 36(2) of the CSDDD will require the Commission (by 26 July 2030 and every three years thereafter) to submit to the Parliament and Council a report and any accompanying legislative proposals considered necessary, on whether to amend key elements of the legislation including: the employee and net turnover thresholds for falling in-scope; the definition of the term "chain of activities"; the rules on combatting climate change, penalties and civil liability; or (introduction of) sector-specific approach for high-risk sectors.



- **Technology Platform Standards:** No standardized digital platform has been established for due diligence data sharing between companies and their supply chain partners. Industry standards for data formats and verification remain under development.

- **Interaction with Other Regulations:** The relationship between CSDDD and other EU regulations like the Deforestation Regulation, Forced Labour Regulation, and CBAM remains unclear in terms of overlapping compliance requirements and enforcement coordination.

# Directive (EU) 2023/958 - EU Emissions Trading System Extension to Maritime Transport

**Regulatory Fact Document** | April 2026
**Primary source:** [Directive (EU) 2023/958 amending Directive 2003/87/EC](https://eur-lex.europa.eu/legal-content/EN/TXT/?uri=CELEX:32023L0958)

---

## What This Regulation Is and Why It Applies



Directive (EU) 2023/958, formally adopted on May 16, 2023 and published in the Official Journal of the European Union, extends the EU Emissions Trading System (ETS) to include maritime shipping from January 1, 2024.

 

This regulation covers shipping companies operating vessels above 5,000 gross tonnage calling at European Economic Area (EEA) ports, requiring them to monitor, report, and pay for a portion of their carbon dioxide (CO₂) emissions.





The regulation applies to all shipping customers involved in transporting goods to, from, and within the European Economic Area (EEA), which consists of EU member states, as well as Iceland, Liechtenstein, and Norway.

 

Carbon pricing is determined based on vessels rather than cargo, with extraterritorial application meaning that vessels sailing between an EU port and a non-EU port face charges for half of their voyage emissions.



This regulation fundamentally changes freight forwarding economics by introducing direct carbon costs that must be passed through to customers. 

The cost of compliance is expected to be significant and will keep increasing with the phased implementation, passed on as a standalone 'Emissions Surcharge' applied to all bookings on voyages subject to EU ETS.

 

Industry analysts expect the emission surcharge in 2025 to be nearly double that of 2024.



**Source:** Articles 3c, 3d, and 12 of Directive 2003/87/EC as amended by Directive (EU) 2023/958

---

## Issues Requiring Immediate Action

### Emissions Surcharge Cost Pass-Through Mechanism



Carriers must submit verified emissions for 2024 by the end of April 2025 and hand over enough allowances to cover their carbon outlay by the end of September 2025.

 

The actual surcharge for Q1 2025 will be published in December 2024, about 30 days before it takes effect.

 

Benchmark EU Allowances (EUAs) have traded between €68 and €76 per tonne throughout 2025, with Reuters reporting €70/tCO₂ on March 27 when France urged a price cap.



**Action Required — Confirm for Your Business:** Review all existing freight contracts for carbon surcharge pass-through clauses and establish quarterly EUA price adjustment mechanisms for 2025 pricing.

### Phased Compliance Obligations Scaling



For 2024, shipping companies surrender allowances for 40% of their reported emissions; for 2025, 70%; and from 2026 onward, 100% of their reported emissions.

 

The EU ETS will be introduced gradually, starting at 40% of emissions in 2024, rising to 70% in 2025 and then 100% from 2026 onwards.

 

From January 1, 2026, the ETS regulations will expand to include emissions of methane and nitrous oxide.



**Action Required — Confirm for Your Business:** Calculate the impact of the 2025 increase from 40% to 70% compliance on your ocean freight cost base and customer contract pricing.

### Geographic Scope and Emission Allocation



Shipping companies must purchase carbon allowances for 50% of emissions from voyages departing from an EU port to/from a non-EU port, 100% of emissions from voyages between EU ports, and 100% of emissions from ships docked at an EU port.

 

Any stops made at ports within 300 nautical miles of an EU/EEA port, where more than 65% of containers are transhipped, will not be considered port calls to prevent evasive behavior by container carriers.



**Action Required — Confirm for Your Business:** Map your trade lanes to determine ETS exposure percentage for each route and identify any routing optimization opportunities that comply with anti-circumvention rules.

### Non-Compliance Penalties and Enforcement



Companies that do not comply with their obligations will have to pay €100 per tonne of CO2 equivalent and will still have to surrender the required allowances.

 

After 2 years of non-compliance, an exclusion order may be issued requiring every EU member state to refuse entry to the ships.

 

If monitoring and reporting requirements are not met, heavy fines are imposed.



**Action Required — Confirm for Your Business:** Verify that all ocean carriers you use have established EU ETS monitoring plans and Union Registry accounts to avoid service disruption risks.

### Working Capital Impact on Cash Flow



EUAs must be bought months before a euro of revenue is booked, tying up working capital - particularly painful for smaller operators.

 

Carriers can choose to buy all allowances right before the September deadline or throughout the year as they emit carbon, creating nuances between carrier strategies.



**Action Required — Confirm for Your Business:** Assess the working capital impact of quarterly EUA price adjustments and consider contract terms that share this financing burden with customers.

---

## Operational Impact by Transport Mode

### Ocean Freight



EU ETS Maritime is a carbon pricing system requiring shipping companies to purchase and surrender EU Allowances (EUAs) corresponding to their verified emissions, marking a fundamental shift because carbon emissions now carry a direct financial cost.

 

Calculations per TEU are based on industry-aligned Clean Cargo methodology for CO2 calculation multiplied by market price for EUAs sourced from the ICEDEU3 Index using a three-month average, resulting in ETS surcharge amounts per TEU updated quarterly.



**Cost Pass-Through Mechanism:** 

If a ship burned 1,000 GJ of heavy fuel and failed FuelEU's 2% intensity cut, the FuelEU fine would be €58,500 - roughly €55 per FEU on an 1,100-TEU feeder.

 

Refrigerated containers draw auxiliary power while alongside, bumping the ETS share by 15-20% for dairy and pharma loads.



### Air Freight



The scope of the EU ETS for aviation has been expanded to emissions from most flights to and from the EU's nine outermost regions as well as departing flights from these outermost regions to Switzerland and the UK.

 

EU institutions agreed to phase-out free allocation to aircraft operators and move to full auctioning of allowances by 2026 to create a stronger price signal.



### Road and Intermodal Services



From 2027, a new emissions trading system (ETS II) will cover fuel distribution for road transport and buildings, initially running parallel to the original system with plans to merge in the early 2030s.

 

Some Mid-West manufacturers now rail containers to Montreal instead of New York to shave a day of steaming and a few euros of carbon cost.



### Customs and Reporting



Shipping companies must already report emissions under the EU Monitoring, Reporting and Verification (MRV) Regulation, requiring vessels above 5,000 GT calling at EU ports to report detailed emissions data.

 

Companies need to submit monitoring plans to authorities, start monitoring carbon emissions from January 1st 2024, have accredited verifiers confirm figures, then purchase allowances corresponding to emissions.

## Open Questions

- **FuelEU Maritime Integration Timeline:** How will the parallel implementation of FuelEU Maritime regulation interact with ETS compliance costs in 2025, and will carriers bundle these charges? Depends on EU implementing acts still being finalized.

- **EUA Price Volatility Management:** What risk management tools will be available to freight forwarders for hedging EUA price exposure given the 75% increase expected by 2027? Depends on development of maritime-specific carbon derivatives markets.

- **Third-Country Reciprocal Measures:** Will major non-EU trading partners implement reciprocal carbon border measures that affect the competitive position of EU ETS-compliant shipping? Depends on ongoing IMO negotiations and bilateral trade discussions.

- **Small Island and Outermost Region Exemptions:** How will the exemption list for small islands and outermost regions evolve, and what impact will this have on routing decisions? Depends on member state requests and Commission implementing decisions.

- **UK ETS Coordination:** How will the UK's domestic shipping ETS (starting 2026) coordinate with EU ETS for vessels operating in both jurisdictions? Depends on post-Brexit regulatory cooperation agreements.

fullBrief:
I need to search for current information about the Carbon Trust's role in carbon measurement and freight sector regulations to create a comprehensive intelligence brief.

Based on my search results, I need to clarify that the Carbon Trust is not actually a regulation itself, but rather a UK-based consultancy and certification body that develops carbon measurement standards and methodologies that affect freight operations. Let me create an intelligence brief focused on the Carbon Trust's regulatory-adjacent role in carbon measurement methodologies and standards that impact freight forwarders.

# Carbon Trust Carbon Measurement Standards and Certification Framework

**Regulatory Fact Document** | April 2026
**Primary source:** [Carbon Trust Official Website](https://www.carbontrust.com/)

---

## What This Regulation Is and Why It Applies



The Carbon Trust is a consultancy established in March 2001, with offices across the world. Its aim is to accelerate the pace of private and public sectors decarbonisation and increase energy efficiency in organisations worldwide.

 While not a regulation per se, the Carbon Trust operates as a quasi-regulatory body through its development of carbon measurement methodologies and certification standards that have become de facto industry requirements for freight forwarders.



The Carbon Trust provides sustainability education to businesses to help them save energy, as well as voluntary carbon certification services and carbon labelling schemes that verify carbon footprint data and companies' adherence to standards.

 

The organisation has a detailed understanding of best practice reporting and value chain footprint methodology given its advisory role in drafting the Greenhouse Gas Protocol's Scope 3 measurement guidance.

 This positions the Carbon Trust as a critical intermediary between regulatory requirements and operational implementation for freight forwarders.

The Carbon Trust's standards matter specifically to freight forwarders because 

Scope 3 emissions represent a staggering 70-90% of most companies' carbon footprint. While measuring these emissions can be challenging, addressing them is key to a credible climate strategy.

 With freight transportation being a major component of Scope 3 emissions for most businesses, Carbon Trust methodologies directly affect how freight forwarders calculate, report, and demonstrate emission reductions to their clients. 

Because your customers have to report their own emissions, and freight sits right in the middle of their Scope 3 footprint. Carbon questions now show up in procurement, supplier scorecards, and tenders.



**Source:** Carbon Trust Annual Report 2024; UK White Paper "Climate Change - the UK Programme" (Cmd 4913)

---

## Issues Requiring Immediate Action

### Carbon Trust Standard Certification Requirements


The Carbon Trust Carbon Standard is only awarded to companies and organisations who measure and reduce their carbon emissions year on year.

 

The Carbon Trust Standard is a certification that is awarded to organizations demonstrating a significant commitment to measuring, managing, and reducing their carbon emissions. This standard provides businesses with a valuable framework for assessing their carbon footprint and crafting strategies to effectively lower it over time.



**Action Required — Confirm for Your Business:** Review whether your freight forwarding operations meet Carbon Trust Standard requirements for year-on-year emission reductions. Determine if clients are requesting Carbon Trust certification as a procurement requirement.

### Scope 3 Measurement Methodology Updates


The Carbon Trust has a detailed understanding of best practice reporting and value chain footprint methodology given its advisory role in drafting the Greenhouse Gas Protocol's Scope 3 measurement guidance.

 

The organisation calculates footprints in line with internationally recognised standards such as the Greenhouse Gas Protocol, PAS 2050 and ISO Standards, as well as guidance specific to your sector, including Partnership for Carbon Accounting Financials.



**Action Required — Confirm for Your Business:** Verify that your current emission calculation methodologies align with the latest Carbon Trust/GHG Protocol updates. Check if your measurement tools are compatible with Carbon Trust verification requirements.

### Supply Chain Standard Implementation


The world's first independent certification for organisations that are measuring, managing and reducing greenhouse gas (CO2e) emissions in their supply chains. Understanding supply chain emissions and engaging suppliers to drive decarbonisation across the value chain is a key step on the journey to net zero.



**Action Required — Confirm for Your Business:** Assess whether your freight operations require Carbon Trust Supply Chain Standard certification to maintain client relationships. Determine if your carrier networks meet the measurement and reporting requirements.

### Route to Net Zero Standard Compliance


The Carbon Trust Route to Net Zero Standard is the only certification that guides and supports your organisation on your journey towards Net Zero and climate leadership.



**Action Required — Confirm for Your Business:** Evaluate if your freight forwarding business needs Route to Net Zero certification to meet client sustainability requirements and demonstrate credible net zero planning.

---

## Operational Impact by Transport Mode

### Ocean Freight
The Carbon Trust's methodologies directly impact ocean freight operations through 

the annual Carbon Intensity Indicator (CII) with ship-specific emissions reduction plans, and a requirement to calculate an Energy Efficiency Existing Ship Index (EEXI), which came into effect on 1 January 2023.

 Freight forwarders must ensure their emission calculations for ocean shipments align with Carbon Trust verification standards to maintain certification.

**Cost Implications:** Freight forwarders may need to invest in Carbon Trust-compliant measurement systems. 

Over 1,100 organisations around the globe have received assurance from the Carbon Trust.

fuel consumption, kilometres travelled, idle time, and load efficiency.

### Customs and Reporting

# Project Drawdown Climate Solutions Intelligence Framework

**Strategic Intelligence Document** | April 2026
**Primary source:** [Project Drawdown Explorer](https://drawdown.org/solutions)

---

## What This Resource Is and Why It Applies

Project Drawdown is 

an independent, internationally trusted organization driving meaningful climate action by connecting people like you to science-based climate solutions and strategies

. 

Project Drawdown is the most comprehensive plan ever proposed to reverse global warming. We gathered a qualified and diverse group of researchers from around the world to identify, research, and model the 100 most substantive, existing solutions to address climate change. What was uncovered is a path forward that can roll back global warming within thirty years

.



Built upon trillions of data points and thousands of hours of scientific analysis by experts from around the world, the Drawdown Explorer provides detailed information on 140-plus technologies and practices proven or proposed to effectively reduce greenhouse gas concentrations in the atmosphere

. The framework matters to freight forwarders because it quantifies precisely which decarbonisation actions deliver the most emissions reduction per dollar invested, enabling data-driven sustainability strategies rather than generic "green" initiatives.



Transport produces 7 gigatons of carbon dioxide-equivalent greenhouse gas emissions annually, or 23 percent of energy-related emissions, which is around 14 percent of all emissions. In individual countries, transport can account for much higher shares, even 35 percent of all emissions. Growth rates in emissions for some subsectors like air transport and international shipping are very high, so the Transport Sector requires special focus to keep emissions from ballooning out of control

. This directly impacts freight forwarders' operational costs, client sustainability requirements, and competitive positioning in ESG-focused procurement processes.

**Source:** Project Drawdown Explorer, Transport Sector Analysis

---

## Issues Requiring Immediate Action

### Client Advisory Service Development 

Freight forwarders need quantified data to advise clients on decarbonisation investments. 

The more ambitious Drawdown Scenario shows a potential for 94 gigatons of emissions reductions over the specified period, or 3.0 gigatons on average per year. Finally, the Optimum Scenario results in 160 gigatons of reduction over the entire period, or 5.2 gigatons per year

.

**Action Required — Confirm for Your Business:** How will you integrate Drawdown's quantified impact data into client sustainability advisory services and procurement decision frameworks?

### Ocean Shipping Efficiency Priority Ranking



Shipping produces 3 percent of global emissions. Fuel-saving ship design, onboard technologies, and practices can have a sizable impact, because of huge shipping volumes. With an efficiency gain of 50 percent across the international shipping industry, 7.9 gigatons of carbon dioxide emissions can be avoided by 2050

.

**Action Required — Confirm for Your Business:** Which ocean shipping efficiency measures rank highest in your client advisory framework and service procurement decisions?

### Investment Allocation Framework



The key financial results of the Plausible Scenario show US$22.7 trillion in operating savings over the study period for the entire sector, but at a marginal investment cost of US$17.8 trillion

.

**Action Required — Confirm for Your Business:** How will you use Drawdown's cost-benefit ratios to prioritise sustainability investments across transport modes?

### Alternative Fuel Assessment Timeline



Green hydrogen is one of the few near-zero-emission fuels with the potential to decarbonize aviation and long-haul trucking, where battery-electric solutions currently face range and weight constraints. If produced using abundant, low-cost renewables, green hydrogen could significantly cut emissions in sectors responsible for nearly 15% of global transport emissions

.

**Action Required — Confirm for Your Business:** When will you integrate "Keep Watching" solutions like green hydrogen into medium-term strategic planning?

---

## Operational Impact by Transport Mode

### Ocean Shipping Operations



The solution achieves 66 percent of the addressable market (93,001.10 billion freight metric ton–nautical miles) in 2050. We based emissions reductions on reduced fuel use resulting from the efficiency measures and slow steaming

. 

With an efficiency gain of 50 percent across the international shipping industry, 7.9 gigatons of carbon dioxide emissions can be avoided by 2050. That could save $424 billion in fuel costs over thirty years and $1 trillion over the life of the ships

.

Cost pass-through mechanisms: Fuel efficiency improvements translate directly to lower bunker surcharges for clients. Slow steaming reduces fuel costs but increases transit time, requiring client communication on service level trade-offs.

### Air Freight Transformation



This analysis focuses on adoption of the latest and most fuel-efficient aircraft; retrofitting existing aircraft with winglets, newer engines, and lighter interiors; and retiring older aircraft early. Over thirty years, 5.1 gigatons of carbon dioxide emissions can be avoided, saving $3.2 trillion on jet-fuel and operating costs. Other efficiency measures could provide additional emissions reductions and savings

.

Surcharge implications: Aviation efficiency improvements reduce fuel surcharges but require airline partner selection based on fleet modernisation programs rather than purely on rates.

### Road Transport Integration



Road freight is responsible for about 6 percent of global emissions. Increasing fuel efficiency in both new trucks and existing fleets can significantly reduce emissions. If adoption of fuel-saving technologies grows from 2 percent to 85 percent of trucks by 2050, this solution can deliver 6.2 gigatons of carbon dioxide emissions reductions. An investment of $544 billion to implement could save $2.8 trillion on fuel costs over thirty years

.

Contract implications: Trucking partners using fuel-efficient technologies deliver lower-cost services over contract duration but may require higher initial rates to cover technology investments.

Based on the search results, I can see that the user's request is about supply chain digitalization regulations rather than a single specific regulation. The key regulations affecting freight forwarders are the eFTI Regulation (EU) 2020/1056 and the Import Control System 2 (ICS2). However, since the original resource mentions "Supply Chain Digital" as a publication, I should clarify this and focus on the key digital supply chain regulations that are actually in force. Let me create a comprehensive brief covering the main digital supply chain regulatory requirements.

# EU Digital Supply Chain Compliance Framework

**Regulatory Fact Document** | January 2026
**Primary source:** [Regulation (EU) 2020/1056 on electronic freight transport information](https://eur-lex.europa.eu/eli/reg/2020/1056/oj/eng) and [Import Control System 2 (ICS2)](https://ec.europa.eu/taxation_customs/business/customs-procedures/general-overview/import-control-system-2-ics2_en)

---

## What This Regulation Is and Why It Applies

The EU Digital Supply Chain Compliance Framework encompasses multiple interconnected regulations fundamentally transforming how freight forwarders handle data and documentation across European trade corridors. 

The eFTI Regulation is set to transform freight transport within the EU by boosting efforts to replace paper-based documentation with electronic data in all transport modes. This digital shift will apply to road, rail, inland waterway, and air transport. It will reduce administrative burdens for operators and authorities, enhance data security, and ensure compliance with EU and national freight regulations.





Regulation (EU) 2020/1056on electronic freight transport information entered into force. Regulation (EU) 2020/1056, adopted on July 15, 2020, establishes a legal framework for the electronic exchange of freight transport information (eFTI) across the European Union.

 

It entered into force on 20 August 2020 and applies from 21 August 2024.

 Simultaneously, 

The most recent and final phase, Release 3, expands ICS2 to maritime, road, and rail carriers, completing the transition and making ICS2 the sole system for all transport modes as of September 1, 2025. From that date on, ICS2 is the only system for managing safety and security data for all incoming goods across air, maritime, road, and rail transport.



This matters to freight forwarders because 

The most significant change under ICS2 is the shared responsibility for providing accurate safety and security data. This system moves far beyond placing the burden solely on the carrier; it affects every key player in the supply chain. If your business is involved in moving goods into the EU, you are considered an Economic Operator (EO) and must comply.

 The regulatory framework creates mandatory digitalization requirements with specific cost implications for non-compliance and operational efficiency gains for early adopters.

**Source:** Articles 1-3 of Regulation (EU) 2020/1056; ICS2 Release 3 Implementation Guidelines

---

## Issues Requiring Immediate Action

### eFTI Platform Certification Requirements by July 2027



As of 9 July 2027: The eFTI Regulation will apply in full. Member State authorities must accept information shared electronically by operators via certified eFTI platforms. As of 9 July 2027: The eFTI Regulation will apply in full. Member State authorities must accept information shared electronically by operators via certified eFTI platforms.

 

In parallel, the European Commission is developing the technical specifications for market-facing eFTI platforms. A first draft of the Implementing Act for these platforms was shared with Member States in March 2025.



**Action Required — Confirm for Your Business:** Evaluate current IT systems' compatibility with eFTI platform requirements and budget for certification costs by Q2 2026.

### ICS2 Full Enforcement - Complete Data Requirements Now Active



Since 1 September 2025, ICS2 Release 3 has applied to all Economic Operators involved in moving goods into or through the EU via sea, inland waterways, road, or rail, subject to limited transitional derogations that expired by the end of 2025 Although Release 3 began rolling out from 3 June 2024, it is now fully implemented and enforced. Since 1 September 2025, ICS2 Release 3 has applied to all Economic Operators involved in moving goods into or through the EU via sea, inland waterways, road, or rail, subject to limited transitional derogations that expired by the end of 2025 in most Member States.



**Action Required — Confirm for Your Business:** Verify all shipments include complete 6-digit HS codes, accurate goods descriptions, and valid EORI numbers to prevent delays and penalties.

### EORI Registration for ICS2 Compliance



What if my company does not have an EORI number yet? An Economic Operator Registration and Identification (EORI) number is mandatory to connect to ICS2. If your company does not yet have one, you must apply with the respective customs authority in the EU member state where you are established.



**Action Required — Confirm for Your Business:** All entities handling EU-bound cargo must obtain valid EORI numbers immediately if not already registered.

### Data Quality Standards and "Stop Words" Compliance



"Stop words" are restricted or generic terms (like "various" or "parts") that cannot be used in ENS data fields. A full list is provided via the EU's CIRCABC (the European Commission's document-sharing platform), along with additional technical guidance on ENS quality standards.



**Action Required — Confirm for Your Business:** Review all standard cargo descriptions and eliminate prohibited "stop words" from data entry systems and templates.

---

## Operational Impact by Transport Mode

### Ocean Freight Operations



The third Release of ICS2 takes effect on 3 June 2024 – and goes beyond air freight. The regulations will then apply to those economic operators transporting goods by sea, on inland waterway vessels or those using road and rail traffic. There are different phases governing the time when it is necessary to start sending a full set of data using the ENS: The third phase immediately affects carriers operating maritime and inland waterway services from 3 June 2024 onwards.



Ocean freight forwarders must now provide complete Entry Summary Declarations (ENS) with house-level filing capabilities. 

Under ICS2 Release 3, Economic Operators are required to submit a complete Entry Summary Declaration (ENS) prior to arrival, including: A minimum six-digit Harmonised System (HS) code for each product · A clear and accurate description of goods, reflecting composition, nature, and intended use

 This creates additional documentation costs estimated at €15-25 per house bill of lading for data verification and compliance checking.

### Air Freight Operations



The second stage of ICS2 has been in force since 1 March 2023 and now affects the entire air freight industry. The economic operators affected include courier and air freight carriers, postal services and freight forwarders. As a result, additional regulations to assess the customs risk have come into effect before goods are loaded and arrive: it is now necessary to specify the entire data set on the Entry Summary Declaration and register their arrival as well as the provision of goods.



Air operations face the strictest timing requirements. 

For short-haul flights (i.e. those lasting less than four hours), the ENS must be sent to the relevant customs authority in the EU member state destination at the latest when the plane takes off.



### Road and Rail Transport



The EU's Import Control System 2 (ICS2) entered its next phase on April 1, 2025, requiring all rail and road carriers transporting goods to or through the EU, Northern Ireland, Norway, or Switzerland to submit complete Entry Summary Declarations (ENS). These requirements for road and rail transport entered into force on April 1, 2025 according to Implementing Decision (EU) 2023/2879 establishing the UCC Work Programme.



Road and rail operators must collect comprehensive data from underlying consignment notes. 

Carriers must collect the necessary data from all underlying house transport documents or consignment notes provided by freight forwarders and other filing parties.



### Customs and Reporting Functions

The eFTI regulation fundamentally changes customs interaction protocols. 

Authorities in all EU Member States will be required to accept electronic data when shared by businesses via eFTIcompliant platforms. Business data will be housed on secure, certified IT platforms that can be easily integrated with companies' existing data management systems. This data will only be shared with authorities upon explicit inspection request, using unique access links in machine-readable formats such as QR codes.

# Mission Innovation Zero-Emission Shipping Mission

**Regulatory Fact Document** | April 2026
**Primary source:** [Mission Innovation Zero-Emission Shipping Mission](https://mission-innovation.net/missions/shipping/)

---

## What This Regulation Is and Why It Applies



Mission Innovation is a global initiative of 23 countries and the European Commission (on behalf of the European Union)

, launched in 2015 as part of international climate cooperation efforts. 

Denmark has initiated the Zero-Emission Shipping Mission, with great partners from the public and the private sector from all over the world

, alongside the United States and Norway as co-leads.



The mission, led by Denmark, Norway, and the US, in collaboration with the Global Maritime Forum and the Mærsk Mc-Kinney Møller Center for Zero Carbon Shipping, aims to accelerate international public-private collaboration to scale and deploy new green maritime solutions, putting international shipping on an ambitious zero-emission course

. 

The mission will also be supported by the governments of India, Morocco, the UK, Singapore, France, Ghana, and South Korea

, with 

Germany stands alongside other support mission members, Canada, European Commission, France, Ghana, Republic of Korea and Core Mission Members, Australia, India, Morocco, Singapore, and the United Kingdom

.

This initiative matters critically for freight forwarders because it establishes the technological roadmap that will determine which zero-emission fuel technologies receive priority government support, infrastructure investment, and regulatory facilitation. While not a mandatory regulation, the Mission's targets directly influence port infrastructure development, vessel availability, fuel supply chains, and ultimately the operational choices available to freight forwarders when booking ocean transport for clients demanding sustainable shipping solutions.

**Source:** Mission Innovation Action Plan 2022

---

## Issues Requiring Immediate Action

### Revised 2030 Targets Requiring Strategic Planning



Today, the Zero-Emission Shipping Mission announced revised, more ambitious 2030 Mission goals during Singapore Maritime Week

 in April 2024. 

These goals include: Increasing the number of large international ships running on zero-emission fuels by 2030 from 200 to 600 vessels; Adopting an annual fuel production goal of 16 million metric tons of heavy fuel oil equivalent (on an energy basis) zero-emission fuels by 2030; and · Increasing the number of key ports offering zero-emission bunkering from 10 to 20

.

**Action Required — Confirm for Your Business:** Assess which trade routes your clients use most frequently and determine which of the target 20 ports with zero-emission bunkering capabilities will affect your ocean freight operations by 2030.

### Port Infrastructure Development Timeline



Together with partners the Zero-Emission Shipping Mission will introduce: a Port Readiness Level web-based tool to help ports and port authorities assess their capacity to support alternative fuels

. This tool will help identify which ports will have green methanol, ammonia, and hydrogen bunkering capabilities first.

**Action Required — Confirm for Your Business:** Map your top 50 port pairs against the Mission's port readiness assessments to identify potential service disruptions or premium routing options for sustainable shipping.

### Technology Focus Areas Affecting Vessel Availability



The fuels considered in scope for the Shipping Mission include: advanced biofuels, green ammonia, hydrogen, and methanol

. 

The second project to be addressed at the Innovation Workshop is titled "Safety of Crew on Low or Zero Emission fueled ships" This project addresses the needed new competencies for seafarers working on vessels using Ammonia, Methanol, or Hydrogen for propulsion

.

**Action Required — Confirm for Your Business:** Determine which fuel types your preferred shipping lines are investing in, as this will affect vessel availability and routing options for zero-emission shipping services.

### Financial Support Mapping Impact



With more than 70 funding opportunities, this study seeks to provide ship owners and governments at a global scale with an overview over financial support and guarantee opportunities for investments in new builds, retrofitting and/or energy efficiency improvements. The study shows that various types of funding options exist for ship owners, including grants, guarantees and loans from regional development banks

.

**Action Required — Confirm for Your Business:** Engage with your carrier partners to understand how government funding for zero-emission vessel investments will translate into service offerings and potential green shipping surcharges in your contracts.

---

## Operational Impact by Transport Mode

### Ocean Freight Operations



International shipping transports the majority of the world's goods and is responsible for 3% of global emissions, potentially increasing by half by 2050 on its current trajectory. To set international shipping on an ambitious zero-emission trajectory, we need commercially viable, zero-emission ocean-going vessels in the global fleet by 2030

.

The Mission's target of 

at least 5% of the global deep-sea fleet measured by fuel consumption capable of running on green hydrogen, green ammonia, green methanol, and advanced biofuels by 2030

 directly affects ocean freight capacity allocation. Carriers investing in zero-emission vessels will likely implement green fuel surcharges, similar to current bunker adjustment factors, but with potentially higher volatility due to emerging fuel supply chains.

### Port Operations and Container Handling



Under the Shipping Mission's "Fueling Infrastructure" pillar, the report provides insights into the sources of green methanol and ammonia that could be available to the shipping industry and how ports can secure supply to meet the International Maritime Organization's (IMO) target of at least 5% use of zero-emission fuels by 2030

.

Port selection will become increasingly strategic as only select facilities develop zero-emission bunkering capabilities. This may require freight forwarders to adjust routing to access ports with sustainable fueling infrastructure, potentially affecting transit times and costs.

### Green Shipping Corridors Development



RMI is accelerating the deployment of vessels running on zero-emission fuels this decade through the development of green shipping corridors. These are shipping routes between two major port hubs on which ships can travel with low-emissions fuels, principally green hydrogen and its derivatives ammonia and methanol. Alongside the Mærsk Mc-Kinney Møller Center for Zero Carbon Shipping, RMI released a handbook to help ports, shipping companies, fuel producers, financiers, and governments start developing green shipping corridors

.

Freight forwarders should expect preferential service development along major trade routes first, with premium pricing for guaranteed zero-emission transport services.

---

## Key Data and Figures

|---|---|---|
| Zero-emission vessels target 2030 | 600 large international ships | US DOE April 2024 |
| Fleet penetration target | 5% of global deep-sea fleet by fuel consumption | Mission goals |
| Zero-emission fuel production target | 16 million metric tons HFO equivalent annually by 2030 | Mission revised goals April 2024 |
| Bunkering ports target | 20 key ports offering zero-emission fueling by 2030 | Mission revised goals |
| Electrolyzer capacity requirement | 60 gigawatts green hydrogen capacity | Mission Action Plan |
| Member countries | 23 countries plus European Commission | Mission Innovation |
| Global emissions from shipping | 3% of global GHG emissions | Multiple sources |
| Current zero-emission ships baseline | 200 vessels (2024) | Mission goals |

---

## Compliance Risk Register
